metadata.title: Eric Morris Acting System - Unified Knowledge Base
source_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

being.name: BEING
being.definition: The foundational state of authentic, unedited presence in which the actor is genuinely affected by internal and external stimuli and responds by expressing feelings on an organic, moment-to-moment basis. BEING is total presence — the acknowledgment, acceptance, and expression of all impulses without intellectual interference, suppression, or social obligation. It is not merely an acting state but a living state that must exist 24 hours a day; the fullest, most real state achievable on stage or in life. BEING BEFORE DOING, BEING BEFORE ACTING. The canvas metaphor: BEING is the canvas; technique is the paint — 90% of training should go to BEING.
being.details:
Morris developed the concept of BEING after observing that actors were fullest on stage when closest to what they actually felt. The split between what an actor really feels and what they express is the core problem BEING addresses. BEING is contrasted with 'natural' acting, which is the imitation of truth rather than truth itself.

When truly BEING, behavior includes infinite colors, distractions, interruptions, and unpredictable emotional changes. All emotions have equal value — 'Everything is a Number Six.' Suppressing any impulse caps everything. The actor who is truly BEING is unique, individual, and unlike any other person. Conditioning, social taboos, and behavioral restrictions are major obstacles to BEING.

BEING is the bridge between process and application of process. It is the first stage in the chain leading to Ultimate Consciousness (Being → Irreverence → Ultimate Consciousness). Without a solid BEING state, the later stages cannot be reached. When an actor achieves BEING, the work paradoxically becomes more theatrical and meets material demands more imaginatively.

The Being and Doing philosophy (from the book of the same name) captures the essence: in real life, people DO things because of real impulses — genuine BEING causes authentic DOING. Stage acting must replicate this organic process rather than imitating the surface result. Common acting problems — imposition, compensation, dependencies — vanish when the actor is in a genuine BEING state. Being in Every Role: versatility comes from accessing different dimensions of oneself, not from becoming different people.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.summary: BEING defined: state of experience/behavior wherein actor is affected by stimuli and responds organically moment-to-moment; the nucleus of talent.
Originated from observing split between actor's real feelings and their expressed behavior.
BEING is the bridge between theory and application; prerequisite to using the Method and craft techniques.
BEING is a living state, 24 hours a day; conditioning and taboos are major obstacles.
BEING is the basis of organic reality, irreverence, Ultimate Consciousness, and ensemble — the foundation of theatrically memorable performances.
Morris introduces BEING as result of working with actors who were fullest on stage when closest to what they really felt.
BEING defined: do no more or less than you feel. BEING is the only place from which you can create organic reality.
In BEING state, emotions contain all personal truth. BEING produces unpredictability and uniqueness.
BEING BEFORE DOING. All emotions equal value — Everything is a Number Six. Suppressing any impulse caps everything.
Canvas metaphor: BEING is the canvas. 90% BEING, 10% technique is proper proportion.
BEING promotes natural stimulus-effect-response-expression cycle. Premeditation short-circuits this process.
Being is stage one — actor in moment-to-moment contact with feelings, expressing impulses freely. First link in chain: being → irreverence → ultimate consciousness.

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Being and Doing philosophy: real life actions result from real impulses; stage acting must replicate this process rather than mimicking Doing without genuine Being.

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Morris developed BEING system over 20 years to find roads into the self, eliminating lifelong inhibitions.
Being state defined as starting from which authentic acting proceeds — an empty blackboard that can receive any stimulus. Still and full, not empty.

instrument.name: The Instrument
instrument.definition: The actor's total self — body, mind, voice, emotions, senses, impulses, and psyche — the medium through which acting is created. Analogous to a musician's instrument: if damaged or blocked, even great talent cannot be expressed. Must be freed and sensitized before craft techniques can function.
instrument.details:
Actors grow up in a society full of rules, taboos, judgments, and criticisms that create inhibitions, fears, and blocks inside the instrument. These obstacles cut the actor off from themselves and others, crippling creative expression. The actor's first job is to liberate the instrument so that talent can flow through it freely — historical acting training largely neglected this dimension.

The instrument has both impressive (receiving) and expressive (outputting) areas — both must be developed. Shy actors may be imaginative and affectable but unable to express; exhibitionists may express freely but be insensitive. Splits form when the actor imposes behaviors not arising from genuine impulse.

Instrumental work uses the Relaxation Cluster: relaxation technique + Sensitizing + Personal Inventory. Over 1,000 instrumental exercises were developed in Morris's 37+ years of teaching. Basic preparation sequence: achieve relaxation → elevate sensory accessibility → contact moment-to-moment feelings → freely express impulses. Instrumental work is the work of a lifetime.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Introduces the Instrument: actor's body, intellect, emotions, voice. Born into an inhibiting world, we develop blocks. These must be eliminated to release creative talent.
Cannot teach person to act if not connected to inner self.
Splits form in instrument when actor imposes behaviors. Impressive and expressive areas both must be developed.
The system has two parts: the instrument and the craft.
Instrumental work eliminates fears one by one via direct confrontation; domino effect — addressing one fear causes others to dissipate.
Definition: actor's mind/body/voice/emotions; analogy to musician's instrument. Damage limits talent expression.
Historical neglect of instrumental liberation; even Strasberg avoided personal work, limiting actors with greater obstacles.
Morris discovered emotional blocks prevent actors from using techniques; devised BEING as prerequisite. Instrumental preparation is the missing link.

instrumental_preparation.name: Instrumental Preparation
instrumental_preparation.definition: Work specifically designed to free the actor's personal instrument so that emotional blocks, inhibitions, fears, and conditioning can be eliminated, making the actor receptive to craft techniques. The 'missing link' in the Stanislavsky Method.
instrumental_preparation.details:
Stanislavsky's system did not recognize the need for instrumental preparation — which is why the Method failed for many actors. Actors who were naturally emotionally free succeeded with the Method; those with blocks could not apply it. Instrumental preparation must address the specific problems standing in the way of the actor's personal truth: fear, insecurity, behavioral conditioning, and social obligations.

Morris's first two books (No Acting Please and Being & Doing) focused almost exclusively on instrumental preparation. The domino effect: addressing one fear causes others to dissipate. Without instrumental preparation, even valid craft techniques are useless. It is the bridge between the Method's theory and its actual application.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.summary: Morris discovered emotional blocks prevent actors from using techniques; devised BEING as prerequisite.
Stanislavsky's system did not recognize need for instrumental preparation.
The system has two parts: the instrument and the craft. Instrumental work must precede craft application.

choices.name: Choices
choices.definition: The specific objects, people, places, events, experiences, or sensory elements an actor selects to stimulate the emotional life required by the material. A choice is what the actor works for; a choice approach is how the actor creates it. Choices are not decisions to do something — they are always objects in the broad sense, selected to personally affect the actor in the way the material demands.
choices.details:
Choices can be: photographs, letters, memorabilia, music, places, people, smells, tastes, animals, imaginary constructs, sounds, time of day, weather conditions, and much more. 'Wherever there is an impulse, a thought, or a feeling, there exists a choice that will stimulate it.' The key to good choices: knowing oneself and knowing what kinds of stimuli do what to you.

Choices must be translated sensorily — the actor identifies what specific element of a complex experience to recreate. Good choices stimulate not just a single identifiable emotion but a complicated emotional life. Choices need not be literal — the actor does not have to experience exactly what the character experiences, only what produces equivalent impulses.

If a choice fails to stimulate the desired response, it is discarded for another. The actor should be cataloguing, trying, using, and exploring choices all the time — an actor is an actor 24 hours a day. The richer the array of choice approaches, the richer the craftsman. The what (choice) and how (choice approach) are both essential elements of Morris's craft system.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Choice: what you work for to stimulate emotional life. Can be place, odor, person, inanimate object, animal. Richer array of choice approaches equals richer craftsman.

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Possible choice areas: photographs, letters, memorabilia.
Choice is the stimulus used sensorily to create behavior and emotional life. Linked to obligation: obligation tells you what to feel; choice is how you get there.
A choice is an object, not an action decision; selected from the actor's life to stimulate the required emotional response.
Infinite possibilities for choices; used to address all seven obligations.
Wherever there is an impulse, a thought, or a feeling, there exists a choice that will stimulate it. Actor catalogues choices 24 hours a day.
The craft system separates choices from the approaches used to create them.

choice_approaches.name: Choice Approaches
choice_approaches.definition: The methods, techniques, and tools an actor actually uses when performing to make a chosen object or experience genuinely real — the 'how' to the choice's 'what.' The pick and shovel of the craft. While obligations and choices are intellectual identification areas, choice approaches are what the actor actually uses in the moment of acting.
choice_approaches.details:
Morris's system has evolved to include 27 choice approaches (Imaging = #26, Affective Memory = #12 in some enumerations; Irreverent Acting lists 22 functional tools). Multiple approaches exist for the same choice — the actor selects the approach best suited to the specific material situation.

The twenty-two (or more) choice approaches include: Sense Memory, Available Stimulus, Sensory Suggestion, Evocative Words, Believability, Selective Emphasis, Endowments, Imaginary Monologues, Imaginary Dialogues, Externals (Animals, People, Inanimate Objects, Insects), Objects that come into contact with the body, Affective Memory, Imaging, Inner Monologue, and others. Originally there were only four or five; the list grew as Morris's teaching evolved.

Selecting the right choice approach is a result of experience and experimentation. Some work far better in certain material situations. The actor must develop fluency across as many approaches as possible to have maximum craft flexibility.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.summary: 22 choice approaches are the pick and shovel of the job — the means by which the actor works on stage with a choice. Originally only 4 or 5; grew over decades.
Affective Memory is choice approach #12. The system separates choices from the approaches used to create them.
There are 27 choice approaches. Imaging = #26. Each suited to different circumstances.
The actor works the choice approach while saying lines and expressing all impulses.

obligations.name: Obligations
obligations.definition: The seven major responsibilities an actor must identify and fulfill when approaching dramatic material — the specific realities the character must experience as defined by the script. What the playwright and material demand of the actor. The actor uses choices and choice approaches to organically create each obligation from personal experience.
obligations.details:
The seven main obligations: (1) Time and Place — the environment where the scene occurs; (2) Relationship — who the other characters are and how the character feels toward them; (3) Emotional — what the character feels; (4) Character — who the character is (physical, emotional, intellectual, psychological); (5) Historic — the historical period's customs, mores, and realities; (6) Thematic — the playwright's statement or message; (7) Subtextual — the unsaid, underlying emotional reality beneath the dialogue.

Morris contrasts obligations with Stanislavsky's 'objectives, superobjectives, actions, activities' — calling the latter counter-creative. Obligations defined in terms of the actor's task to create (not just play) allow the actor to use craft to organically stimulate the needed reality. The order of importance and approach varies with each piece of material; thorough reading of the material is prerequisite to identifying all obligations.

The Obligation-Choice-and-Choice-Approach Chart is the primary organizational tool for mapping this process. Three broader types exist in some formulations: instrumental obligations (relating to the actor's instrument), relational obligations (other people and objects), and role obligations (the character's requirements).
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.summary: Seven major obligations listed; each requires a parallel choice from the actor's life.
The craft system has three areas: Obligations (responsibilities of the material), Choices, and Choice Approaches.

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Five types of obligations: emotional, character, thematic, background/historical, relational. Must identify all by reading material thoroughly.
Seven main obligations listed. Morris contrasts obligations with Stanislavsky's objectives/superobjectives — calling the latter counter-creative.
Three broader obligation types: instrumental, relational, and role obligations. Actor works from BEING toward the obligation through appropriate choices.

emotional_obligation.name: Emotional Obligation
emotional_obligation.definition: One of the seven obligations: what the character feels in every given scene and in every moment throughout the play, and how those emotions change. The emotional life the actor must organically stimulate for themselves through appropriate choices and choice approaches.
emotional_obligation.details:
The emotional obligation is the most central obligation in most scenes. The actor identifies the emotional obligation, then makes choices to create the realities that promote the emotional experience. Choices must address the full emotional picture — not just stimulate surface emotions without the underlying passion and need that motivate them.

Three primary choice types for emotional obligation (using Imaging as choice approach): (1) Creating a Piece of Music — selecting evocative music and hearing it internally while performing; (2) Creating an Experience — selecting a personal past event that produced the needed emotions and re-entering it; (3) Creating a Person — selecting a real or imagined person who evokes the needed feelings and conjuring them.

sources.summary: Three primary choice types for emotional obligation using Imaging: music, experience, person.
Emotional obligation is the most central obligation. Choices must address the full emotional picture including underlying passion and motivation.

character_obligation.name: Character Obligation
character_obligation.definition: One of the seven obligations: the actor's responsibility to create who the character is across four dimensions — physical, emotional, intellectual, and psychological — and assimilate those qualities into their own persona. The goal: the character inhabits the actor.
character_obligation.details:
Process: (1) Compare and contrast self with character physically, emotionally, behaviorally. (2) Create a background life for the character using Imaging — events and experiences prior to the play's action. (3) Incorporate parallel realities from the actor's own life mixed with fantasy (Believability).

For differences between actor and character: find choices and approaches that organically stimulate the character's qualities. For similarities: the actor IS the character in those areas; no additional work is needed. Physical character work can involve externals (animals), exercise, costumes, Sensory Self-Endowments. When a play is cast, actors are usually chosen for similarities to the character — so the gap is often smaller than feared.

Choices need not be literal — the actor does not have to experience exactly what the character experiences, only what produces equivalent impulses in themselves.

sources.summary: Process: compare/contrast, create background life via Imaging, incorporate parallel realities. Choices need not be literal.
Four parts: physical, emotional, intellectual, psychological. Goal: character inhabits the actor.

thematic_obligation.name: Thematic Obligation
thematic_obligation.definition: One of the seven obligations: the actor's responsibility to carry and embody the playwright's statement or message — what the play says and often why the writer wrote it. Sometimes divided among characters; often assigned to a single character as the playwright's spokesman.
thematic_obligation.details: The theme is what the play says; the emotional life of the play expresses the theme through the characters' behavior. The actor must ask: Does my character have a responsibility to promote the statement of the play? Shaw often singled out one character to espouse his philosophy; many playwrights embed their statement in the emotional journey of all characters. When the character carries the thematic statement, the actor uses Imaging to create the inner life that makes that statement real and organic.

sources.summary: Thematic obligation: carry the statement of the play; ask whether character is the playwright's spokesman.
Use Imaging to create the inner life that makes the thematic statement real and organic.

subtextual_obligation.name: Subtextual Obligation
subtextual_obligation.definition: One of the seven obligations: the underlying emotional quality or essence running through the entire play, the unsaid things that live underneath the written words. The most subtle and usually hardest to identify obligation — what is meant but not said.
subtextual_obligation.details: Subtext is the underlying emotional reality that gives the written words their deeper meaning. Every choice must accommodate this underlying quality. Examples: a feeling of hopelessness throughout, or conversely a quality of hope and vitality. The actor's responsibility is to make the subtext real so that it lives in their behavior throughout the scene. Imaging can be used to sustain the subtextual emotional state across scenes.

sources.summary: Subtextual obligation: the unsaid things in the play, the most subtle and hardest to identify of all seven obligations.
Use Imaging to sustain the subtextual emotional state across scenes. Every choice must accommodate this underlying quality.

time_and_place_obligation.name: Time and Place Obligation
time_and_place_obligation.definition: The first and most foundational acting obligation — the requirement that the actor genuinely experience the environment described by the material (location, time of day, season, weather, historical period) as if actually there. Often preempts other obligations and should be addressed first.
time_and_place_obligation.details:
Every scene takes place somewhere at a specific time. The playwright chooses these for communicative/expressive reasons. The actor must create the time and place so as to be affected just as the character is. The character's behavior is influenced by the environment on both obvious and subtle levels.

Options: use available stimuli (the existing set), add meaningful objects, or create a wholly different place from personal life. The actor selects a place that will mean to them what the play's environment means to the character — selection must NOT be made hastily; the place must carry historical, emotional, and behavioral resonance. Even if time/place is not the most important obligation in a scene, it must never be disregarded.

sources.summary: First and most foundational obligation. Selection must carry historical, emotional, and behavioral resonance.
Every scene takes place somewhere at a specific time. Actor must be affected as the character is. Options: available stimuli, added objects, or personal parallel place.

relationship_obligation.name: Relationship Obligation
relationship_obligation.definition: One of the seven obligations: the actor's responsibility to make real — through craft — who the other characters are to their character (relationship responsibility) and how the character feels toward them (emotional obligation of relationship).
relationship_obligation.details: Two components: (1) Relationship Responsibility — who the other characters are (wife, father, adversary, lover, etc.) and establishing a parallel reality with the actual actor playing the role. (2) Emotional Obligation of Relationship — how the character feels about each of the other characters. The actor uses endowments and sensory processes to make the other actor feel like the character they are supposed to be. Unless doing a solo dramatic reading, there will always be relationship obligations.

sources.summary: Two components: relationship responsibility (who they are) and emotional obligation of relationship (how character feels). Uses endowments and sensory processes.

historic_obligation.name: Historic Obligation
historic_obligation.definition: One of the seven obligations: the actor's responsibility to create and embody the realities of the historical period of the play — its manner, speech patterns, thought processes, beliefs, technological realities, dress, and physical movement — so that they are authentically felt rather than merely implied.
historic_obligation.details:
All elements of a historical period impact how the character looks at life, relates to people, speaks, thinks, acts, walks, and talks. The actor must find substitutes or parallels in their own life for each historical element. Emotions have been universally the same since the beginning of recorded time; any character's experience can be restimulated by personally meaningful choices.

Dress and costume can go a long way in exciting imagination and changing contemporary movement. Any of the imaging techniques can be used to create the realities of the historical period — imaging the sights, sounds, smells, and textures of the time and place to inhabit the world of the play authentically.

sources.summary: All elements of historical period impact character behavior. Emotions universal; personal choices create historical truth. Costume helps.
Use any imaging technique to create the realities of the historical period — sights, sounds, smells, textures.

consciousness.name: Consciousness
consciousness.definition: An expanded state of awareness, curiosity, sensitivity, perception, interest, knowledge, imagination, and concern for all life that an actor cultivates both as a life practice and as an acting prerequisite. Its development creates the bridge to unconscious impulses and enables the actor to recognize and address instrumental obstacles.
consciousness.details:
Everyone is born with consciousness, but its level depends on upbringing and deliberate development. Its components include: curiosity, imagination, awareness, sensitivity, perception, interest, knowledge, and affinity/concern for life. Each component reinforces the others.

Freeing the Actor identifies five components: Observation, Awareness, Curiosity, Commitment, and Information. Without consciousness, an actor cannot identify what blocks them or how to address it. Most people live with a restricted level of consciousness limited to survival and personal interests. Developing consciousness is an ongoing discipline, not a one-time achievement.

The actor's consciousness (from My Hollywood Stories) refers to the distinctive inner world actors share that non-actors cannot fully understand — the constant uncertainty, rejection, loneliness, and sporadic income of the profession. Two actors who don't know each other can communicate this shared experience wordlessly.

sources.summary: Consciousness made of curiosity, imagination, awareness, sensitivity, perception, interest, knowledge, affinity for life. Each component reinforces others.
Consciousness is prerequisite for identifying and eliminating obstacles; most people's awareness is restricted to personal survival.
Five components: Observation, Awareness, Curiosity, Commitment, Information.
The actor's consciousness: two actors can meet and without a word 'get it' — the shared experience of the profession that civilians cannot understand.

ultimate_consciousness.name: Ultimate Consciousness
ultimate_consciousness.definition: The pinnacle acting state — the culmination of Being → Irreverence → Ultimate Consciousness — in which unconscious impulses are communicated into and enrich conscious behavior, producing transcendent performance in which both actor and audience are transported. Not mystical but the natural result of genuine BEING combined with irreverent craft.
ultimate_consciousness.details:
Lee Strasberg said ninety-five percent of an actor's talent is in the unconscious. Ultimate Consciousness occurs when unconscious connection is deep enough to take over. It is not consistently achievable and is unpredictable, but it is the pinnacle of unconscious achievement. The closer consciousness and the unconscious are to each other, the deeper, fuller, and more dimensional is all acting work — even short of the Ultimate Consciousness peak.

This is the third and final stage in a sequential behavioral chain: Being → Irreverence → Ultimate Consciousness. When an actor is in a genuine being state and works irreverently with intelligent choices, unconscious impulses begin to color conscious behavior, producing an elevated theatrical life. It is not a mystical or parapsychological phenomenon but the natural product of the system.

sources.summary: Ultimate consciousness defined as unconscious impulses feeding into conscious behavior. Third stage: being → irreverence → ultimate consciousness.
Pinnacle acting experience: conscious and unconscious merge completely, transporting actor and audience. Unpredictable but the pinnacle of unconscious achievement.

eleventh_level_of_consciousness.name: Eleventh Level of Consciousness
eleventh_level_of_consciousness.definition: The state in which the actor is fully and simultaneously aware of their character's reality AND the actual performance environment (cues, other actors, blocking, audience) — a dual consciousness that allows organic character work without losing craft awareness. On the first ten levels the actor is totally in the flow of reality; on the eleventh, they retain awareness of being in performance.
eleventh_level_of_consciousness.details:
This resolves the apparent contradiction between full organic immersion and theatrical craft responsibility. The actor says the proper words, doesn't actually strangle the leading lady, etc. — all from this eleventh level. It is a prerequisite for Programming the Unconscious: the actor must be sufficiently developed to maintain this dual awareness, or the risk of confusing imagined reality with performance reality becomes a psychological danger.

Without the eleventh level of consciousness, working with the unconscious for powerful or extreme roles carries real danger. It is the safety valve of the Morris system.

sources.summary: Eleventh Level: on ten levels actor is in reality; eleventh level knows they are 'acting' and preserves theatrical responsibilities.
Prerequisite for Programming the Unconscious. Maintains dual awareness: character's reality + performance reality. Prevents psychological danger.

irreverence.name: Irreverence
irreverence.definition: The second stage in the Being → Irreverence → Ultimate Consciousness chain: a courageous, playful disregard for social and theatrical conventions, obligations, and inhibitions that allows the actor to honor all impulses freely without self-censorship, judgment, or premeditation. Not disrespect but freedom — the willingness to be affected by anything and express it fully.
irreverence.details:
Irreverence is both philosophical (a way of approaching life and art — disregarding judgments, social taboos, inhibitions) and practical in rehearsal and performance. Philosophical irreverence: the actor ignores society's judgments about what is appropriate to feel or express. Rehearsal irreverence: exploring material without regard for results. Performance irreverence: honoring organic impulses even when they diverge from rehearsed choices.

Irreverence is the bridge between BEING and Ultimate Consciousness. It cannot exist without a solid BEING state as its foundation. The irreverent actor: is without obligations or concepts, is piqued to that part of them that loves to act and play, courageously honors all impulses, and embraces joy in expressing who they are on stage.

sources.summary: Irreverence is the bridge between BEING and Ultimate Consciousness. Three forms: philosophical, rehearsal, and performance irreverence.
Irreverence is stage two in the chain. Without solid being state, irreverence cannot be reached.

sense_memory.name: Sense Memory
sense_memory.definition: A choice approach in which the actor uses one or more of the five senses to recreate an object, person, place, or experience as a tangible, lived reality — building the sensory experience from its component parts until it becomes real enough to genuinely affect them. The foundation technique of the Stanislavsky/Strasberg Method.
sense_memory.details:
Sense memory works by recreating an experience through all five senses rather than through intellectual recall. The actor asks: What do I see? What do I smell? What do I taste? What do I hear? What do I feel physically? Building the sensory object from its parts creates an organic emotional response. Complex objects (e.g., a phone call) require building foundational sensory elements first (the phone itself) before building the more complex experience (a voice on the phone).

In Morris's system, sense memory is choice approach #1 and serves as the foundation for many other approaches. Imaging (choice approach #26) is used to augment or replace classic sense memory when a visual/kinesthetic approach is more effective. Sense memory and Imaging can be combined (Imaging with Sense Memory).
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.summary: Sense memory is foundation of Stanislavsky/Strasberg Method. Building sensory experience from component parts creates organic response.
Some choices need no translation (an obvious smell choice). Others require building up from the ground — creating a phone before creating a voice on the phone.
Sense memory is choice approach #1 in Morris's 22-technique system.

affective_memory.name: Affective Memory
affective_memory.definition: A choice approach (approach #12 in Morris's system) in which the actor uses a personally meaningful emotional experience from their own life — recreated sensorially — to stimulate the emotional response required by the scene. Sometimes called 'emotional memory' or 'emotional recall.'
affective_memory.details:
Affective memory requires the actor to go back to a real past experience and recreate it sensorially — not by thinking about it intellectually or by reliving the pain, but by recreating the sensory circumstances that surrounded the emotional event. The emotional response is a byproduct of successful sensory recreation, not of direct emotional effort.

Morris treats affective memory as one of many choice approaches — not the dominant technique that Strasberg made it. It should be used when other approaches fail or when the emotional obligation is extreme and deeply personal. Affective Memory Imaging: the actor first creates the experience visually/kinesthetically through imaging rather than pure sensory recall.

sources.summary: Affective Memory is choice approach #12 in Morris's system.
One of 22 choice approaches. Morris treats it as one of many tools, not the dominant technique Strasberg emphasized.
Affective Memory Imaging: actor creates the experience visually/kinesthetically through imaging rather than pure sensory recall.

moment_to_moment.name: Moment-to-Moment
moment_to_moment.definition: The ongoing, unpremeditated responsiveness to whatever is actually happening in the present moment of performance — organic impulses arising from genuine stimuli in real time, rather than preconceived or rehearsed responses. The living pulse of authentic acting.
moment_to_moment.details:
Moment-to-moment acting is the antithesis of result-consciousness and premeditation. The actor responds to what is actually happening — what the other actors do, what is genuinely felt, what organically arises — rather than executing predetermined emotional or behavioral choices. It is the lived experience of BEING in a scene.

In Morris's system, BEING itself is a moment-to-moment state. The entire organism of craft techniques exists to support the actor in having rich enough inner life that genuine moment-to-moment responses can be organic AND dramatically compelling. Premeditation is the enemy of moment-to-moment work.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.summary: Moment-to-moment is the living pulse of authentic acting — responding to what is actually happening, not executing predetermined choices.
Being is moment-to-moment contact with feelings. Moment-to-moment is the antithesis of premeditation.

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Moment-to-moment is the lived experience of BEING in a scene. Premeditation is its enemy.

awareness.name: Awareness
awareness.definition: A multi-dimensional component of consciousness involving both heightened sensory perception of one's immediate environment and outward intellectual/social engagement with the world beyond oneself. A daily discipline of attending to what is actually present — internally and externally.
awareness.details:
Acting from the Ultimate Consciousness defines awareness as heightened perception of one's environment, sensory inputs, and the energies of other people — linked to curiosity. Developed by consciously attending to surroundings: dissecting sounds, exploring textures, learning to sense what is behind you, noticing temperature changes and energy in a room. The goal is to eventually do this habitually without conscious effort.

No Acting Please identifies awareness as an ongoing daily conditioning: preparations for elevating awareness should become a way of life. The instrument must be sensitized before other preparations can work. Sensory awareness covers all five senses plus kinesthetic sense. 'Super Consciousness' is the deliberate decision to become hyper-aware of everything.

Freeing the Actor distinguishes sensory/perceptual awareness (observation) from outward awareness: attending to current events, politics, global issues, history — getting out of oneself and becoming genuinely interested in the larger world, which enriches characterization and empathy.

sources.summary: Awareness linked to curiosity; develop by deliberately attending to surroundings — scanning objects, dissecting sounds, sensing energies. Goal: habitual without conscious effort.
Awareness is ongoing daily conditioning. Instrument must be sensitized. Sensory awareness covers all five senses + kinesthetic. Super Consciousness = deliberate hyper-awareness.
Awareness = attending to the world: current events, politics, global issues. Different from sensory observation. Enriches empathy and characterization.

preparation.name: Preparation
preparation.definition: Everything an actor does to get ready to act — comprising 95% of acting itself. Encompasses both instrumental preparation (freeing the instrument to achieve a BEING state) and craft preparation (working for choices to fulfill scene obligations). The largest part of the acting process.
preparation.details:
Preparation is like a continent with many regions. Pre-preparation: getting ready to get ready, using relaxation, sensitizing, and Personal Inventory. Instrumental preparation: bringing the actor to a BEING state by eliminating blocks, fears, and tensions. Craft preparation: specifically addressing and changing emotional states in relation to material.

Eight major categories of preparation (Irreverent Acting): (1) Standard Relaxation Cluster, (2) Involvement Preparations, (3) Emotional Preparations, (4) Ego Preparations, (5) Large Expurgatives, (6) Relationship Preparations, (7) Interim Preparations, (8) Irreverent Preparations. The same exercise used with different emphasis serves either instrumental or craft purposes.

Types in No Acting Please: (1) Instrumental preparations — tension, fear, inhibition; (2) Relational preparations — connecting to people, objects, places; (3) Role preparations — fulfilling the emotional obligations of the material. Must always deal with instrumental problems first. Knowing yourself and your unique instrumental needs is as important as knowing many exercises.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Being prepared is 95% of acting. Two major areas: instrumental and craft preparation.
Eight major categories of preparation. Same exercise with different emphasis serves instrumental or craft purposes.
Three types: instrumental, relational, and role preparations. Deal with instrumental problems first.

the_craft.name: The Craft
the_craft.definition: The set of exercises, techniques, and processes an actor uses to fulfill the emotional and behavioral obligations of a script by creating genuine internal realities. The 'how' of acting. A method of creating realities on stage and in front of the camera. Without BEING as its foundation, craft is ineffective.
the_craft.details:
Craft encompasses: identifying obligations (emotional, character, thematic, historical, stylistic, subtextual, relational), selecting choices that can stimulate required feelings, and working for those choices through various choice approaches. Many actors talk about craft without having one, or practice abstract intellectual techniques that don't stimulate organic behavior. The key question is not 'what' but 'how.'

Morris's craft system has three main areas: Obligations, Choices, and Choice Approaches. The craft exists to serve BEING, not replace it. Proper proportion: 90% BEING work, 10% technique. The craft of acting can be taught in six months — it is not hard. What is difficult are the obstacles to BEING. Both craft AND faith in the craft are required. The craft is designed to do away with itself — like a surgeon confident because of skill and knowledge.

Morris's system has two major components: the instrument (the actor's self) and the craft (the techniques applied to material). Craft without instrumental preparation as a foundation is ineffective.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Craft section introduction. Playwright gives circumstances and obligations; actor must have a process to affect themselves.
Craft is a process of work. Key question: not 'what' but 'how.' Without BEING as foundation, craft is ineffective.
Craft easy to learn (6 months) — obstacles to BEING are the hard part. 90% BEING, 10% technique. The craft is designed to do away with itself.
The system has two parts: the instrument and the craft.
Three areas of craft system: Obligations, Choices, and Choice Approaches.

splits.name: Splits
splits.definition: A disconnection within the actor's instrument in which different components — physical, emotional, intellectual, and vocal — stop functioning as a unified whole, producing incongruence between what is being expressed in one channel and another. 'The instrument's lie detector.' The direct result of imposing behavior rather than responding organically.
splits.details:
Three primary types (Freeing the Actor): (1) Physical-and-emotional split — body appears disconnected from emotional expression, limbs fixed in positions that don't reflect the emotion. (2) Vocal-and-emotional split — the actor manipulates the voice to express emotions not genuinely felt. (3) Intellectual split — the actor functions from the head, directing expression cerebrally. Multiple simultaneous splits are possible (two-, three-, or four-way).

Splits form when the actor short-circuits the natural stimulus-effect-response-expression process by assuming behaviors, imposing attitudes, or acting instead of BEING. One denial sets up a chain reaction of denials until the lack of reality is epidemic. BEING eliminates splits or mends them when they occur. The antidote is Personal Inventory: reconnecting with real feelings and expressing them.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Splits: physical, intellectual, vocal, or emotional. The instrument is divided into four areas; splits occur when these are not working together.
Three primary splits: physical-emotional, vocal-emotional, intellectual. Multiple simultaneous splits possible. Morris calls splits 'the instrument's lie detector.'
Splits defined: caused by actor imposing behavior instead of responding organically. BEING eliminates or heals splits.

tension.name: Tension
tension.definition: The actor's Number One problem and arch enemy: a pervasive physical and psychological constriction that acts as a stopper on all emotional impulses, forcing actors to impose behavior above the cork rather than express genuine feeling. Also paradoxically an indicator of talent: 'Tension is talent' — the more tension, the more unexpressed emotion and sensitivity.
tension.details:
Manifests in thousands of ways: muscle tightening, sweaty palms, dry mouth, butterflies, trembling, difficulty breathing, chest pressure, inability to think, shaking limbs, stomach knots, neck rigidity, voice changes, urge to urinate. The corked bottle metaphor: tension is the stopper — everything below is trapped. Forces the actor to function 'above' the tension by denying real impulses and replacing them with presentational behavior.

Major causes: fear, ego issues, conditioning, track record, prior failures, low ego-state, insecurities, need to succeed, absence of craft, lack of faith. Even very successful actors experience it (Morris cites Jack Nicholson). Two dangerous types: (1) suppressed tension appearing as false calm; (2) Theatrical Hysteria — tension mistaken for genuine emotion.

Tension exercises provide temporary relief; permanent elimination requires addressing root causes. The Relaxation Cluster (relaxation + sensitizing + personal inventory) is the primary tool for working with tension.

sources.summary: Tension is the actor's arch enemy — a pervasive constriction that caps the instrument. Has many causes; tension exercises provide temporary relief; permanent elimination requires addressing root causes.
Tension is Number One problem. Corked bottle metaphor. 'Tension is talent.' Two dangerous types: suppressed (false calm) and Theatrical Hysteria.

vulnerability.name: Vulnerability
vulnerability.definition: The state of emotional openness and availability to be genuinely affected — the actor's receptiveness to stimuli, people, and circumstances without self-protective filtering. A fundamental quality of talent essential for BEING. Vulnerability rises in direct proportion to impulsivity.
vulnerability.details:
Vulnerability is not weakness; it is the actor's openness to being genuinely affected by stimuli. The same elements that make an actor sensitive and vulnerable also make them insecure, anxious, depressed, and tense — two sides of the same coin. Actors who are shy and quiet are often extremely imaginative and affectable (strong impressive area) but may need help in the expressive area.

Fear of exposure is the primary block to vulnerability. As vulnerability level climbs, so does impulsivity — a necessary ingredient for organic reality. Jack Nicholson's foreword to No Acting Please identifies vulnerability as a key topic. The Vulnerability Workout and Intimate Sharing are primary techniques for building vulnerability.

sources.summary: As vulnerability level climbs, so does impulsivity — necessary ingredient for organic reality. Fear of exposure is the primary block.
Sensitivity, vulnerability, volatility are positive elements of talent. Two sides of the same coin as insecurity and anxiety. Shy actors often strong impressive, weak expressive.

endowments.name: Endowments
endowments.definition: A choice approach in which the actor sensorially creates and attributes specific qualities — appearance, voice, smell, feel, emotional attitudes, physical features — to an already-existing object or person, changing its perceived reality to serve the needs of the scene. Virtually no limitations on what can be accomplished through endowment.
endowments.details:
The endowment process: start with the available actor or object and ask sensory questions directed at changing its perceived elements (eyes, skin, posture, sounds, smells, textures). Works by changing hair color, height, weight, complexion, emotional attitudes, physical manifestations of illness or age, architectural features of a room, props, clothing, even gender.

For endowing another actor: start with similarities between the actual actor and the character you need them to be, then work to change the differences sensorially. Used in relationship obligation work: making the other actor feel like the character they are supposed to represent. Often combined with Selective Emphasis, which focuses attention on specific elements. Sensory Self-Endowment: endowing one's own body with physical or character qualities.

sources.summary: Endowments: sensorially work to create different people in your life in relation to the actors in the play. Ask sensory questions and respond with the appropriate sense.
Endowments: changing an object or person by endowing with qualities it doesn't possess. Virtually no limitations. Start with similarities, change differences sensorially.

ensemble.name: Ensemble
ensemble.definition: The highest form of organic acting: two or more actors functioning entirely from BEING, each fully available to the other's moment-to-moment impulses, creating an emotional chain reaction where even the actors don't know where it will lead. One of the highest forms of relating and acting — a prerequisite for Ultimate Consciousness.
ensemble.details:
Ensemble is not merely good team acting; it is a state of moment-to-moment communication between actors where they affect each other genuinely and respond impulsively to how they have been affected. It requires prerequisite building blocks: being state, selfless involvement, and impulsive responsiveness.

It must be structured through a process and cannot be achieved simply with time or experience. Presenting intellectual concepts of behavior makes real ensemble impossible. Irreverent rehearsal with committed choices by both actors creates the dynamic tension from which ensemble emerges. When one actor has a stronger reality and 'wins,' both actors grow.

When one actor reaches an Ultimate Consciousness state, the other actor in genuine ensemble will be catapulted by that unconscious energy too — ensemble and Ultimate Consciousness are mutually reinforcing. BEING is the prerequisite; without BEING, ensemble is impossible.

sources.summary: Ensemble requires being state, selfless involvement, and impulsive responsiveness. When one actor reaches Ultimate Consciousness, the other in genuine ensemble is catapulted too.
Ensemble: highest form of organic acting. Achieved when actors impulsively available to each other second-to-second. Intellectual concepts make it impossible.

personal_inventory.name: Personal Inventory
personal_inventory.definition: An ongoing self-questioning process — asking 'How do I feel?' repeatedly and responding honestly — to discover the actor's actual emotional and physical state moment-to-moment. Part of the Relaxation Cluster and the starting point for any craft work.
personal_inventory.details:
Done after relaxation to discover what obstacles remain. The actor asks: 'What am I feeling right now? What is my physical state? What emotional life is available to me?' The starting point for determining which instrumental or craft step comes next.

Instrumental use: discovering what you are feeling and getting to a place where you can express impulses moment-to-moment. Craft use: done before the first line and during the scene whenever the actor feels disconnected. In performance: run as a silent Inner Monologue under the lines — its responses become the flow of the character's life.

Personal Inventory is the antidote for premeditation, disconnection, and mechanical performance. Can be done aloud, semi-audibly, or silently. Also used in ensemble preparation sequences.

sources.summary: After relaxing, actor does Personal Inventory to find out where he is and what obstacles exist before proceeding.
Actor repeatedly asks 'How do I feel?' and responds in the moment — fundamental self-checking process. Used in ensemble sequences.
Part of Standard Relaxation Cluster. Antidote for premeditation and disconnection. In performance: run as silent Inner Monologue under lines.

available_stimulus.name: Available Stimulus
available_stimulus.definition: A choice approach that uses what is actually present in the environment — real objects, the actual behavior of the other actor, genuinely existing elements — as stimuli for emotional response. The first consideration when approaching scene work because it is the most immediately accessible. Always start from what is real before extending into what is imagined.
available_stimulus.details:
Can be almost anything: other people, the place itself, sounds, odors, energy, objects, photographs, how someone is dressed, the behavior of the other actor. Mechanics: if you can see it, look at it and relate; same for hearing, smelling, tasting, feeling.

Often only a part of an object or element affects you — use Selective Emphasis to focus on that part. Available Stimulus is not about creating something imaginary but responding to and working with what is genuinely present. Often combined with Selective Emphasis (emphasizing specific elements that produce the desired response) or Selective Enhancement (making those elements bigger).

In imaging contexts: available stimulus provides the real anchor before extending into images beyond what actually exists.

sources.summary: Always start from what is real before extending into what is imagined. Available stimulus is the real anchor.
Use the real qualities of the actors selectively emphasizing their attractiveness to stimulate the required response.
First consideration when preparing to work. Can be almost anything. Use Selective Emphasis to focus on affecting part.

believability.name: Believability
believability.definition: A choice approach in which the actor cultivates the willingness and ability to believe in imaginary circumstances as if they were real, and mixes real and fictional elements so deftly that they become indistinguishable. Both actors work together to build a shared fictional reality that serves the scene.
believability.details:
Like Imaging, believability hooks into the child in the actor — connecting to the sense of play and pretend. Two actors identify obligations and conflict elements, then structure a Believability improvisation together. A mixture of some truth and a lot of untruth; starts with reality and adds elements to promote the relationship and emotional needs of the scene. The deft mixing of truth and untruth makes them indistinguishable — the actors lose track of what is real and what isn't.

Believability is not a decision ('I will believe this') but an accumulated sensory-emotional state built through process. Cumulative imaging + Believability: as the actor repeatedly images the play's realities, these are absorbed into the unconscious and the willingness to believe grows organically. Particularly important as a collaborative tool when both actors need to create a shared relational reality.

sources.summary: Believability hooks into the child/sense of play. Cumulative imaging builds believability organically. Not a decision but an accumulated state built through process.
Mixture of truth and untruth. Both actors identify obligations then structure a Believability improvisation. Actors lose track of what is real vs. fictional.

evocative_words.name: Evocative Words
evocative_words.definition: A choice approach that uses specific words or short phrases — personally emotionally laden with meaning — spoken internally, semi-audibly, or externally to access and maintain a specific emotional state during scene work. Works through verbal/linguistic association rather than sensory recreation.
evocative_words.details:
The words are personally specific — they evoke a response because of what they mean to this particular actor, not because of their dictionary definition. Two types of content: (1) Experiences from the actor's life — shorthand phrases describing key sensory/emotional elements; (2) Belief structures — things the actor feels very strongly about (justice, fascism, love, moral codes).

Particularly useful when the actor has many lines and moves quickly — where sensory work would interfere with the behavioral obligation of the scene. Can be done as a preparation, semi-audibly, or silently as an Inner Monologue during scene work. Can be used in conjunction with other choice approaches.

sources.summary: Evocative Words: personally specific words/phrases evoking a response because of their personal meaning. Used internally during scene work.
Two types: experiences (shorthand phrases) and belief structures. Particularly useful with many lines and quick movement. Can be done as preparation or silent inner monologue.

selective_emphasis.name: Selective Emphasis
selective_emphasis.definition: A technique of focusing attention on and amplifying only the part of an object, person, or stimulus that emotionally affects you, rather than the whole — intensifying the emotional impact by creatively manipulating how you perceive something to make it serve the scene's needs.
selective_emphasis.details:
Often only part of an object or person affects you; the whole may have a different or diluted impact. Used in conjunction with Available Stimulus, Endowments, Imaging, and many other choice approaches. Creative Manipulation: adjusting how you interpret or relate to an element so it creates the desired response. Example: 'That twinkle in her eyes: every time she looks at me she lights up for me — it's all for me!' Selective Isolation: close focus on a specific element while letting everything else recede.

Also used with Imaging: when only part of a choice is impelling, the actor does not recreate the whole experience but zeros in on the meaningful component. Example: if an obligation requires grief, and the choice is a complex event, the actor selectively emphasizes only the grief-producing moments.

sources.summary: Selective Emphasis: used with imaging when only part of a choice is meaningful — actor amplifies that part, de-emphasizes the rest.
Focus on specific part of object/person that affects you. Creative Manipulation: adjust interpretation to create desired response. Often combined with Available Stimulus.

disconnected_impulsivity.name: Disconnected Impulsivity
disconnected_impulsivity.definition: A technique for stimulating quick, unpremeditated impulse responses — either as a preparation or in-scene tool — in which the actor expresses impulses, images, words, and associations at high speed with no logical connection required, as a way of eliminating leadership (premeditation) and piquing unconscious material.
disconnected_impulsivity.details:
Originally used to antidote concept and leadership (intellectual control) in acting. Morris discovered it also stimulates unconscious responses: some impulses feel unconnected to anything conscious and seem to come from an unconscious source. All responses are in image form.

One-person version: actor starts with current environment, expresses impulses verbally, vocally, or silently as they arise. Two-person version: a director/facilitator provides a single suggestion ('Grade-school classroom') and calls out 'Switch!' at intervals to force rapid transitions.

In-scene use: impulses activated under lines, between lines, or while listening to the other actor. What it looks like from outside: a person responding naturally to the environment, the other character, and their own thoughts. Very natural; charges low-energy scenes; excites relationships. Should only be used for as long as needed — not indiscriminately.

sources.summary: Originally antidoted concept/leadership; also stimulates unconscious responses. Two versions: one-person and two-person (with facilitator calling 'Switch!').
Used to eliminate leadership. Impulses activated under/between lines or while listening. Very natural; charges low-energy scenes.

imaging.name: Imaging
imaging.definition: A structured, sensory-rich mental visualization process in which the actor creates vivid internal experiences — seeing, hearing, smelling, tasting, and feeling — with the purpose of stimulating authentic emotional life, communicating with the unconscious, and fulfilling acting obligations. Choice Approach #26 in Morris's system and his fifth MEGAPPROACH.
imaging.details:
Morris distinguishes imaging from casual daydreaming or 'head-only' visualization. Imaging must involve all five senses simultaneously, not just the visual. The image should leave the 'inner screen' and surround the actor as a living reality — the actor is IN the image, not watching it from outside.

Imaging can be used instrumentally (to free the actor's instrument) and as craft (to fulfill scene obligations). When practiced repeatedly, images sink into the unconscious and begin to influence behavior organically. The unconscious 'speaks' in images (Jung: 'Images are the language of the unconscious') — imaging is therefore the primary conduit between conscious craft work and unconscious impulse.

Morris identifies seven imaging techniques: (1) Story Imaging — creating an experience as a sequential narrative; (2) Fragmented Imaging — random, disconnected images; (3) Verbal Imaging — creating imagery through evocative words; (4) Fantasy Imaging — imaginary, non-real scenarios; (5) Imaging with Sense Memory — combining sensory recall with visual imaging; (6) Symbolic Imaging — images that represent abstract realities; (7) Involuntary Imaging — allowing the unconscious to generate images without direction.

sources.summary: Imaging defined: must involve all five senses simultaneously; actor is IN the image. Choice Approach #26, fifth MEGAPPROACH. Seven imaging techniques identified.
Jung epigraph: 'Images are the language of the unconscious.' Imaging is the primary conduit to unconscious impulse.

the_unconscious.name: The Unconscious
the_unconscious.definition: The vast reservoir of stored experience, emotion, impulse, and psychic material that exists beneath conscious awareness and that contains approximately 95% of the actor's true talent (Strasberg). The unconscious speaks a different language from consciousness — images and symbols, not words. Reaching the unconscious is the actor's ultimate goal.
the_unconscious.details:
C.G. Jung: 'Images are the language of the unconscious.' The unconscious stores all past experiences in its own organizational system; conscious work with imaging, dreams, and sensory exploration creates a conduit to it. Reaching the unconscious produces unforgettable performances — acting from the unconscious is the highest form of the art.

The unconscious can be programmed intentionally through imaging, dream work, and other techniques. Subpersonalities (sub-personalities) are archetypes of the collective unconscious that can be accessed to enrich character work. Involuntary imaging is one key signal that the unconscious has been reached — images arise without conscious direction.

The homeostasis of the unconscious resists change; programming must overcome this resistance. The bridge between the unconscious and consciousness is built through: the BEING state, the Relaxation Cluster, imaging practice, dream journaling, and expanded consciousness work.

sources.summary: Jung epigraph: 'Images are the language of the unconscious.' 95% of true talent is in the unconscious (Strasberg).
The unconscious is reached through the being → irreverence → ultimate consciousness chain. Unconscious impulses feed into conscious behavior in Ultimate Consciousness.

subpersonalities.name: Subpersonalities (Sub-Personalities)
subpersonalities.definition: Distinct internal personality energies within every person — archetypes and psychological forces derived from Jungian psychology and Dr. Hal Stone's Voice Dialogue technique — that can be accessed and used to fulfill character obligations. The actor identifies which sub-personality the character runs from and elicits the matching sub-personality within themselves.
subpersonalities.details:
Origin: Carl Jung's theory of multiple personality facets; elaborated by Dr. Hal Stone and Dr. Sidra Stone's Voice Dialogue system; adapted by Morris for acting. Primary selves: created to protect the Vulnerable Child — Protector, Controller, Pleaser, Pusher, Inner Critic, Perfectionist, Achiever, Workaholic, Moralist. Disowned selves (often surface in dreams): Magical Child, Mischievous Child, Vulnerable Child, Warrior, Killer, Aphrodite/Don Juan, Satanic energies, Beach Bum, Dictator.

The Aware Ego is the goal of Voice Dialogue work — a conscious center that can dialogue with any sub-personality without being run by it. In acting: the actor identifies which sub-personality dominates the character, then uses journal work or specific choices to elicit and facilitate that sub-personality in themselves.

One of the three choice approaches most recommended for characterization (alongside Externals and Affective Memory). Each sub-personality has one job — to be exactly what it is. Ten specific approach techniques exist for working with sub-personalities.

sources.summary: Sub-personalities are archetypes accessed to fulfill character element obligations. One of three approaches most recommended for characterization.
Derived from Jungian psychology and Voice Dialogue (Dr. Hal Stone). Major archetypes listed. Aware Ego is the conscious center that dialogues without being run by sub-personalities.
Subpersonalities: primary selves (protect Vulnerable Child) vs. disowned selves (often surface in dreams). The Aware Ego is the goal of Voice Dialogue work.

dependencies.name: Dependencies
dependencies.definition: Habitual behavioral patterns an actor develops over time that substitute for organic impulses and block authentic expression. Verbal, physical, intellectual, vocal, or emotional patterns that actors repeat because they feel they work, eventually becoming automatic substitutes for genuine impulse.
dependencies.details:
Dependencies arise when an actor receives positive feedback for a certain behavior and begins repeating it outside of organic context. They are often mistaken for personality traits or personal style. A bag of dependencies substitutes for genuine impulse.

Types: vocal dependencies (repeating a tone or rhythm that 'sounds good'), physical dependencies (repeated gestures or postures), behavioral dependencies (habitual emotional defaults), emotional dependencies (always going to the same emotion), intellectual dependencies (always 'thinking through' a scene the same way). All share the common feature of disconnecting the actor from real moment-to-moment impulses.

Related to mannerisms but broader — includes patterns across all channels. The antidote is returning to BEING: genuine moment-to-moment responsiveness replaces all dependencies with authentic organic behavior.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Dependencies: verbal, physical, intellectual, vocal, emotional. Created by repeating things that seem to work. Actor becomes enamored of their own patterns.
Dependencies arise from positive feedback; repeated outside organic context; mistaken for personality traits. Types: vocal, physical, behavioral, emotional, intellectual.

selfless_involvement.name: Selfless Involvement
selfless_involvement.definition: A state of complete genuine interest in and attention to the other actor, the environment, or an external stimulus — removing self-consciousness and self-monitoring — as a prerequisite for genuine ensemble. The foundational antidote to self-consciousness, compensational behavior, and most acting traps.
selfless_involvement.details:
Selfless involvement doesn't mean the actor is empty — it liberates them from internal commentary and self-evaluation. When an actor is truly selflessly involved, they are not prone to compensating, splitting, or redirecting. The self-consciousness dissolves because attention is fully directed outward.

Achieved through exercises like: Being Workout (two-person), Wonder/Perceive/Observe, Looking for Things You've Never Noticed Before, Observe-Perceive-Wonder, and Personal Point of View. When both actors are selflessly involved, they are ready for ensemble preparation exercises.

sources.summary: Step 4 in ensemble preparation. Options: Two-Person Being Workout, Wonder/Perceive/Observe, silent observation. Takes actors away from concern with themselves.
Selfless involvement liberates actor from internal commentary. When truly selflessly involved, actor doesn't compensate, split, or redirect.

characterization.name: Characterization
characterization.definition: The actor's process of creating the life of a character not by 'becoming someone else' but by absorbing the character INTO oneself — using the actor's own multidimensional humanity as the instrument. Character elements are stimulated through choices and selective emphasis; the actor finds their own personal basis for each behavior. Versatility is a function of range of self-expression, not ability to pretend to be someone else.
characterization.details:
Morris explicitly rejects the conventional notion of 'wearing the cloak of another person.' Morris even discourages the word 'character' because it implies separation from self. Everything onstage must come from the actor's own reality.

Four sources for understanding character: (1) what the author says about the character; (2) what other characters say to or about them; (3) what the character says about themselves; (4) the character's actions and interactions throughout the play.

Three choice approaches most recommended for characterization: Subpersonalities, Externals, and Affective Memory. The character is who the playwright says they are PLUS the unique individuality of the actor. When a play is cast, actors are usually chosen for similarities to the character — so the gap is often smaller than feared.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.summary: Characterization: absorb character INTO oneself, not become someone else. Four sources for understanding character. Three recommended approaches: sub-personalities, externals, affective memory.

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Discourages the word 'character' due to implication of separation from self. Everything from actor's own reality. Versatility from range of self-expression.

fear_of_exposure.name: Fear of Exposure
fear_of_exposure.definition: The terror actors feel about being truly seen — having their authentic feelings, vulnerabilities, and inner life visible to others. The reluctance to express personal, private impulses publicly, rooted in childhood conditioning against sharing personal matters openly. A major block to BEING freedom and vulnerability.
fear_of_exposure.details:
Fear of exposure operates even when the actor intellectually knows exposure is safe. It keeps actors from BEING in life and on stage. Causes actors to impose emotions that don't come from a real place, creating conceptual, presentational acting.

Organic acting requires being able to impulsively express moment-to-moment emotional impulses in public view. Fear of exposure is the primary block to vulnerability. Addressed progressively: begin by sharing intimately with a trusted friend (Intimate Sharing exercise), then expand to larger groups.

sources.summary: Conditioning against sharing private feelings creates presentational actors. Address progressively with trusted friends, then expand.
Major block to BEING freedom. Intimate Sharing exercise specifically designed to address this fear.

inner_monologue.name: Inner Monologue
inner_monologue.definition: The continuous, silent stream of consciousness the actor maintains under and through the spoken lines of a scene during performance — the actor's internal reality running alongside the text, keeping the actor on the 'reality track' and preventing imposed or mechanical behavior.
inner_monologue.details:
In real life, everyone always has some kind of internal monologue going on. Acting cuts this off — the Inner Monologue choice approach restores it. If the choice is genuinely affecting the actor, the Inner Monologue will become the flow of life of the character — a collaboration between actor and character. This allows the character to inhabit the actor. The actor does NOT become the character; the character becomes the actor.

The nature of the Inner Monologue is decided by what the choice approach stimulates — it is not written or scripted but arises organically from the emotional work. Instrumental use: acknowledging distractions, real feelings, and impulses arising from the scene's circumstances. Personal Inventory (asking 'How do I feel?') is a form of Inner Monologue. Craft use: maintaining the character's emotional life silently under lines.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Inner monologue: keep Stream of Consciousness under lines. Acknowledges distractions and real feelings. Keeps actor from drifting into imposed behavior.
Silent stream of consciousness under lines. Character inhabits actor — actor does not become character, character becomes actor. Arises organically from emotional work.

relaxation_cluster.name: Relaxation Cluster
relaxation_cluster.definition: The mandatory group of preparatory exercises that constitute the actor's first step in any work session: a relaxation technique followed by Sensitizing and Personal Inventory. The Standard Relaxation Cluster is done at the beginning of every rehearsal in Morris's system.
relaxation_cluster.details:
Purpose: release tension (which covers real emotional life), elevate sensory accessibility, contact moment-to-moment feelings, freely express impulses. Tension is the actor's primary enemy — it blocks authentic emotional life. Only after achieving the Relaxation Cluster state can the actor address craft obligations organically.

The specific composition of the cluster varies — it is not a single technique but a group. Without it, tension becomes an obstacle to everything else. The Relaxation Cluster is listed as one of the eight major categories of preparation in Irreverent Acting.

sources.summary: Relaxation Cluster: relaxation technique + Sensitizing + Personal Inventory. Purpose: release tension, elevate sensory access, contact moment-to-moment feelings.
Begin every rehearsal with the relaxation-cluster. Once in touch with themselves, move to relationship preparation.

imagination.name: Imagination
imagination.definition: The ability to depart from conventional, reality-bound thinking and create alternate worlds, scenarios, and perspectives — an integral part of consciousness that must be exercised daily. The actor's capacity to pretend, create imaginary circumstances, and believe in realities that do not exist. Essential to all creative work.
imagination.details:
Everyone has imagination. Children's imaginations are available and naturally strong (radio dramas, storytelling, play); social conditioning creates membranes that contain and subordinate it in adulthood. Society does not encourage pretending in adults. Actors must work on imagination daily as part of living preparation.

Even cliched or unoriginal fantasies are valuable because they stretch the imaginative faculty. The key is departing from the conventional. Daily imaginative exercises must go hand-in-hand with ego preparations, sensitizing, sense memory, and vulnerability work. Start from your stretch point and go further — there are no boundaries to imagination within reason.

sources.summary: Even unoriginal fantasies exercise imagination by departing from the conventional. Imagination was strong in childhood; must be recultivated.
Children's imaginations are available; conditioning creates membranes. Daily exercises must accompany ego and vulnerability work. No boundaries within reason.

organic_reality.name: Organic Reality
organic_reality.definition: Genuine, unforced reality created from the actor's actual inner life and BEING state. The opposite of impositional, representational acting. Appears as bumpy, unpredictable, spontaneous behavior — not smooth or polished — because real people in life are bumpy, easily derailed, and spontaneous.
organic_reality.details:
Organic reality comes from BEING and shows up as behavior that may look unprofessional by conventional standards but is far more compelling to audiences. Real people forget what they were going to say, get distracted, are surprised. Organic acting mirrors this.

The craft is designed to do away with itself — the more skilled the actor, the less the skill shows, and the more organic the result appears. Organic reality is the goal of the entire Morris system. BEING is the only place from which organic reality can be created.

sources.summary: Organic reality looks bumpy and unpredictable. The craft is designed to do away with itself — skill becomes invisible.
BEING is the foundation of organic reality. Organic reality is the goal of the entire system.

organic_impulse.name: Organic Impulse
organic_impulse.definition: An authentic, spontaneous feeling or behavioral response that arises naturally from the actor's instrument without imposition or intellectual control. The raw material of truthful acting — stimulated by craft choices and approaches but never manufactured or imposed.
organic_impulse.details:
The goal of both BEING preparation and craft work is to stimulate organic impulses appropriate to the material. Organic impulses are distinguished from premeditated or imposed behavior by their spontaneity, unpredictability, and authenticity. When the actor is in a genuine BEING state, organic impulses flow freely and continuously.

Craft techniques stimulate organic behavior; intellectual techniques prevent it. The stimulus-effect-response-expression process is the natural sequence through which organic impulses arise. Premeditation short-circuits this process.

sources.summary: Craft techniques stimulate organic behavior; intellectual techniques prevent it.
Instrumental exercises help actors access organic impulses through preparation.

impulses.name: Impulses
impulses.definition: The spontaneous internal responses — emotional, physical, and intellectual — that arise moment-to-moment from the actor's genuine engagement with stimuli. The living substance of authentic acting. All impulses have equal value and must be honored, not suppressed or selected.
impulses.details:
Morris's fundamental principle: all impulses are valid and must be expressed or included. Suppressing any impulse caps everything — this is the 'Number Six' principle (all emotions equal value). The BEING state is defined by the free flow of impulses. Impulse expression is what distinguishes BEING from acting.

Impulses can be stimulated through choice approaches (sense memory, imaging, available stimulus, etc.) but must not be manufactured. The Disconnected Impulsivity technique specifically trains rapid, unpremeditated impulse expression. In ensemble work, impulsive responsiveness to the other actor creates the highest form of organic acting.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.summary: All impulses are valid and must be honored. Suppressing any caps everything.
BEING BEFORE DOING. All emotions equal value — Everything is a Number Six.

the_instrument.name: The Instrument (see: instrument)
the_instrument.note: Alias entry. See 'instrument' for the full merged entry.

being_state.name: Being State (see: being)
being_state.note: Alias entry. See 'being' for the full merged entry.

being_and_doing_philosophy.name: Being and Doing Philosophy (see: being)
being_and_doing_philosophy.note: Alias entry. See 'being' for the full merged entry.

sub_personalities.name: Sub-Personalities (see: subpersonalities)
sub_personalities.note: Alias entry. See 'subpersonalities' for the full merged entry.

craft.name: Craft (see: the_craft)
craft.note: Alias entry. See 'the_craft' for the full merged entry.

choice.name: Choice (see: choices)
choice.note: Alias entry. See 'choices' for the full merged entry.

choice_approach.name: Choice Approach (see: choice_approaches)
choice_approach.note: Alias entry. See 'choice_approaches' for the full merged entry.

externals.name: Externals
externals.definition: A category of choice approaches (choice approach #10) that seeks to get a physical sense of an animal, another person, an inanimate object, or an insect through the actor's own instrument — working from the outside in.
