When accomplished and translated into human behavior, the actor is affected on multiple levels: physically, emotionally, intellectually, and vocally.
externals.details:
Four separate involvements: Animals, People, Inanimate Objects, Insects. Goal: experiencing a sense of any one of these externals through one's own body, then translating it into human behavior.

Animals: excite the primitive, shake primal impulses loose; useful for physically animal characters (Stanley Kowalski). People: the actor doesn't need to translate behavior into human terms — still works organically. The entire approach could be an acting system by itself (Morris's statement). Keys to the unconscious: approaching inner life from the outside in.

One of the three choice approaches most recommended for characterization (along with Affective Memory and Sub-Personalities). The primitive pathway from externals → inner life → unconscious is one of the deepest paths to authentic character.

sources.summary: Animals, people, insects, inanimate objects. One of three approaches most recommended for characterization. Excites the primitive.
Choice approach #10. Four involvements. An entire acting approach could be built from just this one area. Keys to the unconscious — outside-in path.

on_the_nose_acting.name: On-the-Nose Acting
on_the_nose_acting.definition: Acting in which the actor literally and directly performs exactly what the text calls for, without any subtext, organic variation, or authentic inner life — the most common and most deadly form of presentational acting.
on_the_nose_acting.details: On-the-nose acting occurs when the actor imposes the 'obvious' emotional or behavioral response called for by the text, rather than finding an organic response from their genuine inner life. The result is predictable, two-dimensional, and theatrically flat. The antidote is returning to BEING and using choice approaches to create genuine internal realities that produce authentic, surprising responses.

sources.summary: On-the-nose acting: literally performing what the text calls for. Produces predictable, flat, two-dimensional performances.

impositional_technique.name: Impositional Technique
impositional_technique.definition: Any acting approach in which the actor decides in advance what to feel and do and then imposes that predetermined behavior onto the performance, rather than allowing genuine organic impulses to arise. The antithesis of BEING.
impositional_technique.details: Impositional technique produces splits — a disconnection between what the actor is actually feeling and what they are showing. It includes: deciding what emotion to 'play,' indicating rather than experiencing, using external gestures or vocal patterns to 'show' an emotion, and executing a preconceived result. The term describes any technique that substitutes intellectual decision-making for organic impulse. It is contrasted throughout Morris's work with BEING and organic reality.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.summary: Impositional technique: actor decides what to feel and imposes it. Produces splits. Antithesis of BEING.

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Imposition: imposing predetermined behavior onto performance rather than responding organically.

stimulus_effect_response_expression.name: Stimulus → Effect → Response → Expression
stimulus_effect_response_expression.definition: The natural organic sequence through which authentic acting proceeds: a stimulus (external or internal) produces an effect on the actor; the effect produces a response; the response finds expression through behavior, voice, and physicality. Premeditation short-circuits this process by bypassing the effect and response stages.
stimulus_effect_response_expression.details: BEING promotes this natural cycle. When the actor is in a genuine BEING state, stimuli (from choices, from the other actor, from the environment) naturally produce effects, which produce responses, which find authentic expression. When the actor imposes behavior, they jump directly to expression without the preceding organic stages — the result is acting rather than BEING.

sources.summary: BEING promotes natural stimulus-effect-response-expression cycle. Premeditation short-circuits this process.

inclusion.name: Inclusion
inclusion.definition: The practice of acknowledging and incorporating everything that is actually happening — internal distractions, external stimuli, personal feelings, technical concerns — into the fabric of the scene rather than fighting against it or pretending it doesn't exist.
inclusion.details: Whatever is happening is real and can be used. If the actor is nervous, that nervousness can be included. If there is a sound from outside, it can be included. Inclusion is the practical application of BEING in performance — whatever is, IS, and can be part of the actor's reality. The opposite of inclusion is suppression or fighting: battling distractions, denying real feelings, maintaining a fictional state against the pressure of reality.

sources.summary: Whatever is happening is real and can be used. Inclusion is the practical application of BEING — whatever is, IS.

emotional_point_of_view.name: Emotional Point of View
emotional_point_of_view.definition: The specific emotional lens through which a character views their world, other characters, and events — the overall feeling-tone that colors all the character's behavior and responses throughout the play or scene.
emotional_point_of_view.details: Different from the emotional obligation (what the character specifically feels in a scene), the emotional point of view is more like a general orientation — a pervasive mood or attitude that underlies all behavior. The actor identifies the character's emotional point of view through analysis of the material and then works to inhabit it organically through choices and choice approaches.

sources.summary: The emotional lens through which a character views their world — pervasive mood underlying all behavior.

social_obligation.name: Social Obligation
social_obligation.definition: The internalized societal rules, behavioral norms, and conditioning that restrict what the actor is 'allowed' to feel, express, or do — both in life and on stage. A primary obstacle to BEING. Morris distinguishes social obligations (external conditioning) from material obligations (what the script demands).
social_obligation.details: Social obligations are among the deepest obstacles to BEING: the conditioning not to show certain emotions, not to be too sexual, too angry, too vulnerable, too silly in public. These restrictions operate even in the relatively 'safe' environment of an acting class or rehearsal. Freedom from social obligations is a prerequisite for BEING and must be actively cultivated through instrumental work.

sources.summary: Social obligations are internalized societal rules restricting what the actor can feel or express. Primary obstacle to BEING.

organic_acting.name: Organic Acting
organic_acting.definition: Acting that arises naturally from the actor's genuine inner life — from real impulses, authentic emotions, and true moment-to-moment responsiveness — as opposed to mechanical, technical, or impositional acting. The goal of the Morris system.
organic_acting.details: Organic acting looks bumpy, unpredictable, and spontaneous. It does not look 'acted.' It emerges when the actor is in a genuine BEING state and the craft has done its work invisibly. The actor's craft is designed to do away with itself — leaving only organic behavior in its wake. Organic acting is distinct from naturalistic acting: it is not 'copying life' but creating genuine inner reality that produces authentic behavior.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Organic acting: arises from genuine inner life. Craft makes itself invisible. The goal of the system.
Organic acting: bumpy, unpredictable, spontaneous. Not copying life but creating genuine inner reality.

talent.name: Talent
talent.definition: Sensitivity, vulnerability, and the capacity to feel and express a wide range of emotions authentically — the innate qualities that make an actor potentially great. Talent is not a fixed quantity; it can be liberated or suppressed depending on how much the actor's instrument is freed.
talent.details: The same qualities that make an actor talented (sensitivity, vulnerability) also make them anxious, insecure, and tense. 'Tension is talent' — suppressed talent manifests as tension. Most actors have far more talent than they can access because of instrumental blocks. The goal of instrumental work is to liberate existing talent rather than to create new talent. 95% of an actor's talent resides in the unconscious (Strasberg).
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Talent is sensitivity and vulnerability. Instrumental work liberates existing talent.
'Tension is talent' — suppressed talent manifests as tension. 95% of talent in the unconscious.

conditioning.name: Conditioning
conditioning.definition: The accumulated social, familial, cultural, and personal training that teaches the actor what is and isn't acceptable to feel or express — creating behavioral restrictions that function as obstacles to BEING. Internalized throughout childhood and reinforced by social norms throughout life.
conditioning.details: Conditioning is the source of most instrumental obstacles. It creates: fear of exposure, social obligations, presentational behavior, splits, and dependencies. It is not wrong — it allowed the person to survive socially — but it restricts the actor's range. Morris's instrumental work is largely the work of de-conditioning: safely confronting and dissolving the restrictions conditioning has placed on the instrument.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.summary: Conditioning creates fear of exposure, social obligations, and behavioral restrictions that block BEING.
Social conditioning teaches what is and isn't acceptable to express. De-conditioning is the work of instrumental preparation.

result_consciousness.name: Result Consciousness
result_consciousness.definition: The actor's preoccupation with how they look, sound, and are perceived from the outside — monitoring the external product of their performance rather than being internally genuine. The opposite of authentic moment-to-moment presence; a primary obstacle to BEING.
result_consciousness.details: Result consciousness makes the actor an observer of their own performance rather than a participant in it. It causes self-consciousness, tension, and the impulse to impose 'good' behavior. The antidote is redirecting attention inward (Personal Inventory) and outward to the other actor (Selfless Involvement), rather than toward self-evaluation. Directors who give result notes ('Be angrier here') rather than process notes perpetuate result consciousness in actors.

sources.summary: Result consciousness: preoccupation with how the performance looks from outside. Antidote: Personal Inventory and Selfless Involvement.

missing_link_in_the_method.name: Missing Link in the Method
missing_link_in_the_method.definition: The gap in the Stanislavsky/Strasberg system caused by the failure to address instrumental preparation — the need to free the actor's emotional instrument before craft techniques can work. Morris identifies BEING as the missing link that bridges theory and application.
missing_link_in_the_method.details: Actors who were naturally emotionally free succeeded with the Method; those with emotional blocks could not apply it. Stanislavsky himself admitted his approach was incomplete. Without instrumental preparation, even valid craft techniques are useless for actors with blocks. Morris's first two books addressed this gap directly.

sources.summary: Morris discovered the missing link: instrumental preparation bridges theory and application. Actors with blocks could not use the Method.

premeditation.name: Premeditation
premeditation.definition: The act of deciding in advance what to feel and how to behave in a scene — planning emotional and behavioral responses rather than allowing them to arise organically in the moment. The primary enemy of authentic acting and the antithesis of moment-to-moment work.
premeditation.details: Premeditation short-circuits the natural stimulus-effect-response-expression cycle. It produces splits, on-the-nose acting, and mechanical performance. The Inner Monologue, Disconnected Impulsivity, and Personal Inventory are choice approaches specifically designed to break premeditated patterns during performance.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.summary: Premeditation short-circuits the natural stimulus-effect-response-expression cycle.
Premeditation is the primary enemy of authentic acting and moment-to-moment work.

imposition.name: Imposition
imposition.definition: The act of forcing predetermined emotional or behavioral responses onto a performance from the outside rather than allowing them to arise organically. The fundamental error of non-BEING acting — related to but broader than impositional technique.
imposition.details: Imposition can occur at all levels: imposing an emotion ('I will be angry'), imposing a characterization ('this character is cold'), imposing a physical behavior ('I will walk slowly'). All are forms of the same error: substituting intellectual decision for organic impulse. The result is splits, on-the-nose acting, and mechanical behavior.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Imposition: forcing predetermined responses from outside. The fundamental error of non-BEING acting.

compensation.name: Compensation
compensation.definition: The substitution of a false, invented, or generic behavior for a genuine organic impulse — the actor 'filling in' a response because they don't actually have the real one. A product of splits, imposition, and result consciousness.
compensation.details: Compensation occurs when the actor doesn't actually feel what the scene requires and substitutes a learned or generic substitute behavior. It creates a veneer of 'acting' that is recognizable as performance rather than reality. Compensational behavior often becomes a dependency over time. The antidote is returning to BEING and doing the genuine instrumental and craft work to create real inner life.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Compensation: substituting false behavior for genuine impulse. Creates veneer of 'acting' recognizable as performance.
Compensational behavior often becomes a dependency. Antidote: genuine BEING and craft work.

the_method.name: The Method
the_method.definition: The Stanislavsky-derived acting system brought to America by Lee Strasberg and the Actors Studio, focusing on sense memory, affective memory, and psychological truth. Morris's system is built on Method foundations but extends and corrects it — adding instrumental preparation as the missing link.
the_method.details: Morris studied with Strasberg and deeply respects the Method's contributions. His critique: it does not address instrumental preparation — the need to free the actor's emotional instrument before craft techniques can work. The Method's failures are not failures of its techniques but failures of application to blocked instruments. Morris's system fills this gap.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.summary: The Method: Strasberg's Actors Studio system. Morris builds on it but adds instrumental preparation as the missing link.

relaxation.name: Relaxation
relaxation.definition: The first step in the Relaxation Cluster and the foundational prerequisite for all acting work — the physical and psychological release of tension that allows the actor's genuine inner life to become accessible and expressible.
relaxation.details: Relaxation is not torpor or sleep but a state of available readiness — the body and mind free from habitual tension patterns. Various relaxation techniques exist in Morris's system. Tension is the actor's arch enemy; relaxation is its antidote. Without relaxation, all subsequent instrumental and craft work is compromised. The Relaxation Cluster (relaxation + sensitizing + personal inventory) is the standard beginning of every work session.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.summary: Relaxation: first step of Relaxation Cluster. Physical/psychological release of tension allowing genuine inner life to be accessible.

sensitizing.name: Sensitizing
sensitizing.definition: The second step of the Relaxation Cluster: exercises that elevate sensory awareness and emotional accessibility after relaxation — waking up the instrument's capacity to be affected by stimuli.
sensitizing.details: After relaxation removes tension, sensitizing opens the instrument's impressive (receiving) capacity. Exercises include: awareness of specific sensory inputs, kinesthetic awareness, emotional inventory, and personal sharing. Sensitizing is what transforms the post-relaxation state from emptiness into availability — the actor is not just tension-free but actively receptive.

sources.summary: Second step of Relaxation Cluster. Elevates sensory awareness and emotional accessibility after relaxation.

philosophical_irreverence.name: Philosophical Irreverence
philosophical_irreverence.definition: The first state of irreverence — accepting and embracing irreverence as a way of life and work. Accepting yourself: who you are, what you feel, how you express yourself, and how you experience the world as the most important contribution you have to make as an actor.
philosophical_irreverence.details: Requires courage not to run scared under the pressure of a take or an entrance. Means standing your ground when others in the production work differently. Requires communicating your process to the director so they understand and support it.

sources.summary: Philosophical irreverence: accepting yourself and your process as the most important contribution you have to make.

rehearsal_irreverence.name: Rehearsal Irreverence
rehearsal_irreverence.definition: The state of irreverence in rehearsal — exploring choices adventurously, following impulses wherever they lead, experimenting without yet knowing what works, and using the rehearsal period for discovery rather than polishing.
rehearsal_irreverence.details: The most adventurous and experimental stage of the creative process. Actor identifies obligations, selects choices, and uses choice approaches while remaining irreverent in their execution. Actor includes all peripheral life stimulated by choices AND what is happening on stage. Blocking imposed by directors should be used creatively rather than resisted.

sources.summary: Rehearsal irreverence: exploring choices adventurously, following impulses wherever they lead. Discovery rather than polishing.

performance_irreverence.name: Performance Irreverence
performance_irreverence.definition: The state of irreverence in performance — by this stage the actor has found the right choices and approaches, knows when to use each, but remains available to unexpected impulses and includes them as nourishment rather than threats.
performance_irreverence.details: The 'seeking and exploring' of rehearsal has borne fruit — the choices are set, the role is created. Actor knows what choices to use and when to shift from one to another. New, unexpected impulses in performance are 'food' of creative impulses rather than problems. In long runs, choices may need refreshing to maintain organic life.

sources.summary: Performance irreverence: choices set, role created. Unexpected impulses are food, not problems. Long runs require choice refreshing.

threshold_of_interest.name: Threshold of Interest
threshold_of_interest.definition: The principle that it is always easier and more productive to relate to things that genuinely interest you than to force involvement with things that don't. Start with what is actually interesting to you — even a small part of an object or place — and work outward from there.
threshold_of_interest.details: Applied to involvement work, this principle means: look for the parts of a place, object, or person that actually fascinate you. Applied to choice work, it leads you right into the heart of the choice. Saves time and energy — authentic interest generates real involvement without effort.

sources.summary: Start with what genuinely interests you. Authentic interest generates real involvement without effort.

involvement.name: Involvement
involvement.definition: The natural enemy of tension and self-consciousness: the capacity to be genuinely absorbed by — selflessly interested in — people, objects, and the environment. Essential for authentic relationship work and a prerequisite for selfless involvement.
involvement.details: Threshold of Interest is the principle: start with what genuinely interests you. Never force involvement. Available-stimulus sweep, wonder/perceive/observe, and sensory exploration are the primary involvement techniques. Without selfless involvement, the actor cannot achieve authentic relationship or ensemble.

sources.summary: Involvement is the natural enemy of tension. Start with threshold of interest. Never force.

ego_preparation.name: Ego Preparations
ego_preparation.definition: A category of craft preparations designed to address and build the actor's ego state in relation to the character's ego state. The first step is identifying what the character's ego state is, then what the actor's current ego state is, and then choosing a preparation to bridge the gap.
ego_preparation.details: The gauge: examine the character's ego state (how do they feel about themselves generally? In the world? In this scene?) vs. the actor's current state. If the gap is large, a preparation targeting ego is needed. Techniques include Sensory Self-Endowment (feeling better about one's body), positive self-affirmations, and selective emphasis on one's genuine strengths. Part of the eight major categories of preparation.

sources.summary: Ego preparations: identify character's vs. actor's ego state. Choose preparation to bridge the gap. Part of eight major preparation categories.

large_expurgatives.name: Large Expurgatives (The Terrific Trio)
large_expurgatives.definition: A cluster of three high-energy exercises (Abandonment, Vesuvius, Exorcism) used to purge tension, stimulate large emotional responses, and build foundations for volatile or emotionally extreme characters. Designed to produce large expressive emotions.
large_expurgatives.details: Each exercise starts arbitrarily — the actor jumps in and starts, hoping it will accomplish the desired state. For subtler emotional levels, large expurgatives are not the right choice — they are specifically for extreme emotional states. Additional large-expurgative preparations exist beyond the Terrific Trio. Part of the eight major categories of preparation.

sources.summary: Large Expurgatives: Abandonment, Vesuvius, Exorcism. For extreme emotional states. Jump in arbitrarily. Not for subtler emotional levels.

relationship_preparations.name: Relationship Preparations
relationship_preparations.definition: A category of craft preparations specifically designed to build, deepen, or modify the actor's real relationship with their scene partner so that it approaches the relationship demanded by the material.
relationship_preparations.details: Nonverbal communication techniques remove the obligation to verbalize, transferring energy to direct emotional contact. Key techniques: Nonverbal Communication, Telepathy, Looking for Things in the Other Actor You've Never Noticed Before, Being Workout. Part of the eight major categories of preparation.

sources.summary: Relationship preparations: build/deepen/modify relationship with scene partner toward material requirements. Nonverbal techniques central.

interim_preparation.name: Interim Preparation
interim_preparation.definition: A craft preparation that bridges the gap between the actor's current emotional state and the emotional state required by the first scene obligation when that gap is too large to bridge directly. The priming emotion that leads to what the character is experiencing.
interim_preparation.details: In a sense, all craft preparations are interim preparations since they all get the actor ready for the first emotional responsibility of the material. The specific distinction is 'stretch and difference': when the obligation is so far from the actor's current state that a stepping-stone preparation is needed. Part of the eight major categories of preparation.

sources.summary: Interim preparation: bridges gap when current state is too far from required state. Priming emotion as stepping stone.

character_inhabiting_actor.name: Character Inhabiting the Actor
character_inhabiting_actor.definition: The principle that the actor does not become the character; rather, the character becomes the actor. The character inhabits the actor's authentic organic life rather than the actor imposing a concept of the character from outside.
character_inhabiting_actor.details: This is a fundamental reversal of the common acting trap of 'becoming' a character by imposing external behavior. When organic choices successfully stimulate the character's emotional life, the character's impulses flow through the actor's authentic being. The Inner Monologue becomes the character's thought life because the choices are creating the character's reality, not a concept of it.

sources.summary: The actor does not become the character; character becomes the actor. Character inhabits actor's authentic organic life.

curiosity.name: Curiosity
curiosity.definition: Active questioning and wondering about people, objects, and phenomena in the world — the foundational component of consciousness and a skill that can be deliberately cultivated. The starting point for expanding consciousness.
curiosity.details: When curiosity is suppressed (e.g., by school systems ridiculing questions), interest withers. It can be re-awakened by deliberately asking questions about things that don't yet interest you — through repetition, genuine curiosity develops. It forms the base of a 'pyramid of involvement': curiosity → interest → knowledge → expanded consciousness.

sources.summary: Curiosity is the starting point for expanding consciousness. Re-awakened by deliberate questioning. Forms the base of a pyramid of involvement.

sensitivity.name: Sensitivity
sensitivity.definition: The capacity to perceive, be affected by, and respond to stimuli — including the feelings of other people — with emotional openness and expressiveness. A component of consciousness and a foundational quality of the actor's instrument.
sensitivity.details: Includes: capability of perceiving with senses, responsiveness to small changes, responsiveness to others' feelings, expressiveness of small changes in feeling, and being easily hurt or irritated. For the actor, sensitivity is developed through Sensitizing exercises (isolating each sense) and the Personal-Point-of-View technique.

sources.summary: Sensitivity: capacity to be affected by and respond to stimuli. Developed through sensitizing exercises and Personal Point of View.

perception.name: Perception
perception.definition: The ability to observe deeply and deduce meaning from what one sees — going beyond surface appearances to understand underlying truths about people and situations. A component of consciousness developed through curiosity and interest.
perception.details: Perception grows from curiosity and interest. Morris uses 'Deductive Observation' as the primary technique for developing it. Whenever something meaningful is at stake, a person automatically becomes perceptive — the actor should simulate that condition. Asking 'why' is central to building perception.

sources.summary: Perception: going beyond surface to understand underlying truths. Deductive Observation is primary technique. Asking 'why' is central.

interest.name: Interest
interest.definition: Active engagement with and caring about a wide range of people, objects, ideas, and phenomena beyond those that directly affect oneself. A component of consciousness; the enemy of interest is self-involvement.
interest.details: Interest grows from curiosity and feeds knowledge. Listening deeply is a primary tool for elevating interest. The pyramid: awareness → curiosity → interest → knowledge. Interest in things that don't directly relate to you is particularly valuable because it breaks the cycle of self-absorption.

sources.summary: Interest grows from curiosity, feeds knowledge. Interest beyond self breaks cycle of self-absorption.

knowledge.name: Knowledge
knowledge.definition: Being informed about a wide range of subjects — history, science, culture, current events, nature — as a component of expanded consciousness that enriches the actor's inner resources and humanity.
knowledge.details: Knowledge is part of the consciousness pyramid: awareness → curiosity → interest → knowledge. It can be acquired through reading, listening deeply, observing nature and people, questioning everything, travel, taking classes, and seeking out documentaries. Knowledge expands the actor's emotional and intellectual range.

sources.summary: Knowledge: part of the consciousness pyramid. Acquired through reading, deep listening, observation, travel. Expands emotional/intellectual range.

affinity_for_life.name: Affinity for Life / Concern
affinity_for_life.definition: A growing sense of care, empathy, and moral engagement with all living things — people, animals, nature — that emerges as consciousness expands and deepens the actor's humanity. The outermost ring of consciousness.
affinity_for_life.details: As sensitivity and awareness grow, the actor begins to feel injustice, develop moral principles, and respond to the trials of others. This concern for life infiltrates behavior and expression, adding dimensions to the actor's personality that carry over into roles.

sources.summary: Affinity for life: growing care and empathy with all living things. The outermost ring of consciousness.

unconscious_connection.name: Conscious-Unconscious Connection
unconscious_connection.definition: The link between expanded conscious awareness and the flow of unconscious impulses into behavior — the more open the actor is to conscious stimuli, the more accessible unconscious signals become. The foundation of the Ultimate Consciousness experience.
unconscious_connection.details: Morris argues that expanding consciousness is the pathway to the unconscious. The two are not opposed — heightened consciousness creates a bridge. This connection is the foundation of the ultimate consciousness experience, where unconscious material enriches deliberate craft.

sources.summary: Expanding consciousness creates a bridge to the unconscious. The two are not opposed — heightened consciousness enables unconscious access.

ultimate_consciousness_choices.name: Ultimate-Consciousness Choices
ultimate_consciousness_choices.definition: Specific acting choices known — through personal experience — to have the power to access unconscious responses: deep fears, profound losses, primal experiences, or childhood memories. Catalogued in the actor's journal for reliable access in future work.
ultimate_consciousness_choices.details: The actor keeps a journal listing all strong choices, underlining the ones that reliably access unconscious responses. Sources include: deep fears (heights, water, darkness), dream material, nightmares, affective memories of emotionally charged experiences. These choices form the toolkit for intentionally reaching Ultimate Consciousness.

sources.summary: Choices known to access unconscious responses: deep fears, dream material, profound losses. Catalogued in actor's journal.

being_workout.name: Being Workout
being_workout.definition: A two-person exercise in which both actors sit or stand face to face and communicate verbally what is going on for them in the moment — acknowledging all obstacles, distractions, and feelings — as a way of eliminating self-consciousness and reaching a genuine being state.
being_workout.details: The actors acknowledge everything that prevents comfortable relating: tension, self-consciousness, fear, concern with being good, attraction or repulsion to the other actor. Expressing these aloud reduces their power. The exercise continues until both actors feel genuinely comfortable and related. A key ensemble preparation exercise.

sources.summary: Both actors face each other and communicate what is happening in the moment — acknowledging obstacles aloud reduces them.

wonder_perceive_observe.name: Wonder / Perceive / Observe
wonder_perceive_observe.definition: An exercise in which the actor engages with another person or environment by taking the stance of wonder — looking at them with the fresh eyes of someone who has never seen them before — then perceiving (deducing meaning from what they see) and observing (noting specific details). A primary technique for selfless involvement and consciousness expansion.
wonder_perceive_observe.details: Wonder breaks the automaticity of habitual perception. Perceiving goes beyond looking to asking 'what does this mean?' Observing goes beyond perceiving to noting specific details and building knowledge of the other person. The three stages form a progression from aesthetic engagement to analytical engagement to detailed knowledge.

sources.summary: Wonder/Perceive/Observe: takes actors away from concern with themselves. Three-stage progression from fresh seeing to analytical engagement.

impulse_expression.name: Impulse Expression
impulse_expression.definition: The free, unfiltered external manifestation of organic impulses — what the actor actually does with what they genuinely feel. The expressive half of the BEING equation: BEING generates impulses; impulse expression is their output into behavior, voice, and body.
impulse_expression.details: Impulse expression requires both the impressive (receiving) instrument and the expressive (outputting) instrument to be free. Social conditioning blocks expressive freedom just as it blocks impressive freedom. Working on impulse expression includes: practice expressing small impulses immediately without filtering, building the courage to act on what you feel.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Impulse expression: free external manifestation of organic impulses. Both impressive and expressive instrument must be free.

truth_in_acting.name: Truth in Acting
truth_in_acting.definition: The state in which the actor's inner experience and outer expression are genuinely aligned — when what the actor shows corresponds to what they actually feel. The foundational goal of Morris's system and the standard by which all craft is measured.
truth_in_acting.details: Truth in acting is not naturalism or copying life — it is the genuine correspondence between inner reality and outer expression. Morris argues that truth paradoxically becomes more theatrical, not less, because it is unexpected, specific, and alive. The craft is designed to create conditions in which truth can emerge organically.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Truth in acting: genuine alignment between inner experience and outer expression. Paradoxically becomes more theatrical.

individuality.name: Individuality
individuality.definition: The quality of being distinctly and authentically oneself — the actor's unique combination of sensitivities, experiences, quirks, and responses that makes their BEING unlike anyone else's. The source of star quality and the actor's most valuable asset.
individuality.details: Morris argues that the goal of acting training is not to create uniform technical proficiency but to liberate and celebrate individuality. The actor who is truly BEING is unique, individual, and unlike any other person. Conditioning, social norms, and training can all suppress individuality. The work of the Morris system is to restore and celebrate it.
appears_in_books: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors

sources.book: Being and Doing: A Workbook for Actors
sources.summary: Individuality: the actor's unique BEING is their most valuable asset. Training should liberate, not suppress it.

theatrical_hysteria.name: Theatrical Hysteria
theatrical_hysteria.definition: A dangerous form of acting in which tension that has not been genuinely addressed is mistaken for genuine emotion — the actor confuses the physical sensations of tension (shaking, breathlessness, agitation) with authentic emotional life.
theatrical_hysteria.details: Theatrical hysteria is one of two dangerous types of tension (the other being suppressed tension appearing as false calm). It can fool the actor into thinking they are in a genuine emotional state when in fact they are only experiencing physiological stress responses. The antidote is returning to Personal Inventory to discover what is actually being felt.

sources.summary: Theatrical hysteria: tension mistaken for genuine emotion. Confuses physiological stress responses with authentic emotional life.

ego_state.name: Ego State
ego_state.definition: The actor's fundamental sense of self-worth and confidence — the psychological foundation from which all performance arises. A strong ego state enables BEING; a damaged ego state produces tension, result-consciousness, and compensational behavior.
ego_state.details: Ego state is not arrogance but authentic self-regard. A healthy ego state means the actor can take risks, make bold choices, and express freely without fear of judgment. A damaged ego state causes the actor to withhold, compensate, and monitor themselves. Ego preparations are specifically designed to address and build ego state.

sources.summary: Ego state: fundamental sense of self-worth. Healthy ego state enables BEING; damaged ego state produces tension and compensation.

impressive_obstacles.name: Impressive Obstacles
impressive_obstacles.definition: Blocks that prevent the actor from receiving and being genuinely affected by stimuli — obstacles in the instrument's 'impressive' (receiving/perceiving) capacity. The actor cannot be moved by what they cannot receive.
impressive_obstacles.details: Impressive obstacles include: intellectual barriers that analyze instead of experiencing, emotional numbness from conditioning, tension that prevents sensitivity. Contrasted with expressive obstacles (which prevent output). Shy actors tend to have strong impressive capacity but weak expressive capacity; exhibitionists tend to be the reverse.

sources.summary: Impressive obstacles: blocks in receiving/perceiving capacity. Contrasted with expressive obstacles. Shy actors often have strong impressive, weak expressive.

expressive_obstacles.name: Expressive Obstacles
expressive_obstacles.definition: Blocks that prevent the actor from outputting, sharing, and expressing what they genuinely feel — obstacles in the instrument's expressive capacity. The actor may feel deeply but cannot let it out.
expressive_obstacles.details: Expressive obstacles include: fear of exposure, social conditioning against showing emotion, vocal restrictions, physical blocks, and religious/cultural prohibitions. Contrasted with impressive obstacles (which prevent receiving). Addressing expressive obstacles requires both instrumental preparation and courage to express in public.

sources.summary: Expressive obstacles: blocks in outputting capacity. Fear of exposure, social conditioning against showing emotion, vocal restrictions.

presentational_acting.name: Presentational Acting
presentational_acting.definition: Acting in which the actor performs for the audience by showing emotions and behaviors rather than experiencing them — the technical, external, 'indicating' approach that Morris's system is designed to overcome.
presentational_acting.details: Presentational acting is the result of imposition, tension, and fear of exposure working together. The actor substitutes a performance of emotion for genuine emotion. It is recognizable to audiences as acting rather than living. Even technically skillful presentational acting lacks the unpredictability and vitality of genuine organic experience.

sources.summary: Presentational acting: showing emotions rather than experiencing them. Recognizable as acting, not living. Lacks organic unpredictability.

imaging_as_choice_approach.name: Imaging as Choice Approach
imaging_as_choice_approach.description: Using the imaging process (creating a vivid, sensory-rich mental visualization of a person, place, event, or experience) as the method by which the actor makes a chosen object real, thereby stimulating authentic emotional life to fulfill a scene obligation.
imaging_as_choice_approach.instrumental_use: Not primarily instrumental, but imaging exercises also build sensory accessibility and imagination as instrumental benefits.
imaging_as_choice_approach.craft_use: Actor identifies an obligation, selects a choice from personal life, then creates that choice through imaging with full sensory engagement. As the image becomes real, it stimulates the desired emotional response organically.
imaging_as_choice_approach.when_to_use: When the obligation calls for an emotionally rich parallel that can be most efficiently created through visualization. Best when extended, immersive sensory engagement with the choice is needed.

sources.summary: Imaging is Choice Approach #26 in Morris's system.
Imaging is the 5th MEGAPPROACH — more powerful than most other choice approaches.

imaging_with_sense_memory_technique.name: Imaging with Sense Memory
imaging_with_sense_memory_technique.description: A combined technique in which the actor begins with a mental image of a place, person, or event and enriches it by asking systematic sensorial questions directed at each sense organ, requiring the sense to respond specifically in its own domain.
imaging_with_sense_memory_technique.instrumental_use: Creates deeply real states — relaxation, environments of safety or beauty — that prepare the actor to access authentic feelings.
imaging_with_sense_memory_technique.craft_use: Creates the most dimensionally real version of a choice; especially important for Affective Memory work. Used to fulfill time-and-place, relationship, and emotional obligations.
imaging_with_sense_memory_technique.when_to_use: When maximum tangibility and dimensional reality of a choice are needed. Especially for Affective Memory, complex emotional obligations, or when establishing an entire environment as a starting condition.

sources.summary: Process: sketch image visually, ask sensorial questions responded to in specific sense organs, support images with deepening sensorial exploration.
Distinguished from pure Sense Memory: in imaging+SM combination, while one sense is emphasized, all five respond simultaneously.

imaging_sensations_technique.name: Imaging Sensations
imaging_sensations_technique.description: An alternate imaging approach where the actor allows all five senses to function simultaneously as equal imagers, each with its own processing, shifting emphasis organically from sense to sense without structured question-and-response.
imaging_sensations_technique.instrumental_use: Trains the actor to give equal weight to all senses — overcoming culturally conditioned visual dominance — deepening overall sensory accessibility.
imaging_sensations_technique.craft_use: Used when the question-response format would intrude on image flow: Free-Association Imaging, sleep-wake state work, unconscious programming sessions.
imaging_sensations_technique.when_to_use: When a smooth, uninterrupted imaging flow is needed — for unconscious programming, free association, and any imaging where analytical interruption would break the state.

sources.summary: Senses function simultaneously without questions; each sense becomes its own imager.
Used when sensorial questioning would intrude — sleep-wake work, free association, unconscious programming.

involuntary_imaging_technique.name: Involuntary Imaging (as Acting Technique)
involuntary_imaging_technique.description: The practice of becoming conscious of spontaneous images that arise during work on a choice or obligation, then deliberately re-creating those involuntary images to access the unconscious emotional material they represent.
involuntary_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Raises self-awareness; helps the actor learn how his instrument processes experience; reveals personal emotional patterns and potential choices.
involuntary_imaging_technique.craft_use: After identifying involuntary images generated during rehearsal, the actor re-creates them as a choice approach — producing richer, more dimensional emotional life than the original conscious choice, because the images come from the unconscious's translation of the obligation.
involuntary_imaging_technique.when_to_use: During rehearsal, when the actor wants to go beyond initial conscious choices and access deeper unconsciously mediated emotional material; also useful for repeated performances.

sources.summary: Involuntary Imaging used as an acting technique: become aware of the images, re-create them.
Class exercise: re-creating involuntary images produced more impacting and dimensional emotional life than original choices.
Theory: involuntary images are the unconscious's translation of the conscious choice, more deeply connected to the obligation.

symbolic_imaging_technique.name: Symbolic Imaging (as Acting Technique)
symbolic_imaging_technique.description: Collecting personal symbolic images (especially from dreams and sleep-wake states), recording them in a journal, and deliberately re-creating them to use as emotional triggers or as choice approaches for specific scene obligations.
symbolic_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Deepens connection with the unconscious through regular engagement with its symbol-language.
symbolic_imaging_technique.craft_use: A symbolic image associated with a particular person, place, or emotional state can be re-created to instantly trigger that state — a powerful shortcut to specific feeling tones.
symbolic_imaging_technique.when_to_use: When a shortcut to a deep emotional state is desired; when the actor has identified reliable symbolic triggers from personal unconscious; when working to program the unconscious for a character.

sources.summary: Symbolic images can be collected, re-created, and used as emotional triggers — a powerful shortcut.
Keep a journal of symbolic images; cross-reference with dreams; use as choices.

fragmented_imaging_technique.name: Fragmented Imaging
fragmented_imaging_technique.description: A deliberately initiated, thematic but non-sequential stream of images that can rapidly address any obligation or instrumental issue — faster and less elaborate than Story Imaging, suited for speedy emotional transitions and on-the-spot work.
fragmented_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Used to rapidly elevate vulnerability, address ego, change emotional state, or shift from depression; especially useful on-the-spot before performance.
fragmented_imaging_technique.craft_use: Used as a choice approach when speedy transitions are needed, when little time is available for a more elaborate approach, and when addressing all seven obligations quickly. Analogous in speed to Sensory Suggestion relative to Sense Memory.
fragmented_imaging_technique.when_to_use: When material calls for speedy emotional transitions; when time is limited; when the actor needs a fast on-the-spot instrumental adjustment. Less appropriate when deep, sustained emotional work is needed.

sources.summary: Fragmented Imaging is to Imaging as Sensory Suggestion is to Sense Memory — faster, less elaborate.
Can address all seven obligations; involves creative direction and selective emphasis unlike Free-Association Imaging.

additions.note: Fragmented Imaging is also the primary technique for Emotional Transitions within a scene: when the emotional demand shifts, the actor rapidly generates contrasting sensory fragments that produce the new emotional state. The transition is wordless and immediate – pure sensory flashes. Also central to building character relationships (Death of a Salesman / Biff): actor uses Fragmented Imaging with Imaging Sensations across a series of selectively emphasized memory episodes spanning childhood to adulthood.

free_association_imaging_technique.name: Free-Association Imaging
free_association_imaging_technique.description: A deliberate but direction-free imaging process in which the actor sits or lies down, clears the mind, and allows images to arise without any steering or leadership — functioning as an involvement preparation and self-discovery tool.
free_association_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Eliminates self-consciousness and performance anxiety by creating selfless involvement; elevates affectability and emotional energy; yields insights into the actor's psychology and origins of obstacles.
free_association_imaging_technique.craft_use: During rehearsal, free-associating can liberate unconscious images related to the character's life, opening doors to emotional material that supplements the chosen approach. Also a preparation before addressing the first choice of a scene.
free_association_imaging_technique.when_to_use: As a daily practice for self-discovery and imagination elevation; before rehearsal or performance as an involvement preparation; during the rehearsal period while working on a specific role.

sources.summary: Free-Association Imaging is hands-off; initiated by design but then fully released without steering.
Six rewards: facile with imaging; involvement preparation; elevates affectability; self-understanding; new choices; unconscious access.

story_imaging_technique.name: Story Imaging
story_imaging_technique.description: Imaging that follows a coherent narrative — a story with beginning, middle, and end — created either with Sense Memory or Imaging Sensations, used to create complete choice experiences or stimulate specific emotional states.
story_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Used to stimulate ego, shift depression, elevate emotional state, create inspiring self-images, or prepare instrumentally when a sustained narrative is more effective than fragments.
story_imaging_technique.craft_use: Creates the character's complete background (birth to first scene); creates environments and relationships; addresses any obligation that requires a sustained, logically coherent scenario. Can also supply extra-textual life between scenes.
story_imaging_technique.when_to_use: When sustained narrative is needed; when filling in character backstory; when the actor wants to engage the child-like storytelling imagination; when the obligation requires a complete scenario rather than fragments.

sources.summary: Story Imaging has continuity — beginning, middle, end; done with either Sense Memory structure or Imaging Sensations.
Can be episodic; used to elevate emotional state, stimulate ego, create backgrounds; also works instrumentally.

guided_imaging_technique.name: Guided Imaging
guided_imaging_technique.description: A two-person imaging process in which one actor facilitates the other through an experience by asking sensorial questions and making suggestions, allowing the facilitatee to explore a choice without the bias of anticipation.
guided_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Creates a creative dependency relationship between the actors that deepens their bond and can be carried into the scene.
guided_imaging_technique.craft_use: Especially productive for scenes where characters have shared history; used to explore choices without premeditation; helps actors learn the imaging process when they can focus only on responding, not creating.
guided_imaging_technique.when_to_use: After some rehearsal time together (so each knows what affects the other); when the obligation involves intimate shared history; when the actor keeps anticipating and guiding his own choice exploration.

sources.summary: Guided Imaging: one leads, one responds with silent sensory images; facilitator must know all elements of the experience.
Best for scenes with shared character history; begin with surrounding realities, then reach impelling core.

shared_imaging_technique.name: Shared Imaging
shared_imaging_technique.description: A process in which one actor verbally shares images of a personal experience while the partner specifically images the same events — building mutual history, understanding, and relational depth that transfers directly to performance.
shared_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Deepens the actors' relationship and creates a felt sense of shared experience — reducing the need for representational relationship-acting.
shared_imaging_technique.craft_use: Used scene-by-scene through a play to build complete shared history including extra-textual life; can be used in hypnagogic state to deepen unconscious bonding between actors.
shared_imaging_technique.when_to_use: When building a relationship between scene partners; when the characters have a long shared history not depicted in the script; when the actors need to experience the texture of each other's inner lives.

sources.summary: Shared Imaging: one shares images verbally; other images the same events specifically, not parallels.
Can be done scene-by-scene through a play; hypnagogic-state shared imaging may enter dreams.

imaging_physical_relaxation_technique.name: Imaging Physical Relaxation
imaging_physical_relaxation_technique.description: Visualization of the actor's entire body as physically relaxed, combined with tactile Imaging Sensations throughout the body, to promote actual relaxation and a long-term shift in physical habits.
imaging_physical_relaxation_technique.instrumental_use: Directly eliminates tension — the actor's primary obstacle. Can also build new physical habits over time by repeatedly imaging relaxed behavior in stressful situations.
imaging_physical_relaxation_technique.when_to_use: At the start of any work session when tension is present; as a daily practice for long-term physical change; before auditions or performances.

sources.summary: Image yourself relaxed through the whole body; use tactile Imaging Sensations; also image yourself relaxed in tense situations to build new patterns.

fear_elimination_imaging_technique.name: Fear Elimination through Imaging
fear_elimination_imaging_technique.description: A two-step imaging process for permanently eliminating acting fears: (1) fully re-experience the fear's origin through sensory imaging; (2) systematically change the image to an empowered, successful outcome — reprogramming the unconscious.
fear_elimination_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Permanently addresses lifelong fears — not a Band-Aid but a deep reprogramming. Works because the unconscious learned the fear through images and can unlearn it the same way.
fear_elimination_imaging_technique.craft_use: N/A — purely instrumental, but the result frees the actor to use the craft organically.
fear_elimination_imaging_technique.when_to_use: As a daily practice when addressing a persistent fear; not for on-the-spot performance use (for that, use the benevolent-audience Fragmented Imaging variant).

sources.summary: Two-step: re-experience fear's origin sensorially, then change the image to empowered outcome.
On-the-spot: acknowledge fear, then Fragmented Image audience as supportive and accepting.

relationship_imaging_technique.name: Relationship Imaging
relationship_imaging_technique.description: A silent imaging technique in which the actor, while relating to the real other actor, simultaneously images modifications in the other's behavior — creating new emotional relationship realities from available stimuli without the partner's knowledge or coordination.
relationship_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Deepens the actor's involvement with and interest in the scene partner; promotes authentic moment-to-moment impulses.
relationship_imaging_technique.craft_use: Creates the specific relationship dynamic required by the material — endowing the partner with qualities, feelings, and behaviors the character would have. Can be started from the first rehearsal.
relationship_imaging_technique.when_to_use: When meeting an actor for the first time; when a relationship needs to be quickly established; when the actor needs to create specific relationship dynamics without relying on the partner's behavior alone.

sources.summary: Silent imaging of modifications in the other actor's real behavior; both respond authentically; creates the beginning of a real relationship.

parallel_imaging_technique.name: Parallel Imaging
parallel_imaging_technique.description: An individual imaging practice in which the actor creates images of himself living the character's life — using the play's actual circumstances as the scenario and weaving in personal parallels — building the unconscious background of the character through sustained immersive imaging.
parallel_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: N/A — purely a craft/unconscious programming technique.
parallel_imaging_technique.craft_use: Builds the character's complete reality — experiences, relationships, history — in the actor's unconscious through sustained repeated imaging. Personal parallels ensure the images are authentic rather than intellectual.
parallel_imaging_technique.when_to_use: During the rehearsal period; done individually at home or in private; repeated across multiple sessions to allow cumulative unconscious absorption. Especially important for plays with complex character backgrounds.

sources.summary: Image oneself having the character's life using the play's circumstances; include personal parallels; blend reality with hypothesis; builds unconscious character background.

i_am_five_years_old_imaging_technique.name: I'm Five Years Old (Imaging Variant)
i_am_five_years_old_imaging_technique.description: An on-the-spot instrumental technique using the prompt 'I'm five years old and I...' to generate spontaneous childhood images that eliminate spot obstacles, elevate affectability, and pique unconscious involvement simultaneously.
i_am_five_years_old_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Eliminates on-the-spot instrumental obstacles (tension, self-consciousness, self-involvement, fear of failure) by creating selfless involvement with childhood images that stretch the emotional state in varied directions and dimensionalize the BEING state.
i_am_five_years_old_imaging_technique.craft_use: N/A — purely instrumental, but the heightened BEING state enables craft work.
i_am_five_years_old_imaging_technique.when_to_use: When on-the-spot obstacles prevent the actor from reaching a BEING state; when standard relaxation techniques have not fully freed the instrument; before beginning craft preparations.

sources.summary: I'm Five Years Old imaging: respond to prompt with images not words; progress through ages; triple benefit.
Complete when involved, affected, and off the spot.

endowment_via_imaging.name: Endowment via Imaging
endowment_via_imaging.description: Using imaging to transform the actual other actor into the character's specific person — simultaneously imaging the chosen person (the choice) while looking at and responding to the real actor, blending the two until they merge into one reality.
endowment_via_imaging.craft_use: Used in 'night, Mother: each actress talks to the other while imaging her own real-life mother/daughter in the other's place. The actress images the choice's expressions, voice, specific mannerisms, and then blends those images with the actual responses received. Technique works to make the hypothetical relationship acceptable as truth.
endowment_via_imaging.when_to_use: When the relationship obligation requires specific personal parallels; when the material calls for specific emotional dynamics (mother-daughter, brothers, romantic partners) that the actor has real-life parallels for.

sources.summary: Night Mother: each actress images her real-life equivalent while talking to the other actress — blend images with real responses.
Technique progressively absorbs hypothetical into real; cumulative unconscious absorption.

time_and_place_imaging.name: Time and Place Imaging
time_and_place_imaging.description: Using imaging — either with Sense Memory or with Imaging Sensations — to create the environment of the material as a genuine sensory reality, so that the actor is truly affected by the place and time as the character would be.
time_and_place_imaging.craft_use: Select a personal place with the same emotional and historical meaning as the play's environment. Create it with all five senses simultaneously; put yourself in it. Choose Imaging with Sense Memory if the place is remote or needs maximum tangibility; choose Imaging Sensations if the place is vivid and the flow should be uninterrupted.
time_and_place_imaging.when_to_use: First in the craft process, or whenever the place is the dominant factor in the material. Re-created at each rehearsal and in each performance.

sources.summary: Select a personal place with the same meaning as the play's; create it with both imaging approaches depending on vividness.
Time and Place stimulates unconscious emotional points of view; actor's relationship to the place affects behavior on multiple levels.

creating_music_as_choice.name: Creating a Piece of Music (Emotional Obligation)
creating_music_as_choice.description: The actor selects a specific piece of music that personally evokes the emotions demanded by the scene, then uses Imaging (typically Imaging Sensations) to hear and feel that music internally while performing.
creating_music_as_choice.instrumental_use: Can be used instrumentally to access and sustain an emotional state for personal healing or emotional liberation exercises.
creating_music_as_choice.craft_use: Used as a choice for the emotional obligation. The actor images the music playing internally throughout the scene, allowing the music's emotional impact to drive organic impulses. Example: actor playing a union organizer images the 'Big Country' theme music to feel heroic grandeur appropriate to the role.
creating_music_as_choice.when_to_use: When the scene demands a sustained emotional quality that the actor has difficulty generating through purely personal memory. Music can be pre-loaded in preparation and then internally maintained during performance.

creating_experience_as_choice.name: Creating an Experience (Emotional Obligation)
creating_experience_as_choice.description: The actor selects a personal past experience that produced the needed emotions and uses Imaging to re-enter that experience sensorially, reliving its emotional impact during the scene.
creating_experience_as_choice.craft_use: Used for the emotional obligation. The actor identifies a past event with the right emotional quality and images it in preparation or during the scene. Example: actress in 'Hearts' who must experience terror of falling uses her real memory of a motorcycle accident, imaging the specific sensory details to re-experience the terror.
creating_experience_as_choice.when_to_use: When the actor has a personal experience with the exact emotional quality needed. Works especially well when the experience is viscerally remembered with strong sensory detail.

creating_person_as_choice.name: Creating a Person (Emotional Obligation)
creating_person_as_choice.description: The actor selects a real or imagined person who evokes the needed emotions and uses Imaging to conjure that person sensorially – typically in two parts: (1) imaging the person to elicit feelings toward them, then (2) Fantasy Story Imaging of the person in a scene-relevant situation.
creating_person_as_choice.craft_use: Two-part technique for the emotional obligation. Part 1: Fragmented Imaging of the person (sensory details of their appearance, sounds, smells, mannerisms) to kindle love, connection, or whatever feeling is needed. Part 2: Fantasy Story Imaging of that person experiencing the scene's parallel situation, e.g., imaging them receiving a letter, to produce the actor's appropriate emotional response. Example: 'Dear Jane' letter scene – actor images Tim (friend) via Fragmented Imaging for love, then Fantasy Story Imaging of Jane reading the letter.
creating_person_as_choice.when_to_use: When the scene involves powerful feelings toward a specific person (love, grief, longing) and the actor can use a real person from their life as a parallel choice.

character_background_story_imaging.name: Character Background Story Imaging
character_background_story_imaging.description: Using Story Imaging (and optionally Fantasy Story Imaging) to construct a series of imagined biographical episodes that establish the character's psychological history prior to the play's action.
character_background_story_imaging.instrumental_use: Can be done as an instrumental exercise when the actor needs to develop empathy for a character whose psychology is foreign to them.
character_background_story_imaging.craft_use: Central technique for the character obligation. The actor creates multiple episodes in chronological order – formative events, relationships, humiliations, joys – that explain why the character is who they are. Each episode is a full Story Imaging experience with all senses engaged. The actor mixes real parallels from their own life with imagined events (Believability), so that the constructed history integrates with unconscious memory.
character_background_story_imaging.when_to_use: During the rehearsal period before staging begins. Especially important for psychologically complex or morally extreme characters (villains, killers, severely traumatized individuals) where the actor's own experience is insufficient.

imaging_craft_technique_endowments.name: Imaging as Craft Technique with Endowments
imaging_craft_technique_endowments.description: Using Fragmented Imaging + Imaging Sensations to sculpt a chosen person's features, voice, smell, texture, and essence onto a scene partner, creating a conglomerate endowment.
imaging_craft_technique_endowments.craft_use: The actor first exhausts Available Stimulus (real similarities between partner and choice), then begins imaging the choice's unique qualities in relation to the partner: hair, skin, eye fleck, nostril shape, breathing sounds, skin texture against cheek, tears in corners of eyes, etc. All five senses work simultaneously. The partner and choice gradually merge into a conglomerate. The actor responds to the partner's real behavior as if it were the choice person's behavior.
imaging_craft_technique_endowments.when_to_use: In rehearsal when the scene requires deep relationship feelings that Available Stimulus alone cannot produce. Most effective when the actor has had an intimate relationship with the chosen person.

imaging_craft_technique_affective_memory.name: Imaging as Craft Technique with Affective Memory
imaging_craft_technique_affective_memory.description: Using Story Imaging + a combination of Sense Memory (sensory questions) and Imaging Sensations to chronologically re-create a traumatic or impactful past event, triggering genuine unconscious reliving of the original emotions.
imaging_craft_technique_affective_memory.instrumental_use: Not primarily instrumental; however, the re-experience can have therapeutic value.
imaging_craft_technique_affective_memory.craft_use: Process: Start with Sense Memory sensory questions in the present tense to build the environment (What do I see? What do I smell? Who else is there?). As specificity deepens and all senses activate simultaneously, transition into Imaging Sensations (the questions cease and images flow automatically). Address all component parts chronologically up to and through the emotional peak. Repeated practice compresses the duration from hours to minutes. The experience then unfolds alongside the written scene in performance.
imaging_craft_technique_affective_memory.when_to_use: When the scene demands an extreme emotional state that the actor has actually experienced in their life. The experience must be specific and well-remembered enough to be reconstructed sensorially.

imaging_subpersonalities_technique.name: Imaging to Access Subpersonalities (Four-Part Process)
imaging_subpersonalities_technique.description: A four-step imaging technique for eliciting specific subpersonalities: (1) image the subpart sensorially in a chosen physical space; (2) step into its energy field and inhabit it; (3) use mirror work to verify and deepen embodiment; (4) image through the subpart's perspective toward character obligations.
imaging_subpersonalities_technique.instrumental_use: Used instrumentally to develop an aware ego: the actor learns to recognize, access, and hold the various selves in balance. Helps actors become familiar with primary, opposite, and disowned selves as a prerequisite to using subpersonalities as an acting tool.
imaging_subpersonalities_technique.craft_use: Used as the choice approach for accessing the subpersonality that matches the character's dominant energy. The actor images the subpart, inhabits it, and then allows it to carry the performance. Can be done in 8-10 minutes. Any imaging technique (Fragmented, Story, Sensations) can be used within the four-step structure.
imaging_subpersonalities_technique.when_to_use: In preparation before a rehearsal or performance, or during the rehearsal period to establish which subpersonality best serves the material. Prerequisite: familiarity with the subpersonality through prior voice dialogue or other approach techniques.

voice_dialogue_technique.name: Voice Dialogue (Subpersonality Approach Technique)
voice_dialogue_technique.description: A two-person facilitation process in which a facilitator speaks directly with the subject's various subpersonalities, each of which physically moves to a different area of the room and speaks as a distinct entity.
voice_dialogue_technique.instrumental_use: Primary instrumental use: helps the actor identify primary and disowned selves, develop an aware ego, understand how subparts function in their life. Prerequisite for all other subpersonality work. Can visibly transform the subject's physical appearance, posture, voice, and attitude while in a specific self. Facilitator guidelines: maintain benevolent demeanor, never disagree, notice vocal changes and physical cues, start with 'What's going on in your life?' to establish a psychic map of the territory.
voice_dialogue_technique.craft_use: After sufficient experience with instrumental voice dialogue, can be used to elicit the desired subpersonality before or during performance. Requires a skilled facilitator; cannot be done solo.
voice_dialogue_technique.when_to_use: Early in the actor's subpersonality work, before attempting other techniques. Can continue throughout the actor's life as a tool for self-awareness and accessing new or disowned selves.

role_playing_subpersonality.name: Role-Playing (Subpersonality Access Technique)
role_playing_subpersonality.description: Solo technique: the actor achieves an aware-ego state, asks a specific subpart to come forth, moves to a physical space that feels like its location, and encourages it to speak in an uninterrupted monologue.
role_playing_subpersonality.instrumental_use: Allows solo access to subpersonalities without a facilitator. The actor must have prior experience with voice dialogue to know who the selves are. The monologue runs without the actor trying to communicate back to the subpart—conversation would be confusing. If the subpart meanders, the actor returns to an aware-ego state and redirects.
role_playing_subpersonality.craft_use: The actor elicits the subpersonality and then moves directly into the scene or monologue while permeated with that energy. Example: accessing the Inner Critic before performing a monologue adds a judgmental, critical energy to the performance.
role_playing_subpersonality.when_to_use: When a facilitator is not available. Requires considerable prior experience with voice dialogue.

primal_moan_technique.name: Primal Moan (Unconscious Access Technique)
primal_moan_technique.description: Fetal-position primal exercise: deep moaning from the lower abdomen, building in volume and depth, crashing through emotional blocks and soliciting unconscious impulses and images.
primal_moan_technique.instrumental_use: Primary use: break through emotional blocks; access unconscious material; condition the instrument to communicate with the unconscious. Also useful for actors who are cut off or extremely blocked emotionally before rehearsal.
primal_moan_technique.craft_use: When used as preparation for a scene: prime unconscious participation, then transition to working for a choice. The unconscious impulses and images solicited carry over into the scene work.
primal_moan_technique.when_to_use: When the actor is emotionally blocked or disconnected from unconscious impulses. Also as a regular daily practice to build a bridge to the unconscious over time.

primitive_abandonment_technique.name: Primitive Abandonment Exercise
primitive_abandonment_technique.description: Standing large-movement exercise with animal-like sounds, building in rhythmical intensity toward a prehistoric, primal energy that liberates blocked emotions and piques unconscious impulses and disowned subpersonalities.
primitive_abandonment_technique.instrumental_use: Liberates angry, hostile impulses in conflict-phobic actors; frees sexual energy; accesses satanic/demonic subpersonalities; produces primal images including unfamiliar faces (possibly unconscious in origin). Often used as a cluster with Primal Moan and other primitive exercises.
primitive_abandonment_technique.craft_use: When used as preparation: move directly from the exercise into a choice that addresses the material. The unconscious priming carries into the scene. Transition: quiet assimilation/meditative state → choice work.
primitive_abandonment_technique.when_to_use: When the actor needs to access primal, raw, or extremely suppressed emotional energies. Also as a regular practice to condition the unconscious bridge.

disconnected_impulsivity_technique.name: Disconnected Impulsivity
disconnected_impulsivity_technique.description: Encouraging a rapid stream of moment-to-moment impulses—internal thoughts, environmental responses, and unrelated observations—run silently under the lines of a scene to eliminate premeditation and restore organic responsiveness.
disconnected_impulsivity_technique.instrumental_use: Primary: antidote to conceptual acting and preplanning. Secondary: piquing unconscious responses. After completing the exercise, do Personal Inventory to assess the emotional state. Meaningful items that arose can be used as starting points for a second, directed run (Adding Meaningful Suggestions variant).
disconnected_impulsivity_technique.craft_use: Used as a preparation before a scene: having identified the desired emotional state, the actor suggests a flow of images related to a particular time or issue, expecting them to hook into the unconscious and support the scene's emotional obligations.
disconnected_impulsivity_technique.when_to_use: When an actor is intellectually controlling their work, or when they need to prime the unconscious before rehearsal or performance. Daily or weekly practice recommended.

sources.summary: Disconnected Impulsivity: last resort when other impulse-restoring techniques fail.
Eliminates premeditation; charges low-energy scenes; looks like natural environmental responsiveness from outside.

logy_exercise.name: Logy Exercise (Hypnagogic State Induction)
logy_exercise.description: The actor lies prone, exaggerates body weight to slothlike heaviness while making the sound 'logy,' deliberately approaching the sleep threshold to induce the hypnagogic state and access unconscious images.
logy_exercise.instrumental_use: Induces the hypnagogic state—the threshold between waking and sleep where the unconscious liberates images. The actor makes a sustained sound using 'logy' through most of the exercise and stops when entering the hypnagogic state. Duration: up to 30 minutes. Actors return looking as if they had awakened from impacting dreams—permissive, benevolent, ready to work. Risk: falling fully asleep. Trick: keep coming out of the hypnagogic state and re-entering it. With repetition, the actor becomes facile at 'working' the unconscious from this state.
logy_exercise.craft_use: Used as a preparation just before rehearsal: enlists unconscious cooperation, then transition directly into choice work for the material.
logy_exercise.when_to_use: As a regular preparation before rehearsal or as a dedicated daily practice to build a bridge to the unconscious. Also: every night as you go to sleep, to build facility with the hypnagogic state over time.

unconscious_suggestions_technique.name: Unconscious Suggestions (Systematic Place Imaging)
unconscious_suggestions_technique.description: Systematic imaging of specific categories of life environments (bedrooms, schoolrooms, religious spaces, playgrounds) to liberate unconscious impulses associated with formative memories.
unconscious_suggestions_technique.instrumental_use: Used as a regular exercise to deepen unconscious access. In workshops: one hour of Unconscious Suggestions immediately before a monologue produces remarkable depth and inspiration. The actor must not comment, acknowledge, or evaluate impulses—just express them. Key rule: When images feel disconnected from the environment—that's a sign the unconscious is responding. DO NOT COMMENT. Just express.
unconscious_suggestions_technique.craft_use: Can be used as a preparation to prime specific emotional states if the chosen environment is known to contain impetus relevant to the material's obligations.
unconscious_suggestions_technique.when_to_use: As part of a regular daily or weekly unconscious-access practice. Also immediately before a rehearsal or performance when the actor needs deep emotional connection.

active_imagination_technique.name: Active Imagination (Dream Work Technique)
active_imagination_technique.description: Consciously dialoguing with dream figures by placing them in a chair, writing in stream-of-consciousness, or moving expressively—as a way of receiving unconscious messages from dream content.
active_imagination_technique.instrumental_use: Primary: establish communication with unconscious content from dreams. Ask the dream figure questions; receive surprising answers from the unconscious. Determine whether the figure is subjective (represents a part of self) or objective (represents another person in life). Use in combination with Imaging: create the figure through Imaging Sensations while dialoguing, making them more tangible and increasing unconscious access.
