evangelist_rainmaker.when_to_use: Low self-esteem, need for confidence before performing, any time ego needs dramatic elevation.

sources.summary: Evangelist/Rainmaker: over-the-top passionate promotion of chosen subject in front of group. Example about acting. Lifts ego even when not organic.

pollyanna.name: Pollyanna
pollyanna.description: Actor speaks from the perspective of Pollyanna — the mythical optimist who sees everything through rose-colored glasses. Relates only to beautiful and positive things in the environment and within themselves. Arbitrary start quickly connects to real feelings.
pollyanna.instrumental_use: Addresses tendency to see the glass as half-empty or empty. Accesses child energy and magical child perspective. Provides permissive positive state — allows actor to feel good about themselves and everything in the environment. Also elevates self-esteem.
pollyanna.craft_use: Accesses joyful, optimistic emotional color — available for characters with that energy. Recommended preparation for Fantasticks-style material.
pollyanna.when_to_use: Low self-esteem, depressive tendency, loss of joy, difficulty accessing positive emotional colors.

sources.summary: Pollyanna: mythical optimist exercise, rose-colored glasses perspective. Addresses empty-glass tendency. Accesses child energy. Example given.

accepting_academy_award.name: Accepting the Academy Award
accepting_academy_award.description: Actor imagines and performs a full acceptance speech for the Academy Award — expressing genuine gratitude, acknowledging the honor of nomination and winning, in front of the group. Taps into the universal actor fantasy.
accepting_academy_award.instrumental_use: Elevates sense of self in a positive, aspirational way. Provides the ego boost needed to begin working. Taps into deep desire for professional recognition.
accepting_academy_award.craft_use: Can be adapted as a craft preparation for character ego states requiring strong public validation.
accepting_academy_award.when_to_use: As an ego preparation; whenever the actor needs to build a strong positive self-image.

sources.summary: Accepting Academy Award: full acceptance speech performance. Universal actor fantasy. Elevates sense of self. Ego boost.
Accepting the Academy Award: widely appealing ego fantasy preparation for actors.

magic_pocket.name: The Magic Pocket
magic_pocket.description: Actor imagines having something very special in their pocket — a Ferrari key, a million-dollar bond, a seven-picture studio deal, a love letter from someone desired. Uses sense memory to create the feeling of the object.
magic_pocket.instrumental_use: Elevates ego state through sensory fantasy of success and abundance. Can be done in any venue at any time.
magic_pocket.when_to_use: Any time ego state needs quick elevation; can be done discreetly in any setting.

sources.summary: Magic Pocket: imagine very special object in pocket (Ferrari key, million-dollar bond, studio contract, love letter). Sense memory supports fantasy. Ego boost.

progress_report.name: A Progress Report
progress_report.description: Sharing monologue based on truth — reviewing personal growth and change over a specific time period, from the past to the present. Done alone or with others. Can cover from childhood to present or focus on a recent period.
progress_report.instrumental_use: Provides objective evidence of actual growth and positive change, counteracting depression, stagnation feelings, and low self-esteem. Can be done on a daily basis.
progress_report.when_to_use: Depression, stagnation, feelings of not growing, low self-esteem, discouragement.

sources.summary: Progress Report: truth-based sharing monologue reviewing personal growth. Past → present. Example of actor's progress. Daily use.

self_esteem_imaging.name: Imaging (for Self-Esteem)
self_esteem_imaging.description: Using imaging with all five senses to create self-aggrandizing fantasies — Monte Carlo casino with enormous gambling chips, meeting Steven Spielberg for a starring role, being pampered in Hawaii, receiving a standing ovation. All senses must be specifically created.
self_esteem_imaging.instrumental_use: Enormous tool for elevating ego and self-esteem through the vivid sensory experience of success, abundance, and admiration.
self_esteem_imaging.craft_use: Builds general imaging facility that has many craft applications.
self_esteem_imaging.when_to_use: Low self-esteem; before performing or auditioning; any time ego needs elevation.

sources.summary: Self-Esteem Imaging: all-sense images of self-aggrandizing success scenarios. Monte Carlo, Spielberg meeting, Hawaiian veranda, standing ovation.

self_esteem_journal.name: Keeping a Self-Esteem Journal
self_esteem_journal.description: Daily diary entries recording anything that created good feelings about oneself — discoveries, compliments received, rewarding conversations, helping others, acting accomplishments. Each entry dated; all entries read at day's end; all seven days read at week's end.
self_esteem_journal.instrumental_use: Builds cumulative self-esteem through systematic documentation of positive experiences. The daily reading and weekly review reinforce positive self-perception consistently.
self_esteem_journal.craft_use: Documenting acting accomplishments specifically builds craft confidence.
self_esteem_journal.when_to_use: Ongoing daily practice for self-esteem maintenance.

sources.summary: Self-Esteem Journal: daily entries of positive experiences; read at day's end; weekly review. Part of a seven-journal actor system.

two_person_validation.name: Two-Person Validation Workouts
two_person_validation.description: Two people sit facing each other and validate each other from a real place in the framework of ordinary conversation — selectively emphasizing genuine positive feelings. Not arbitrary or imposed. Must come from authentic real feelings between the two people.
two_person_validation.instrumental_use: Builds self-esteem through genuine received validation. The authenticity requirement makes it more impactful than arbitrary compliments.
two_person_validation.craft_use: Builds genuine positive relational connection between acting partners.
two_person_validation.when_to_use: Self-esteem building; relationship building between scene partners; class community building.

sources.summary: Two-Person Validation: genuine, not arbitrary. Selectively emphasize real positive feelings. Example conversation given.

affirmations.name: Affirmations
affirmations.description: Daily mantras beginning with 'I deserve' — I deserve to be happy, successful, loved, recognized, to make money, have what I want, have a loving relationship, be healthy. Also variations: 'I am accomplished...' Repeated several times daily to feed statements into the unconscious.
affirmations.instrumental_use: With daily repetition, feeds positive beliefs into the unconscious — over time, the actor begins to genuinely believe what has been repeatedly input. Gradually elevates ego and self-esteem.
affirmations.when_to_use: Daily practice — especially effective in the morning and just before sleep. Any time self-esteem is challenged.

sources.summary: Affirmations: 'I deserve' statements as daily mantra. Repeated to feed into unconscious. Reference to Sondra Ray's 'I Deserve Love.'
Daily repetition causes unconscious to gradually accept and believe the inputs. Long-term self-esteem elevation.

re_creating_the_experience.name: Re-Creating the Experience (Repairing the Damage)
re_creating_the_experience.description: The primary damage-repair technique: (1) Recall and identify a specific damaging experience. (2) Re-create it sensorially using sense memory and imaging — all five senses simultaneously, specific to time, place, people, sounds, odors, temperature. (3) After being affected by the original, settle down. (4) Re-create the experience again, changing all damaging elements to positive, affirming, nurturing outcomes — including the final outcome. (5) Repeat many times over a period of time. Must be done on a here-and-now basis, not retrospectively.
re_creating_the_experience.instrumental_use: Aims to permanently repair self-esteem damage by replacing the unconscious's stored negative experience with a repeatedly input positive version. Jung's 'language of unconscious is images' — sensory imaging has direct unconscious impact.
re_creating_the_experience.craft_use: Also removes the damaging experience as a choice (which is acceptable — hundreds of other choices available), while the amended version creates a new, positive choice possibility.
re_creating_the_experience.when_to_use: Identified specific damaging historical events; chronic self-esteem problems traced to specific origins. Core technique of Repairing the Damage process.

sources.summary: Re-creating experience: four steps — recall, re-create sensorially, settle, re-create positively. Must repeat many times.
Sensorially create Miss Bloom scenario with positive content. Example given with Jonah (teacher humiliation). Everything in silence, all senses.

second_chance_exercise.name: The Second-Chance Exercise
second_chance_exercise.description: Through imaginary monologues, the actor revisits hurtful encounters and says to the person what they didn't say in the original. Takes their due, stands up for themselves, puts the person in their place — OR expresses unfulfilled love and positive feelings. The person is sensorially created in the chair if possible; otherwise, their responses are imagined.
second_chance_exercise.instrumental_use: Expurgates incubated unresolved feelings; relieves the nagging 'if only I had said...' regret. Repairs self-esteem by retroactively taking one's due. Also addresses unfulfilled loving expressions.
second_chance_exercise.when_to_use: Unresolved encounters with damaging people from any period of life; also for unexpressed love toward deceased or absent people (Morris's example with his late son Jeff).

sources.summary: Second-Chance Exercise: imaginary monologue saying what wasn't said. Taking due, standing up, expressing love. Example with Miss Bloom and personal grief example.
Morris's example to his late son: unexpressed love. Second chance can be hostile or loving.

verbal_approach_repair.name: The Verbal Approach (Repairing the Damage)
verbal_approach_repair.description: Third damage-repair method: relates only the modified, positive version of the experience — in conversation with another person OR alone in a sharing monologue out loud. Must be repeated a great number of times, keeping content consistent. Based on the principle: 'if you tell a lie often enough, it becomes the truth.'
verbal_approach_repair.instrumental_use: Input of positive modified experience through repeated verbalization causes the unconscious to gradually accept the new version as the original. Accessible method requiring no advanced imaging skill.
verbal_approach_repair.when_to_use: When full imaging re-creation is difficult or premature. Good starting point for repair work. Can be used alongside imaging methods.

sources.summary: Verbal Approach: tell modified version repeatedly — in conversation or sharing monologue. 'Tell a lie often enough it becomes the truth.' Unconscious accepts new input.

appealing_to_unconscious.name: Appealing to the Unconscious
appealing_to_unconscious.description: Going through the amended experience 3-4 times just before going to sleep — to stimulate a related dream and to implant the experience into the conscious/unconscious at the moment just before sleep activates the unconscious.
appealing_to_unconscious.instrumental_use: Uses the sleep-onset state to directly input modified experiences into the unconscious when it is most receptive.
appealing_to_unconscious.when_to_use: Daily practice as part of repairing the damage process. Every night during active repair work on a specific experience.

sources.summary: Appealing to Unconscious: review amended experience 3-4x just before sleep. Stimulates related dream and implants experience at sleep onset.

revisiting_childhood_environments.name: Revisiting and Re-Creating Childhood Environments, Events, Friends, and Toys
revisiting_childhood_environments.description: Using sense memory to sensorially re-create specific childhood memories: rooms, backyards, hiding places (environments); birthday parties, vacations, firsts (events); specific childhood friends (friends); specific childhood toys. All done in here-and-now, not retrospectively. Five senses simultaneously.
revisiting_childhood_environments.instrumental_use: Resurrects the inner child by reconnecting the actor sensorially with the specific emotional world of their childhood. Creates a conduit to the inner child's imaginative, believing, playful energy.
revisiting_childhood_environments.craft_use: Builds emotional memory access and sensory specificity. Also surfaces damaging experiences for repair work.
revisiting_childhood_environments.when_to_use: Inner child resurrection work; as part of regular daily actor practice; for actors who have lost imaginative spontaneity.

sources.summary: Revisiting Childhood: sense memory re-creation of environments, events, friends, toys. Here-and-now basis. All five senses.
Events: birthday parties, vacations, firsts. Friends: key childhood relationships. Toys: strong memories. Each resurfaces inner child energies.

original_being_exercise.name: Original Being Exercise
original_being_exercise.description: An exercise to access the inner child and unconscious impulses by lying down, clearing the mind, and then slowly opening the eyes to experience everything — sights, sounds, movements — as if for the very first time.
original_being_exercise.instrumental_use: Connects the actor to the preverbal, pre-conceptual state of the very young child, stimulating inner child energy and opening access to unconscious impulses. Reduces the actor's habitual assumptions about the world and reinvigorates genuine curiosity.
original_being_exercise.when_to_use: When working to access inner child qualities, unconscious impulse responses, or a state of pure wonder and discovery in the instrument.

two_persons_sharing_childhood.name: Two Persons Sharing Childhood Experiences
two_persons_sharing_childhood.description: Two people sit facing each other and share their childhood experiences in the present tense, taking emotional responsibility for being affected by what they share. Done in a conversational manner without taking turns.
two_persons_sharing_childhood.instrumental_use: Resurrects the inner child through shared present-tense vulnerability. The present-tense framing ('I'm playing with my little red car right now') makes the childhood memories emotionally immediate and stimulates genuine inner child energy.
two_persons_sharing_childhood.when_to_use: In class or with a scene partner to stimulate inner child energy and deepen availability to vulnerability.

daydreaming_and_fantasizing.name: Daydreaming and Fantasizing
daydreaming_and_fantasizing.description: A daily practice of consciously creating vivid imaginative fantasies — romantic, sexual, confrontational, or adventurous — involving all the senses. Includes both single fantasies and ongoing episodic fantasies that continue across nights.
daydreaming_and_fantasizing.instrumental_use: Exercises and expands the imagination; reconnects the actor to the child's natural capacity for vivid belief. Trains the instrument to generate rich internal imagery on demand. The unconscious treats sensorial fantasies as real experience, expanding the actor's emotional range.
daydreaming_and_fantasizing.craft_use: Develops the imaginative capacity needed for rich, sensorial choice work in scenes.
daydreaming_and_fantasizing.when_to_use: Daily as an ongoing practice; especially valuable when the actor feels imaginatively blocked or overly literal.

personal_inventory_for_vocal_dependency.name: Personal Inventory (for Vocal Dependency)
personal_inventory_for_vocal_dependency.description: The actor repeatedly asks 'How do I feel?' to connect with moment-to-moment impulses, then carries those real feelings into the lines of the material — saying them irreverently, connecting organic feelings to the words.
personal_inventory_for_vocal_dependency.instrumental_use: Breaks vocal dependency by establishing authentic emotional grounding before speaking the lines, so the voice carries real feeling rather than an imposed coloring.
personal_inventory_for_vocal_dependency.craft_use: Once organically connected, the actor can work for a choice that takes him to the emotional obligation of the material while maintaining vocal-emotional connection.
personal_inventory_for_vocal_dependency.when_to_use: When the actor recognizes he is manipulating his voice rather than allowing it to carry organic impulses; before working on a scene or monologue.

nonverbal_imaginary_monologues.name: Nonverbal Imaginary Monologues
nonverbal_imaginary_monologues.description: The actor places imaginary people with whom he has emotional agendas in empty seats and relates to each one through sounds only (no words), allowing the emotional content to be carried through the voice organically without the interference of logic or word meaning.
nonverbal_imaginary_monologues.instrumental_use: Removes the crutch of words and their emotional suggestions, forcing the actor to find organic vocal expression. Directly addresses vocal dependency by bypassing the intellectual mind-mouth connection.
nonverbal_imaginary_monologues.when_to_use: As a preparation exercise when the actor is vocally dependent; also as a warmup before working on monologue material.

one_sound_exercise.name: One-Sound Exercise
one_sound_exercise.description: The actor makes a neutral sound and holds it continuously, encouraging everything he feels from moment to moment to affect the sound. The sound will vary and take on many colors as organic impulses flow through it.
one_sound_exercise.instrumental_use: Creates direct consciousness of the connection between what the actor feels and the varied expressions of those impulses through the voice. Antidotes vocal dependency by making the voice-feeling link undeniable.
one_sound_exercise.when_to_use: To address vocal dependency; when the actor needs to become conscious of how organic feeling affects vocal expression.

irreverent_communication.name: Irreverent Communication
irreverent_communication.description: The actor uses the words of a written monologue (or gibberish) to communicate with another person or group, attempting to make herself understood by expressing her real feelings through the lines — making the connection between the verbal channel and organic feeling.
irreverent_communication.instrumental_use: Antidotes verbal dependency by using words as vehicles for genuine feeling rather than as the primary carrier of emotional content. When the actor feels strongly in relation to her listener, her voice will carry those emotions through even prescribed lines.
irreverent_communication.when_to_use: When addressing vocal dependency; as a bridge between instrumental freedom and returning to material.

being_irreverent_to_concept.name: Being Irreverent to Your Concept of the Material
being_irreverent_to_concept.description: In rehearsal, the actor says the lines while being totally irreverent to the obligations of the material, going moment to moment with his real impulses and expressing everything that comes up — free from any responsibility to fulfill his emotional concept.
being_irreverent_to_concept.instrumental_use: Frees the actor from conceptual attachment, preventing the dependency trap from taking hold. Allows organic impulses to surface.
being_irreverent_to_concept.craft_use: When the actor is working within the scene, including all impulses even if irreverent to the concept ultimately produces more colorful, organic behavior that can be shaped toward the material's obligations.
being_irreverent_to_concept.when_to_use: In rehearsal when the actor recognizes he is leading himself toward a preconceived emotional result; when addressing any acting dependency.

observe_perceive_wonder.name: Observe, Perceive, and Wonder
observe_perceive_wonder.description: The actor looks deeply into another person, observing physical details, perceiving feelings and meanings from body language and voice, and wondering about the person on any level — elevating curiosity to its highest degree. The actor does not relate anything back to himself.
observe_perceive_wonder.instrumental_use: Creates selfless involvement — getting the actor completely out of himself. Antidotes compensational behavior by shifting concentration from internal monitoring to genuine external curiosity.
observe_perceive_wonder.craft_use: A preparation technique before scenes; can be used with the actual scene partner to create genuine investment in and curiosity about the other actor before addressing scene obligations.
observe_perceive_wonder.when_to_use: As a preparation before rehearsal or performance; whenever the actor recognizes compensational or self-conscious behavior; as part of the Two-Person Being Workout.

ensemble_exercise.name: Ensemble Exercise
ensemble_exercise.description: Two actors face each other and relate using the 'You make me feel... / That makes me feel... / Now I feel...' structure, responding in each moment to how they are genuinely affected by what is said, creating a complete creative dependency on each other.
ensemble_exercise.instrumental_use: Liberates actors from self-involvement; stimulates genuine moment-to-moment responsiveness; creates ensemble cohesion through authentic mutual responsiveness.
ensemble_exercise.craft_use: Establishes the creative dependency between scene partners that is the foundation of effective scene work; an excellent preparation for rehearsal.
ensemble_exercise.when_to_use: Before rehearsal (as part of Two-Person Being Workout); whenever actors need to strengthen their ensemble connection; to address compensational behavior and self-involvement.

moment_to_moment_inclusions.name: Moment-to-Moment Inclusions
moment_to_moment_inclusions.description: The actor includes and expresses every feeling and impulse that arises in the moment — as a stream of consciousness or within a scene/monologue, audibly acknowledging distractions, discomforts, reinvestment decisions, and irreverent impulses alongside the lines.
moment_to_moment_inclusions.instrumental_use: Establishes the organic moment-to-moment flow of impulses; prevents parenthesizing of authentic experience that creates splits; keeps the actor connected to what is real.
moment_to_moment_inclusions.craft_use: When used in a scene, the included impulses — even if irreverent to the author's intentions — make the actor's behavior more colorful and unpredictable while the choice keeps him on track emotionally.
moment_to_moment_inclusions.when_to_use: Whenever the actor becomes aware that he is imposing premeditated behavior; when compensational behavior or splits appear; as an ongoing practice in rehearsal.

using_available_stimulus.name: Using Available Stimulus
using_available_stimulus.description: A simple choice approach in which the actor uses what is actually present — the real other actor — selectively emphasizing the attractive or negative qualities depending on the emotional obligation, to stimulate the required emotional response.
using_available_stimulus.craft_use: After achieving selfless involvement, the actor identifies the emotional obligation of the scene and selectively emphasizes elements of the other actor that will stimulate the needed feelings. Works for both positive feelings (emphasizing attractive qualities) and negative feelings (emphasizing critical, rejecting, or judgmental qualities).
using_available_stimulus.when_to_use: When the actor has no specific choice process available; as a simple, immediate craft approach that almost always works to stimulate desired responses.

inner_monologue_with_personal_inventory.name: Inner Monologue with Personal Inventory
inner_monologue_with_personal_inventory.description: The actor speaks internally — forming words in the mind without intellectual processing — while simultaneously asking 'How am I feeling?' and responding honestly. The discovered impulses are then included in the ongoing scene or monologue expression.
inner_monologue_with_personal_inventory.instrumental_use: Reconnects the actor with real feelings when he becomes aware of redirection or splitting. Not an intellectual process — the actor simply forms words internally and responds to what he discovers.
inner_monologue_with_personal_inventory.craft_use: The inclusion of real impulses as part of the character's experience causes the actor's behavior to be more unpredictable and colorful while the underlying choice addresses the scene's emotional obligations.
inner_monologue_with_personal_inventory.when_to_use: When the actor becomes aware of redirection; when splits occur; as an ongoing in-scene technique to maintain organic connection.

i_feel_then_say_the_line.name: 'I Feel...' Then Say the Line
i_feel_then_say_the_line.description: Before each line of a monologue, the actor asks how he feels and responds with that impulse, then carries that feeling into the next line — establishing real organic grounding for each successive line.
i_feel_then_say_the_line.instrumental_use: Forces the actor to acknowledge his real moment-to-moment feelings rather than assuming the emotional state he thinks the material demands. Reveals the difference between imposed emotions and genuine impulses.
i_feel_then_say_the_line.craft_use: With practice, establishes the habit of carrying genuine feeling into lines rather than imposing emotional states from a cerebral origin.
i_feel_then_say_the_line.when_to_use: To address vocal dependency (keep voice organically connected) and concept (liberate from intellectual attachment); requires doing Personal Inventory first if the actor is habitually disconnected.

disconnected_impulsivity.name: Disconnected Impulsivity
disconnected_impulsivity.description: A three-part exercise done with great speed: (1) pointing to objects and responding impulsively with sounds; (2) same rapid impulsive response in gibberish; (3) same in English — rapid, stream-of-consciousness associations without logic or theme.
disconnected_impulsivity.instrumental_use: Creates complete impulsivity and gets the actor out of his head. Speed is essential — the pace prevents the intellect from organizing the responses. Liberates from the attachment to logic and meaning; breaks conceptual thinking habits; stimulates trust in the next moment.
disconnected_impulsivity.when_to_use: To antidote concept, premeditation, or any trap rooted in cerebral control of expression; as a warmup to stimulate impulsivity before scene work.

potpourri_of_monologues.name: Potpourri of Monologues
potpourri_of_monologues.description: The actor uses three separate monologues, moving freely between them. If he is coming from an organic place and expressing impulses moment to moment, all three will flow as one coherent stream of experience — sounding like a character free-associating.
potpourri_of_monologues.instrumental_use: Trains the actor to honor what he is experiencing and carry it into lines regardless of their textual source. Reveals whether the actor is truly coming from organic impulses or is conceptually attached to each piece.
potpourri_of_monologues.craft_use: Develops the skill of carrying real organic feeling into any material — demonstrating that the emotional source is the actor's reality, not the text.
potpourri_of_monologues.when_to_use: As a training exercise to antidote concept; when the actor needs to break loyalty to a specific piece; to develop trust in organic impulses.

being_exercise.name: The Being Exercise
being_exercise.description: The actor sits comfortably facing a group, doing Personal Inventory as a stream of consciousness — acknowledging all obstacles (tension, fear, self-consciousness, anger) until they are released and a genuine being state is achieved, usually over 15-20 minutes.
being_exercise.instrumental_use: The primary exercise for achieving a being state. By exposing and expressing all the 'demons' — tension, fear of judgment, discomfort — the actor gradually releases them and arrives at a state of authentic presence. Starting from a being state is a profound preparation for experiential acting.
being_exercise.when_to_use: Before scene work; as a regular class or training exercise; whenever the actor is aware of obstacles preventing authentic presence.

expanded_personal_inventory.name: Expanded Personal Inventory
expanded_personal_inventory.description: After eliminating tension through relaxation exercises, the actor asks himself where he is instrumentally in relation to where he needs to be for the beginning of the scene — identifying any gap between his current state and the emotional requirement of the material.
expanded_personal_inventory.instrumental_use: Assesses the actor's instrumental readiness and identifies what interim preparation is needed. For example, if the character is vulnerable but the actor feels closed, this inventory reveals that gap so it can be addressed before the scene begins.
expanded_personal_inventory.craft_use: Bridges instrument and craft by clarifying the emotional starting point required by the material and identifying specific exercises needed to reach that state.
expanded_personal_inventory.when_to_use: After relaxation exercises and before beginning rehearsal or performance; the second step in instrumental preparation after addressing tension.

two_person_being_workout.name: Two-Person Being Workout
two_person_being_workout.description: A three-part preparation exercise for two actors: (1) both actors express how they feel and acknowledge all obstacles to being present with each other; (2) Observe, Perceive, and Wonder together; (3) Ensemble Exercise. Together they establish a being state, selfless involvement, and creative dependency between scene partners.
two_person_being_workout.instrumental_use: Systematically eliminates the barriers between scene partners — self-consciousness, sexual distraction, performance anxiety — replacing them with genuine presence and ensemble connection.
two_person_being_workout.craft_use: Creates the foundation of authentic ensemble that makes all subsequent scene work organic; an excellent preparation before addressing any scene obligations.
two_person_being_workout.when_to_use: Before rehearsal with any scene partner; particularly important when partners don't know each other well or when previous rehearsals have felt mechanical.

preparing_instrumentally_for_audition.name: Preparing Instrumentally for an Audition
preparing_instrumentally_for_audition.description: Solo instrumental preparation for an audition: relaxation exercises, ego/self-esteem check (counting blessings, Pollyanna workout), then selfless involvement in the waiting room environment and with the people there — before addressing the craft obligations of the material.
preparing_instrumentally_for_audition.instrumental_use: Ensures the actor arrives at the audition in a being state rather than in a state of anxiety and self-consciousness. The sequence: eliminate tension → check ego state → selfless involvement.
preparing_instrumentally_for_audition.craft_use: Once instrumentally prepared, the actor can then address the material's obligations through his craft process.
preparing_instrumentally_for_audition.when_to_use: Before any audition; the instrumental preparation is a prerequisite for the craft preparation.

emotional_point_of_view_inventory.name: Emotional-Point-of-View Inventory
emotional_point_of_view_inventory.description: A self-questioning practice done out loud where the actor asks how they feel about every object, person, sound, and sensation in their environment, then expresses those responses verbally and physically.
emotional_point_of_view_inventory.instrumental_use: Builds awareness of real impulses, trains moment-to-moment responsiveness, and develops the habit of authentic emotional expression. Helps identify obstacles to BEING.
emotional_point_of_view_inventory.craft_use: Can be used as a pre-scene BEING preparation to establish an organic baseline before approaching material.
emotional_point_of_view_inventory.when_to_use: Daily practice; also as warm-up before rehearsal or performance. Whenever connection to authentic emotional flow is needed.

sources.summary: Described as gathering awareness of how objects affect you, building ability to respond and express all impulses.
Extended example of the inventory in action, moving through room, sounds, people, feelings.

emotional_point_of_view_nonverbal.name: Emotional Point of View, Without Words (Nonverbal)
emotional_point_of_view_nonverbal.description: A variation of the Emotional-Point-of-View exercise where all responses to the environment are expressed as unintelligible sounds rather than words, preventing intellectualization.
emotional_point_of_view_nonverbal.instrumental_use: Establishes connection with real organic impulses by bypassing verbal/intellectual processing. Produces greater vocal variety than word-based expression. Promotes BEING state.
emotional_point_of_view_nonverbal.craft_use: Foundation practice for achieving authentic expression; feeds into the craft process.
emotional_point_of_view_nonverbal.when_to_use: Daily practice; especially useful when an actor tends to describe feelings rather than express them.

sources.summary: Nonverbal version: translate impulses into unintelligible sounds while scanning environment. Avoids intellectualization; words often describe rather than express feelings.

inclusion_technique.name: Inclusion
inclusion_technique.description: The practice of allowing all impulses—whether relevant to the scene or not—to express themselves through the actor's behavior and lines, rather than suppressing them.
inclusion_technique.instrumental_use: Trains the actor to trust all impulses and maintain an unbroken flow of organic life. Eliminates the short-circuiting caused by conditioned taboos.
inclusion_technique.craft_use: Used during scene work to maintain reality even when distracting, irrelevant, or conflicting impulses arise. Prevents the split between internal reality and performed behavior.
inclusion_technique.when_to_use: During rehearsals, scene work, and performance whenever the actor notices impulses that seem inconsistent with the material.

sources.summary: Inclusion technique: using monologue to practice allowing all impulses to express through the words.
How to practice: recite known monologue, allow everything felt to express through the words regardless of its consistency with the material.
Including short-circuit feelings at taboo moments restores authentic impulse flow.

being_workout.name: BEING Workout
being_workout.description: Two actors face each other and relate moment-to-moment, conversationally expressing all obstacles (fears, distractions, tensions, concerns) as they arise. Done before rehearsing a scene together.
being_workout.instrumental_use: Achieves a threshold of real life and authentic impulse flow between two actors. When fears and concerns are exposed openly, the need to hide them is eliminated. Creates permission to be distracted or tense as part of the reality.
being_workout.craft_use: Establishes the organic baseline needed for authentic scene work with a partner. The choices explored from that place stimulate true emotional responses.
being_workout.when_to_use: At the start of any rehearsal with another actor. Especially important when both actors feel disconnected or perform artificially.

sources.summary: BEING Workout: two actors face each other, express all obstacles moment-to-moment; including obstacles creates unbroken reality flow.
By starting rehearsal this way, actors achieve threshold of real life from which authentic choices can be explored.

making_my_statement.name: Making My Statement
making_my_statement.description: An ego preparation where the actor largely expresses their personal point of view about everything they see, hear, and feel, including personal statements about life, values, and how they want to work.
making_my_statement.instrumental_use: Establishes a strong personal center; promotes the philosophical acceptance of irreverence. Roots the actor in their own perspective before approaching material.
making_my_statement.craft_use: Pre-rehearsal preparation that liberates the actor to bring their individual voice to the role.
making_my_statement.when_to_use: Before rehearsal, especially when feeling intimidated, subordinated to concept, or lacking self-permission.

sources.summary: Making My Statement: express personal point of view about all things seen, heard, felt around you.

due_taking_preparation.name: Due-Taking Preparation
due_taking_preparation.description: Acknowledging one's right to work creatively—done silently or audibly before or during rehearsal. The actor affirms their right to take time, explore, fail, and demand a supportive environment.
due_taking_preparation.instrumental_use: Establishes the actor's sense of entitlement to the creative process. Can be done 'on the record' so others hear it, which increases permission to risk.
due_taking_preparation.craft_use: Prepares the actor to explore choices freely and adventurously without social anxiety about the process.
due_taking_preparation.when_to_use: Before rehearsals; whenever the actor feels pressured, rushed, or denied the creative space needed.

sources.summary: Due-Taking: acknowledge right to work creatively; can be done silently or audibly; going 'on record' frees actor to risk more.

feeling_special.name: Feeling Special
feeling_special.description: An ego preparation where the actor focuses on their own uniqueness—that no one else in the world is exactly like them—as the foundation for irreverent self-expression.
feeling_special.instrumental_use: Achieves a sense of specialness that allows courageous expression of all impulses and opens doors to individual emotional facets. Empowers the actor to dimensionalize the character beyond the author's concept.
feeling_special.craft_use: Helps the actor bring their unique personal qualities to a role rather than playing a generic version of the character.
feeling_special.when_to_use: Before rehearsal or performance; when the actor feels they must conform to a standard or generic interpretation.

sources.summary: Feeling Special: focusing on uniqueness—no one exactly like you—allows courageous expression and brings unique individuality to the role.

sounds_with_a_choice.name: Sounds with a Choice
sounds_with_a_choice.description: A rehearsal irreverence technique where actors replace spoken dialogue with pure sounds while working for their scene choices. They relate to each other and respond to impulses entirely through non-verbal sound.
sounds_with_a_choice.instrumental_use: Eliminates concept and premeditation. Allows the unexpected and fills scenes with emotional colors. Forces real moment-to-moment responsiveness.
sounds_with_a_choice.craft_use: Used in rehearsal to explore emotional content of a scene without being tied to the meaning or logic of the written words. Helps actors discover impulses they wouldn't find through the text.
sounds_with_a_choice.when_to_use: In rehearsal when concept is taking over; when actors feel locked into line readings or predetermined emotional responses.

sources.summary: Sounds with a Choice: actors replace dialogue with sounds while working for choices; eliminates concept and premeditation.

gibberish_with_choice.name: Gibberish with a Choice
gibberish_with_choice.description: Two actors relate using gibberish words (abstract, meaningless syllables) instead of written dialogue while working for their scene choices and encouraging moment-to-moment reality.
gibberish_with_choice.instrumental_use: Eliminates concept and premeditation, like sounds, but is more like talking. Allows abstract word-formation without semantic meaning.
gibberish_with_choice.craft_use: Good intermediate step between pure sounds and returning to written dialogue. Preserves sentence structure without content, keeping emotional reality active.
gibberish_with_choice.when_to_use: In rehearsal as a step between sounds and scripted text; when actors need to free themselves from the conceptual burden of the written words.

sources.summary: Gibberish with Choice: like sounds but more talk-like; eliminates concept while preserving sentence-structure feeling. Good interim technique.

paraphrasing_improvisation.name: Moment-to-Moment Paraphrasing Improvisation
paraphrasing_improvisation.description: Actors paraphrase the scene—not verbatim but in their own words—paralleling the content and achieving the essence of the material while expressing their own personal feelings and impulses.
paraphrasing_improvisation.instrumental_use: Frees actors to allow, permit, and include all impulses without being tied to the logic of the words. Prevents premeditation and head-trapping.
paraphrasing_improvisation.craft_use: Explores the emotional and relational content of the scene while building authentic impulse-based behavior. When actors return to the written text, they carry the irreverent impulse quality with them.
paraphrasing_improvisation.when_to_use: In rehearsal when actors are trapped in their heads by the written lines; when emotional life isn't connecting to the text.

sources.summary: Paraphrasing improvisation: non-verbatim paraphrasing frees actor to include all impulses without word-logic trap.
Extended example showing paraphrasing that stays with scene circumstances while using personal emotional life.

nursery_rhymes.name: Nursery Rhymes
nursery_rhymes.description: Actors replace the written dialogue with nursery rhymes while working for their choices, taking care to avoid falling into the meter of the rhyme, which would reproduce the original trap of servicing the text.
nursery_rhymes.instrumental_use: Transfers emphasis from the meaning of words to impulses. Encourages irreverence and a wider emotional palette.
nursery_rhymes.craft_use: When actors return to the script after nursery rhymes, they typically feel more impulsive and irreverent in the scene.
nursery_rhymes.when_to_use: In rehearsal when concept is taking over or actors are too tied to line readings.

sources.summary: Nursery rhymes replace written lines; must avoid servicing the meter. Transfers emphasis from word-meaning to impulses; promotes irreverence.

choice_irreverence.name: Choice Irreverence
choice_irreverence.description: Being adventurous and experimental with the execution of the choice approach itself—using unexpected or even outrageous 'trick questions' or directions within the choice approach to stimulate accelerated impulsivity.
choice_irreverence.instrumental_use: Promotes a greater thrust in the process of exploring choices; prevents routine execution of choice approaches.
choice_irreverence.craft_use: Applies to any choice approach—Sense Memory, Imaginary Monologue, Believability, etc. The irreverent element exists in the direction the actor takes within the approach technique.
choice_irreverence.when_to_use: In rehearsal, when the choice approach is becoming mechanical or routine.

sources.summary: Choice irreverence: be adventurous in the execution of the choice approach; use unexpected trick questions within Sense Memory or other approaches.

antithetical_explorations.name: Antithetical Explorations
antithetical_explorations.description: Exploring the opposite of what the character seems to be—finding the loving side of an angry character, the vulnerable side of a tough one—to discover unusual behavioral facets and fragment concepts while encouraging irreverence.
antithetical_explorations.instrumental_use: Fragments concepts, promotes irreverence, and discovers the 'underlife' that motivates a character to behave in an opposite manner from the obvious.
antithetical_explorations.craft_use: Builds strong organic foundation for complex characters. Helps avoid superficial behavior by creating the impetus from which the character's emotional life comes rather than just expressing the emotion.
antithetical_explorations.when_to_use: In rehearsal, when character seems one-dimensional or when the actor is playing the result of the character's emotion rather than the cause.

sources.summary: Antithetical Explorations: explore opposite of character's obvious quality to discover underlife and fragment concept.
Actor creates loving impulses for angry character, then finds ways to be rejected, building resentments organically.

switch_trick_exploration.name: Switch-Trick Exploration
switch_trick_exploration.description: A technique where suggestions are rapidly fired at the actor during scene work, throwing them off balance so quickly that it creates a state of high impulsive irreverence, which then carries over when they return to the scripted lines.
switch_trick_exploration.instrumental_use: Creates accelerated impulsivity by overwhelming the actor's intellectual planning mechanisms. When actor returns to scene, they are more in touch with real impulsive inner life.
switch_trick_exploration.craft_use: Used exclusively in rehearsals to shake loose premeditation and result consciousness.
switch_trick_exploration.when_to_use: In rehearsal only; when actor is stuck in concept or premeditation and needs a radical reset.

sources.summary: Switch-Trick: rapid-fire suggestions throw actor off balance, creating high impulsive irreverence that transfers back to scene.

outrageous_exaggerations.name: Outrageous Exaggerations
outrageous_exaggerations.description: Actor exaggerates all behavior—vocally, physically, and verbally—to an outrageous degree, turning the scene into something like a ballet. The point is to break free of seriousness and concept, then carry the liberated impulsive quality back to the scene.
outrageous_exaggerations.instrumental_use: Brings flamboyance and music to lines; breaks the grip of concept and result consciousness. Creates impulsive freedom.
outrageous_exaggerations.craft_use: After the exaggeration phase, the actor returns to their choice with the impulsive freedom gained.
outrageous_exaggerations.when_to_use: In rehearsal when the actor feels stifled or trapped by the seriousness of the material.

sources.summary: Outrageous Exaggerations: exaggerate all behavior to break free of concept; carry impulsive freedom back to the written scene.

personal_inventory_technique.name: Personal Inventory
personal_inventory_technique.description: A self-questioning process asking 'How do I feel?' at any moment in the creative process. Responses are expressed—verbally or through behavior—or run silently as an Inner Monologue under the scene's lines.
personal_inventory_technique.instrumental_use: Stimulates organic state by discovering what is actually felt and expressing those impulses. Part of the Standard Relaxation Cluster.
personal_inventory_technique.craft_use: Used before the first line and during a scene whenever connection to internal reality is lost. Becomes an ongoing Inner Monologue that restimulates impulses and prevents premeditation.
personal_inventory_technique.when_to_use: Before scene work; whenever feeling disconnected, wooden, or caught in premeditation during a scene or performance.

sources.summary: Personal Inventory as relaxation cluster technique; instrumental and craft uses distinguished.
Example of Personal Inventory run as Inner Monologue mid-scene when feeling disconnected.

two_people_trick_each_other.name: Two People Trick Each Other
two_people_trick_each_other.description: During a scene (especially in a long run), one or both actors do unexpected but scene-appropriate things—unexpected physical touches, exaggerated moments, staring at the wall, not relating to force the other to work for attention—to keep each other on their toes and prevent predictability.
two_people_trick_each_other.instrumental_use: Stimulates fresh impulsive responsiveness and prevents the flatness that comes from repetition.
two_people_trick_each_other.craft_use: Promotes performance irreverence; keeps the play fresh and impulsive through the run.
two_people_trick_each_other.when_to_use: In a long run when scenes become stale and predictable; when impulsive responsiveness between actors has diminished.

sources.summary: Two People Trick Each Other: actors do unexpected scene-appropriate things to maintain freshness; not a game—a creative approach to prevent predictability.
Story of Lunt and Fontanne tricking each other with unexpected telephone ring; created fresh impulsiveness.

weight_gravity_relaxation.name: Weight-and-Gravity Relaxation
weight_gravity_relaxation.description: Slowly exaggerating the felt weight of the body while gently moving arms and body parts upward, then allowing them to fall with the exaggerated gravity. Done with deep breathing.
weight_gravity_relaxation.instrumental_use: Relaxes the body by using the sensation of exaggerated weight and gravity to release tension. Can be done unobtrusively on set while waiting.
weight_gravity_relaxation.craft_use: Physical relaxer used to reach the ready state before craft work begins.
weight_gravity_relaxation.when_to_use: Before scenes; whenever tension needs to be released quickly and unobtrusively.

sources.summary: Weight-and-gravity relaxation: exaggerate body weight, move arms up and let fall with gravity. Can be done while waiting on set.

tensing_relaxing.name: Tensing and Relaxing
tensing_relaxing.description: Tiring muscles by tensing/constricting the body to extreme, then slowly relaxing. Can be done whole-body at once or progressively from head to feet.
tensing_relaxing.instrumental_use: Forces physical tension to dissipate by exhausting the muscles. More extreme than weight-and-gravity relaxation; works quickly when actor is under immediate pressure.
tensing_relaxing.craft_use: Physical relaxer that can be used quickly on the spot when tension accumulates during a scene.
tensing_relaxing.when_to_use: When under immediate pressure and fast tension relief is needed; when body tension is extreme.

sources.summary: Tensing and Relaxing: tense whole body or progressively head-to-feet, then release. More extreme but effective on the spot.

stretching_yawning.name: Stretching and Yawning
stretching_yawning.description: Natural physical activities that serve important functions: yawning takes in oxygen and opens the voice box, releasing throat tension; stretching eliminates kinks from fatigue and tension.
stretching_yawning.instrumental_use: Improves voice quality (richer, lower after yawning), releases physical tension, oxygenates.
stretching_yawning.craft_use: Can be used as quick preparation before lines; yawning specifically improves vocal quality immediately.
stretching_yawning.when_to_use: Before scenes; as a quick vocal preparation; whenever physical fatigue or throat tension is present.

sources.summary: Stretching and yawning: yawn opens voice box, releases throat tension, lowers voice several octaves. Natural physical functions.

sensitizing_technique.name: Sensitizing
sensitizing_technique.description: Heightening responsiveness of specific senses by 'living in' that sense with concentrated attention. Used instrumentally with all five senses; craftually when a specific sense is sluggish during choice work.
sensitizing_technique.instrumental_use: Opens and heightens all sensory responsiveness as part of overall relaxation and BEING preparation.
sensitizing_technique.craft_use: When senses are sluggish during sensory choice exploration—isolate the unresponsive sense and concentrate entirely in it until it responds, then reinvest in the original choice.
sensitizing_technique.when_to_use: Instrumentally as part of relaxation cluster; craftually during sensory choice work when a sense is unresponsive.

sources.summary: Sensitizing as craft technique: used when senses are sluggish during choice exploration; intersperse while working for choice.
'Live' in the specific sense until it tingles with responsiveness; same process for all five senses with different concentrations.

available_stimulus_sweep.name: Available-Stimulus Sweep
available_stimulus_sweep.description: Letting the eyes sweep the room or stage, stopping at objects that appeal to genuine interest, exploring them with as many senses as possible, then moving on.
available_stimulus_sweep.instrumental_use: Gets the actor out of themselves and into the environment through genuine curiosity. Natural enemy of tension and self-consciousness.
available_stimulus_sweep.craft_use: Builds involvement and relationship with the environment and other actors; useful as pre-scene engagement.
available_stimulus_sweep.when_to_use: Before scenes; whenever tension or self-consciousness is present and involvement needs to be built.

sources.summary: Available-Stimulus Sweep: eyes sweep room, stop at interesting objects, explore with senses. Works when you allow interest, not force it. Can be done silently.

wonder_perceive_observe.name: Wonder, Perceive, and Observe
wonder_perceive_observe.description: A three-part involvement exercise for getting into the other actor. Observe (look for obvious things), Perceive (look deeper with more complex attention), Wonder (encourage curious exploration). Done as an inner monologue or out loud with the other actor.
wonder_perceive_observe.instrumental_use: Gets the actor into the other actor through genuine curiosity; establishes the foundation of a real relationship.
wonder_perceive_observe.craft_use: Used before or during scene work to build authentic relationship involvement with the scene partner. Can be run silently throughout an entire scene.
wonder_perceive_observe.when_to_use: Before scene work with a partner; when relationship feels artificial or the actor is not genuinely engaged with the partner.

sources.summary: Wonder, Perceive, Observe: three-part involvement exercise for the other actor. Observe = obvious; Perceive = deeper; Wonder = curious exploration.
Extended example of perceiving into the other actor: reading physical tension, eye movement, emotional state. Silent inner monologue.

sensory_exploration_involvement.name: Sensory Exploration (Involvement)
sensory_exploration_involvement.description: Using sensory questions about the other actor or objects on stage to heighten involvement. Asking 'What do I see in her eyes?' and responding sensorially. Used as an involvement technique distinct from sensory choice-creation.
sensory_exploration_involvement.instrumental_use: Heightens level of involvement through genuine sensory engagement with the environment and partner.
sensory_exploration_involvement.craft_use: Used as a prelude to exploring the first choice in a scene—sensory exploration aligned with the first emotional obligation warms up the actor's responsiveness.
sensory_exploration_involvement.when_to_use: In the minutes before a rehearsal or scene; when involvement needs to be heightened; when transitioning from instrumental preparation to craft preparation.

sources.summary: Sensory Exploration for involvement: use sensory questions about the other actor or objects to heighten involvement before exploring choices.
Example: sensory exploration about actress aligned with first emotional obligation of concern and empathy.

imaginary_monologue_preparation.name: Emotionally Meaningful Imaginary Monologues
imaginary_monologue_preparation.description: Talking to an absent person as if they were present—saying things that are affecting and meaningful to stimulate desired emotional responses. The imagined person's responses are allowed for (unlike Coffin Monologue).
imaginary_monologue_preparation.instrumental_use: Stimulates strong emotional responses by addressing significant people in the actor's life about meaningful matters.
imaginary_monologue_preparation.craft_use: Used as an emotional preparation to create a specific emotional state before a scene. The content is chosen to stimulate the desired emotional foundation.
imaginary_monologue_preparation.when_to_use: As emotional preparation before a scene; when specific emotional states need to be reached and sense memory or music haven't worked.

sources.summary: Imaginary Monologue: talk to absent person as if present; allow imagined responses; intelligently manipulate content to stimulate strong emotional responses.

sense_memory_with_impacting_objects.name: Sense Memory with Emotionally Impacting Objects
sense_memory_with_impacting_objects.description: Using Sense Memory to recreate a meaningful object—a person, pet, place, photograph, letter, sound, music—in order to elevate emotional state. The object is chosen for its emotional impact.
sense_memory_with_impacting_objects.instrumental_use: Can be used silently without calling attention to itself; doesn't interfere with what the actor is doing or saying.
sense_memory_with_impacting_objects.craft_use: A silent, unobtrusive way to prepare emotionally before or during a scene. Can be done while waiting on set; preparation anytime and anywhere.
sense_memory_with_impacting_objects.when_to_use: As emotional preparation; before scenes or during a long run to prime the instrument; when emotional accessibility needs to be elevated.

sources.summary: Sense Memory with impacting objects: can be done silently; doesn't call attention to itself; choice can be anything meaningful—person, dog, place, music.
Example: creating father sensorially using multiple senses—visual, tactile, olfactory. Continue until emotional responses arise naturally.

music_preparation.name: Music (Emotional Preparation)
music_preparation.description: Using a meaningful piece of music to prepare emotionally. Can be listened to, hummed internally, or recreated sensorially. Music is described as a 'very rich choice area.'
music_preparation.instrumental_use: Stimulates emotional responses by evoking memories, feelings, and associations tied to specific music.
music_preparation.craft_use: Prime the instrument with music related to the scene's emotional needs; catalogue music and its effects to have it available when needed. Humming is also valid—'if it works and is not intrusive, use it.'
music_preparation.when_to_use: As emotional preparation before or during a scene; especially effective when the emotional obligation involves nostalgia, past experiences, or strong feeling-associations.

sources.summary: Music: a rich choice area; catalogue music and responses to it; humming is not cheating—use whatever works organically.
Example: using nostalgic music to prime instrument before a scene about past times.

joy_euphoria.name: Joy and Euphoria
joy_euphoria.description: An exaggerated state of large experience and flamboyant expression where the actor commits to expressing joy and euphoria with full physical and vocal commitment.
joy_euphoria.instrumental_use: Stretches the instrument to expressive extreme. Makes the actor feel good, that anything is possible, and that no task is too difficult. Acting should be fun.
joy_euphoria.craft_use: Creates internal excitement and external expression that is visually compelling. Good preparation for scenes requiring high positive energy or for loosening tight instruments.
joy_euphoria.when_to_use: When the actor is feeling restricted, low-energy, or overly serious about their work; as a craft preparation for high-energy characters.

sources.summary: Joy and Euphoria: exaggerated state, full physical/vocal commitment; stretches instrument to expressive extreme; feeling that anything is possible.

superman_exercise.name: Superman
superman_exercise.description: An ego preparation where the actor declares their absolute superiority, power, and capability in all areas—'I have always done what I set out to do! I am a Superman!'—with full commitment.
superman_exercise.instrumental_use: Builds ego strength and self-confidence; instills belief in one's own abilities.
superman_exercise.craft_use: Meets the challenge of large ego obligations in characters. Used when the character has a much larger or more confident ego than the actor currently has.
superman_exercise.when_to_use: When the character has a powerful, dominant, or supremely confident ego state and the actor does not match this currently.

sources.summary: Superman: ego preparation for large ego obligations; commit to declaration of total capability and superiority.
Can continue for many minutes until in a very different ego state, hopefully closer to the character's.

sensory_self_endowment_technique.name: Sensory Self-Endowments
sensory_self_endowment_technique.description: Using Sense Memory questions to create the felt experience of different physical features on the body—adding or eliminating hair, weight, body size, attractiveness—to build ego state or character physicality.
sensory_self_endowment_technique.instrumental_use: Helps the actor feel better about themselves by creating a sensory experience of desired physical features.
sensory_self_endowment_technique.craft_use: Establishes a foundation for how the character feels about themselves, especially when the character's physical self-perception differs from the actor's. Has limitless possibilities.
sensory_self_endowment_technique.when_to_use: When the character's physical self-image (attractive, unattractive, physically powerful, weak, etc.) differs from the actor's, creating a dichotomy that would interfere with fulfillment of the piece.

sources.summary: Sensory Self-Endowments: create sensory experience of different physical features; used to build ego state matching character.
Example: creating sensory experience of long, thick hair using weight, texture, sound questions. Limitless possibilities.

fantasies_self_aggrandizements.name: Fantasies and Self-Aggrandizements
fantasies_self_aggrandizements.description: Creating elaborate personal fantasies of success, admiration, achievement, and specialness—as an inner monologue, a sharing exercise with another actor, or by 'walking through it.'
fantasies_self_aggrandizements.instrumental_use: Elevates ego state; generates a pleasant, confident sense of self that maintains itself for hours. Can be practiced as entertainment and then becomes easy to deploy when needed.
fantasies_self_aggrandizements.craft_use: Elevates the ego states of both actors when done as an improvisation before rehearsal. Creates sense of reality and fun.
fantasies_self_aggrandizements.when_to_use: Before rehearsal or performance; as an antidote to insecurity or low self-confidence. Can also be done 'for fun' to make it easier to access later.

sources.summary: Fantasies: best done by sneaking into them; personal appeal works best. Can be running inner monologue while doing other things.
Morris's airplane fantasy example; nice sense of self maintains for hours after; can be done as improvisation with other actor.

progress_reports.name: Progress Reports
progress_reports.description: Acknowledging and articulating specific positive changes and growth in oneself over a defined period of time. Must not embellish reality—must be genuinely believed.
progress_reports.instrumental_use: Puts ego into proper perspective; helps actors who tend to be self-critical by building evidence-based self-appreciation.
progress_reports.craft_use: Good craft preparation when the character has achieved positive growth or change that the actor currently doesn't feel about themselves.
progress_reports.when_to_use: Anytime; especially when the actor is being self-critical or when the character's self-awareness of growth differs from the actor's.

sources.summary: Progress Reports: acknowledge specific real growth; cannot embellish; must be believed. Good for actors hard on themselves.

magic_pocket_exercise.name: Magic-Pocket Exercise
magic_pocket_exercise.description: Imagining that one has extraordinary items in their pocket—a Hollywood contract, a ten-million-dollar check, car keys to a Ferrari—things that would create a state of great power, security, and specialness.
magic_pocket_exercise.instrumental_use: Stretches imagination while building ego. Creates a feeling of power, security, and specialness.
magic_pocket_exercise.craft_use: Can be used as a craft preparation when a character is in a very powerful, secure, or privileged position.
magic_pocket_exercise.when_to_use: As an ego-building or imagination-stretching exercise; before scenes requiring characters of great power or privilege.

sources.summary: Magic-Pocket Exercise: imagine having extraordinary items in your pocket; stretches imagination while building ego.

two_person_validations.name: Two-Person Validations
two_person_validations.description: Two actors, before rehearsing, sit together and honestly share specific positive things they see and appreciate in each other. Done conversationally, based on truth, using selective emphasis on the good things.
two_person_validations.instrumental_use: Helps both actors feel good about themselves and each other.
two_person_validations.craft_use: Works even better when the positive observations relate to the material's obligations. Especially good for scenes requiring mutual affection, respect, or appreciation.
two_person_validations.when_to_use: Before rehearsal or performance; especially valuable for scenes requiring genuine appreciation, affection, or mutual admiration between characters.

sources.summary: Two-Person Validations: actors share honest positive observations about each other; based on truth; can be done while making up or getting dressed.

abandonment.name: Abandonment
abandonment.description: Lying on the floor and having an all-out tantrum—moving the body with abandonment in all directions with no physical interference or leadership, while screaming and making huge uninterrupted vocal expurgations.
abandonment.instrumental_use: Purges tension and conglomerated impulses. Done until tension is purged.
abandonment.craft_use: Used with specific content to build a foundation for volatile, irrational character behavior. Must have a specific goal: what emotional state to stimulate and what content the Abandonment must have.
abandonment.when_to_use: When the character is highly volatile and irrational; as an instrumental tension-purger. Not appropriate for subtle emotional states.

sources.summary: Abandonment: all-out tantrum on floor with body and voice; instrumental = purge tension; craft = specific goal to build foundation for volatile character.

confront_encounter.name: Confront and Encounter
confront_encounter.description: Aggressively confronting real people—present or imaginary—with unfinished business, grievances, indictments, or strong impulses. Done as an imaginary monologue with absent people or conversationally with present ones.
confront_encounter.instrumental_use: Purges suppressed feelings of grievance, resentment, and unfinished relational business.
confront_encounter.craft_use: Stimulates strong confrontational or aggressive emotional states needed for specific scenes. The content of the imaginary monologue is chosen to produce the desired emotional quality.
confront_encounter.when_to_use: When the scene requires strong confrontational impulses; when the character has a history of grievance or unresolved conflict.

sources.summary: Confront and Encounter: confront real or imaginary people with unfinished business; usually done as imaginary monologue since real people not present at needed times.

anger_rage_expression.name: Anger-and-Rage Expression
anger_rage_expression.description: Expressing anger and rage with large vocal and physical commitment. The craft use requires intelligent choice of content—what to express anger about—to ventilate the right kind of emotional life.
anger_rage_expression.instrumental_use: Purges anger and tension instrumentally with any content.
anger_rage_expression.craft_use: Used to stimulate underlying anger that a character may be hiding with rational control—creates the underlife before the controlled surface is added. Content must be chosen to produce the right emotional quality.
anger_rage_expression.when_to_use: When the character's underlife is anger/rage that is covered by a composed exterior; when anger preparation is needed for a scene. Not appropriate for subtle material.

sources.summary: Anger-and-Rage Expression as craft technique: must choose content that ventilates the right emotional life; good for characters who are seething inside but appear composed.

nonverbal_relating_exercise.name: A Nonverbal Relating Exercise
nonverbal_relating_exercise.description: Two actors sit facing each other and relate without words, gestures, or mime—simply allowing whatever is happening in the moment to happen between them, responding to each other in an unbroken chain of emotional responses.
nonverbal_relating_exercise.instrumental_use: Deepens involvement by removing verbal description; transfers energy to direct emotional contact.
nonverbal_relating_exercise.craft_use: Establishes a real emotional relationship with the scene partner based on non-verbal emotional communication. The relationship deepens as the exercise proceeds.
nonverbal_relating_exercise.when_to_use: As a relationship preparation before scenes; when the emotional relationship between scene partners needs to be deepened before work on text.

sources.summary: Nonverbal Relating Exercise: no words, no gestures, no mime; allow what happens to happen; unbroken chain of emotional responses.

looking_for_new_things_in_partner.name: Looking for Things in the Other Person You Haven't Seen Before
looking_for_new_things_in_partner.description: Deliberately observing and discovering specific features and qualities in the other actor that have previously been taken for granted—physical features, behavioral patterns, emotional qualities.
looking_for_new_things_in_partner.instrumental_use: Trains genuine curiosity and involvement with other people. The actor as student of life and behavior must be genuinely interested in people.
looking_for_new_things_in_partner.craft_use: Builds authentic relationship investment in the scene partner; promotes genuine caring and interest.
looking_for_new_things_in_partner.when_to_use: As a relationship preparation; when the relationship with the scene partner has become habitual, taken-for-granted, or routine.

sources.summary: Looking for new things in the partner: deliberately discover previously overlooked features. Actor as student of life must find everything interesting.

feedback_technique.name: Feedback
feedback_technique.description: Two actors ask each other honest, important questions in a conversational framework to build authentic relationship investment. Goes beyond pleasantries to genuine curiosity about each other.
feedback_technique.instrumental_use: Builds genuine curiosity and relationship between actors.
feedback_technique.craft_use: Relationship preparation specifically designed to build the authentic relationship needed for material obligations.
feedback_technique.when_to_use: Before scenes requiring deep relationship investment; especially when actors don't know each other well.

sources.summary: Feedback: conversational exchange of important questions; no rigid structure but structured; builds real relationship.

like_dislike_feedback.name: Like-and-Dislike Feedback
like_dislike_feedback.description: A version of Feedback where actors exclusively share what they like about each other and what they 'like least'—never using the word 'dislike' to keep the exercise positive and productive.
like_dislike_feedback.instrumental_use: Builds mutual appreciation while addressing genuine concerns in a positive framework.
like_dislike_feedback.craft_use: Relationship preparation that creates authentic mutual regard while acknowledging real differences.
like_dislike_feedback.when_to_use: When relationship needs to be both honest and positive; as variation of Feedback.

sources.summary: Like-and-Dislike Feedback: exclusive focus on what is liked and liked least; 'dislike' word avoided to keep exercise positive.

intimate_sharing_relationship.name: Intimate Sharing (Relationship Use)
intimate_sharing_relationship.description: Two actors share genuinely intimate and meaningful personal material with each other specifically to build closeness and shared understanding rather than to purge fear of exposure.
intimate_sharing_relationship.instrumental_use: Builds genuine intimacy between actors through authentic personal sharing.
intimate_sharing_relationship.craft_use: Creates a foundation of closeness and real relationship investment needed for scenes requiring deep intimacy. Different from the emotional preparation use—goal is closeness with the other person, not elevation of vulnerability state.
intimate_sharing_relationship.when_to_use: Before scenes requiring genuine intimacy, closeness, or deep personal familiarity between characters.

sources.summary: Intimate Sharing for relationship: goal is establishing intimacy/involvement with another person (vs. emotional preparation goal of elevating vulnerability).

endowments_technique.name: Endowments
endowments_technique.description: Sensorially adding or substituting qualities in the other actor or an object through the Sense Memory process—making them have features, qualities, or characteristics they don't actually have.
endowments_technique.instrumental_use: Not primarily instrumental; occasionally used to increase comfort with a scene partner.
endowments_technique.craft_use: Changes the other actor into the character they are supposed to be (relationship obligation) or creates specific relational feelings—attraction, repulsion, love, fear. Core technique for relationship fulfillment.
endowments_technique.when_to_use: When the scene partner doesn't naturally evoke the needed relationship feelings; when character relationship obligations are not being felt.

sources.summary: Endowments: totally dependent on Sense Memory; cannot be done any other way effectively.
Like sculpting with Sense Memory: add/subtract until the needed reality is created.

animation_workout.name: Animation Workout
animation_workout.description: An irreverent preparation where the actor expresses everything they feel in a highly animated, exaggerated manner—large vocal and physical expression—over an extended period.
animation_workout.instrumental_use: Sets up freedom to allow whatever is felt to come out; encourages emotional abandonment of expression; creates a permission state that carries into rehearsal.
animation_workout.craft_use: Freeing quality promotes irreverence in all that follows; impulses flow into the choice, choice stimulates new impulses, those impulses express through the lines.
animation_workout.when_to_use: Before rehearsal in a restrictive environment; when the actor needs to establish permission for large, uninhibited expression.

sources.summary: Animation Workout: express everything in highly animated manner; freeing quality carries into rehearsal.
Can start humorous and move to heart-wrenching grief; chain reaction of impulse flow follows.

freedom_workout.name: Freedom Workout
freedom_workout.description: Similar to Animation Workout but with more physical freedom and less requirement for organic expression—can look like ballet, leaping and twirling, or animal-like movement. The freedom of the body liberates the instrument.
freedom_workout.instrumental_use: Creates physical and expressive freedom; liberates the instrument from physical and emotional restrictions.
freedom_workout.craft_use: Used as an irreverent preparation before scenes requiring physical freedom and expressive range.
freedom_workout.when_to_use: Before rehearsal when physical and expressive restriction are problems; when the body is tight and movement is constrained.

sources.summary: Freedom Workout: similar to Animation but more physical freedom; less organically required; can be ballet-like or animal-like.

nonverbal_approach.name: Nonverbal Approach (Irreverent Preparation)
nonverbal_approach.description: Doing a scene without words—replacing the dialogue with movement, physical expression, or silence—to liberate the actor from the predictable emotional expectations carried by the words.
nonverbal_approach.instrumental_use: Removes predictable emotional associations with specific words; creates new variety of expression and colors of life.
nonverbal_approach.craft_use: Frees the actor to be as expressive without words, then carry that freedom back to the scene with words. Words should not be the governor of emotional flow.
nonverbal_approach.when_to_use: When the written words are triggering predictable emotional responses rather than authentic impulse-based behavior.

sources.summary: Nonverbal Approach: strange freedom replaces words; infinite more colors and kinds of life emerge without verbal expectations.

sense_memory_choice_approach.name: Sense Memory (Choice Approach)
sense_memory_choice_approach.description: The systematic use of sensory questions—directed to each of the five senses—to create absent objects, people, places, or events so that the actor can actually experience their presence. The foundational choice approach.
sense_memory_choice_approach.instrumental_use: With sufficient daily practice, the senses can be trained to respond to absent objects as if present.
sense_memory_choice_approach.craft_use: The primary choice approach for creating any object, person, place, or event that will fulfill an obligation. Process continues during scene work—between lines, while listening to other actor. Can be done silently and unobtrusively.
sense_memory_choice_approach.when_to_use: As the primary approach for most obligations; whenever a specific absent object needs to be made real for the actor during scene work.

sources.summary: Sense Memory as choice approach: explorative process using all five senses; creates absent objects so actor can actually experience them.
Process: directed sensory questions to specific senses; can ask 50+ questions per sense area; go back and forth between senses.
Process continues during scene—between lines, while listening; actor still hears and is affected by other actor.

task_inventory_technique.name: Task Inventory
task_inventory_technique.description: A ten-step systematic process for finding the right choice when stuck, concluding with a Fail-Safe Device. Identifies obligation, finds personal parallel experience, specifies common denominator, selects choice, determines approach.
task_inventory_technique.craft_use: Eliminates confusion when trying to find the right choice. Can be done quickly with experience; even usable in the limited time of film work.
task_inventory_technique.when_to_use: When the actor cannot identify a choice for an obligation; when all other approaches to finding a choice have failed; as preventive practice even when not needed.

sources.summary: Task Inventory: 10-step process plus Fail-Safe Device; practice it even when not needed to become facile.
Fail-Safe Device: when all else fails, ask what would create that feeling right now.

daily_inventories_technique.name: Daily Inventories
daily_inventories_technique.description: The disciplined daily practice of cataloguing emotional responses to everyday stimuli in specific sensory detail, building a personal library of choices for future use.
daily_inventories_technique.instrumental_use: Trains the actor to be alive and responsive to all stimuli; develops the habit of awareness and specific emotional responsiveness.
