daily_inventories_technique.craft_use: Builds a rich, personally relevant library of choices that can be used in scene work. The actor who catalogues daily will always have choices available.
daily_inventories_technique.when_to_use: Every day, throughout the day—a discipline of living as an actor 24 hours a day.

sources.summary: Daily Inventories: catalogue emotional responses to everyday stimuli specifically and sensorially for future use.
Inventory questions for a specific encounter: sensory details of every element.

choice_hunt_technique.name: Choice Hunt
choice_hunt_technique.description: A lying-down, eyes-closed exercise where the actor goes back in time through meaningful personal experiences, allowing memories to surface and cataloguing experiences with strong emotional potential.
choice_hunt_technique.instrumental_use: Opens access to buried memories and emotional experiences; encourages expression of stimulated impulses.
choice_hunt_technique.craft_use: Discovers potential choices from personal history that haven't been considered before; expands the actor's choice repertoire.
choice_hunt_technique.when_to_use: In solitude; when the actor's choice repertoire feels limited; as a regular practice to continually expand available choices.

sources.summary: Choice Hunt: lying down, eyes closed, revisiting meaningful events; generates unexpected impulses; memory begins releasing spontaneously with regular practice.

available_stimulus_approach.name: Available Stimulus (Choice Approach)
available_stimulus_approach.description: Using what is actually present in the environment—the other actor, the place, sounds, objects, energy—as the stimulus to fulfill scene obligations. First consideration before any other choice approach.
available_stimulus_approach.instrumental_use: Not primarily instrumental—but can be used instrumentally when the environment itself stimulates involvement.
available_stimulus_approach.craft_use: Most efficient choice approach when the environment contains elements that naturally stimulate the needed emotional response. Use Selective Emphasis to focus on the most impelling element. Use Inner Monologue to intensify the available stimulus response.
available_stimulus_approach.when_to_use: Always check first—before considering other choice approaches. If Available Stimulus can do the job, use it; it requires the least additional work.

sources.summary: Available Stimulus: should be first consideration; most available if it exists.
Checklist of questions for Available Stimulus exploration: environment, objects, people, sounds, light, words in the play.

sensory_suggestion_approach.name: Sensory Suggestion (Choice Approach)
sensory_suggestion_approach.description: Combining narrative description ('He looks enraged and moves toward me') with targeted sensory questions to quickly build an imaginary reality that pure Sense Memory would take much longer to establish.
sensory_suggestion_approach.craft_use: Used when time is limited and a specific emotional response must be quickly established; when changing the perceived reality of the other actor in the scene; when the full Sense Memory process would take too long relative to the scene's demands.
sensory_suggestion_approach.when_to_use: When speed of establishment matters; when working with an imaginary person or trying to change the perceived reality of the actual scene partner.

sources.summary: Sensory Suggestion: narrative + sensory questions combined; ten times faster than pure Sense Memory.
Can change perceived behavior/reality of actual other actor in scene using Sensory Suggestion.

evocative_words_approach.name: Evocative Words (Choice Approach)
evocative_words_approach.description: Using carefully selected clusters of words, phrases, and associative language—from personal experiences or belief structures—to evoke the desired emotional state, run as an inner monologue or audible preparation.
evocative_words_approach.craft_use: Particularly effective in scenes with many lines and rapid dialogue where sensory work would interfere. Run as inner monologue under the scene's dialogue. Also excellent in combination with other approaches (Sense Memory for environment + Evocative Words for emotional sustaining).
evocative_words_approach.when_to_use: When rapid dialogue makes sensory choice approaches impractical; when working from strong personal belief structures; when other approaches have not produced the needed emotional state.

sources.summary: Evocative Words: good when rapid dialogue prevents sensory work; relates to experiences or belief structures.
Can use belief structures (Holocaust, fascism) to fuel character obligations throughout play.

believability_approach.name: Believability (Choice Approach)
believability_approach.description: Both actors (or one alone) build a shared fictional reality by starting with threads of actual truth and progressively adding untrue elements—until truth and untruth become indistinguishable. Selectively emphasize both.
believability_approach.instrumental_use: Can be used instrumentally to build genuine relationship and trust between actors.
believability_approach.craft_use: One of the most enjoyable choice approaches. Particularly powerful as a collaborative technique—both actors structure the Believability together before or during rehearsal to create shared relational reality for the scene.
believability_approach.when_to_use: When the relationship obligation requires a history or emotional depth that doesn't exist between the actual actors; when building shared circumstantial reality for complex relational scenes.

sources.summary: Believability: enjoyable; can be done alone or collaboratively; mixture of truth and untruth.
Deftly mix truth and untruth until indistinguishable; selectively emphasize both.

trick_believability.name: Trick Believability
trick_believability.description: Launching a real improvisation with the other actor, without revealing that it is a structured Believability approach, in order to stimulate the emotional life and relationship dynamics the scene requires. The other actor's authentic responses feed the initiating actor's reality.
trick_believability.instrumental_use: Can be instrumental in generating genuine irritation, conflict, or other emotional states between actors.
trick_believability.craft_use: Used when the scene requires a real, charged relationship reality and conventional preparation has not produced it; particularly effective when actors know the material's obligations and can calibrate the improvisation toward those ends.
trick_believability.when_to_use: When working with actors who would welcome such an approach (must know your collaborators); when conventional approaches have not generated the required relational life; use common sense about when to reveal what was done.

sources.summary: Trick Believability: one actor launches real improvisation without the other knowing; their response feeds the reality.
Extended example: actor criticizes actress's lateness to stimulate irritation; she doesn't know it's a crafted approach.
Possible consequences; use with actors who would welcome it; use common sense.

endowments_approach.name: Endowments (Choice Approach)
endowments_approach.description: Sensorially changing an available object or person by endowing it with qualities it does not possess—starting with the existing reality and asking sensory questions that progressively transform perception of it.
endowments_approach.craft_use: Used when: the scene partner needs to evoke a specific emotional relationship (endow with features of someone important); the character is older/sicker/different from the actual actor; a prop needs to feel like a priceless object; a room must feel like a different architectural space; one obligation must be addressed through another actor's perceived reality.
endowments_approach.when_to_use: When available stimulus doesn't produce the needed relationship or emotional response; when endowing the other actor with the features of someone from the actor's own life who produces the right emotional reality.

sources.summary: Endowments: sensorially changing object or person with qualities it doesn't have.
Extended aging endowment process example; one choice addressing two obligations simultaneously.
Endowing other actor with features of someone else: start with similarities, use contrasts, endow sensorially.

imaginary_monologue_approach.name: Imaginary Monologues (Choice Approach)
imaginary_monologue_approach.description: Talking out loud (or as inner monologue) to an imaginary person as if they were present, selecting the right person and the right things to say in order to stimulate the emotional state required by the scene.
imaginary_monologue_approach.instrumental_use: Not primarily instrumental—but can ventilate deep personal material.
imaginary_monologue_approach.craft_use: Most common use: as preparation before a scene or as inner monologue under dialogue. Can be combined with Sense Memory (sensory questions interspersed with words) to dimensionalize the imaginary person's presence and create unpredictability. Can be used to address any of the seven major obligations.
imaginary_monologue_approach.when_to_use: When a specific emotional state or relationship dynamic is required and it can be paralleled by a real person from the actor's life; when the actor has much to say to someone living or dead; when fast dialogue makes sensory approaches difficult (run as inner monologue).

sources.summary: Imaginary Monologues: talk to an imaginary person as if present; pick right person and right content.
Combined with Sense Memory for dimensionality; sensory questions interspersed; allows imaginary person to 'respond.'

imaginary_dialogue_approach.name: Imaginary Dialogues (Choice Approach)
imaginary_dialogue_approach.description: Two actors simultaneously doing Imaginary Monologues out loud to their respective imaginary choices, relating what they say to each other, and allowing the other's words to come from their respective imaginary person.
imaginary_dialogue_approach.instrumental_use: Not primarily instrumental—though it fires the emotional furnace of both actors.
imaginary_dialogue_approach.craft_use: Highly collaborative; requires agreement on choices. Both actors identify obligations, each selects an imaginary person from their own life who parallels the scene's dynamics. Done best before or during rehearsal; the stimulated life carries directly into the scene text.
imaginary_dialogue_approach.when_to_use: When the scene requires a deeply charged relational reality between two characters; when the relationship between the actual actors is insufficiently charged; when both actors work in this manner and can collaborate on choices.

sources.summary: Imaginary Dialogues: two actors doing Imaginary Monologues together out loud; each allows the other's words to come from their choice.
Waiting for Lefty example: detailed choices and obligations for both characters; each actor picks from personal life.

externals_approach.name: Externals (Choice Approach)
externals_approach.description: Getting a physical sense of an animal, another person, an inanimate object, or an insect through one's own instrument, then translating that sense into human behavior for use in a scene. One of the richest and most complex choice approach categories.
externals_approach.instrumental_use: Strongly instrumental—can pique primal and primitive parts of the actor's instrument that no other technique can reach, effecting total physical, emotional, intellectual, and vocal change.
externals_approach.craft_use: Animals: observe and absorb through the eight-element approach technique; translate to human behavior by degrees; use to fulfill character, emotional, historic, and time-and-place obligations. People: same approach, no translation needed. Inanimate Objects and Insects covered separately.
externals_approach.when_to_use: When the character requires physical attitudes, thought patterns, or behavior the actor cannot reach through any other means; when character obligations involve primal/primitive qualities; when the role explicitly invokes animal-like qualities; when conventional choices have not produced the needed transformation.

sources.summary: Externals: four involvements; richest category; can be used for almost all obligation areas.
Six purposes; keys to the unconscious: approaching inner life from outside in.
Using an animal as a choice: can address historic, character, and emotional obligations; examples from Morris's own career.

objects_contact_body_approach.name: Objects that Come into Contact with the Body (Choice Approach)
objects_contact_body_approach.description: Using clothing, accessories, weapons, or props worn or held by the actor—real or sensorially created—to stimulate changes in emotional state, physical rhythm, ego, and behavior.
objects_contact_body_approach.instrumental_use: Can have instrumental dimension: putting on boots or a weapon may access physical confidence, ego, or aggression that opens the actor's instrument.
objects_contact_body_approach.craft_use: When the real article is available in the scene: it becomes Available Stimulus—just relate to it. When not available: use Sense Memory to systematically create the object's presence through sensory questions about weight, pressure, texture, temperature, movement, sound.
objects_contact_body_approach.when_to_use: When character obligation involves specific physical attitudes or emotional states that an object would naturally produce; when the character's world includes meaningful objects not available in performance.

sources.summary: Objects that Contact Body: clothing and objects worn/held stimulate emotional and behavioral state changes.
When real object unavailable: Sense Memory creates it; when available: becomes Available Stimulus.

moment_to_moment_inner_monologue.name: Moment-to-Moment Inner Monologue
moment_to_moment_inner_monologue.description: Including between the words of a scene, as an internal verbal flow, what is actually going on inside the actor right now—allowing those impulses to express through and affect the dialogue.
moment_to_moment_inner_monologue.instrumental_use: Strongly instrumental: prevents result-consciousness and leadership by anchoring the actor in the present moment's actual experience.
moment_to_moment_inner_monologue.craft_use: Primary antidote for leadership and result-consciousness. Keeps the actor in contact with his real impulsive reality. Also used to take advantage of Available Reality—when the actor feels things similar to what the character feels, ventilating those through the lines promotes scene reality.
moment_to_moment_inner_monologue.when_to_use: When the actor has lost touch with internal impulsive reality; when leadership or result-consciousness has been identified; when what the actor actually feels in the moment parallels the scene's requirements.

sources.summary: Moment-to-Moment Inner Monologue: include what is going on inside between words; antidote for leadership.
Nursery rhyme example showing internal monologue between words; impulses encouraged to express through dialogue.

character_inner_monologue.name: Character Inner Monologue
character_inner_monologue.description: Structuring an internal monologue that parallels the character's thought process by using the actor's own personal realities—not thinking the character's literal thoughts, but finding personal parallels that create an emotionally equivalent inner life.
character_inner_monologue.craft_use: Run under the lines throughout a scene or play to fuel the desired emotional life. The actor identifies how the character thinks—his points of view, prejudices, emotional biases—and finds personal equivalents from his own life and belief structures. The monologue varies somewhat from performance to performance.
character_inner_monologue.when_to_use: When the character's inner thought process must be sustained beneath ongoing dialogue; when the character has strong prejudices, beliefs, or biases for which the actor can find personal parallels.

sources.summary: Character Inner Monologue: parallel the character's thought process using actor's own realities.
'Chickenshit colonel' example: Morris uses his own unbending commitment to craft as parallel to the colonel's rigidity.

sensory_speculation_approach.name: Sensory Speculation (Choice Approach)
sensory_speculation_approach.description: Using the Sense Memory process speculatively—exploring through imagined sensory questions what it would feel like to touch, smell, taste, or hear an object or person the actor has never physically experienced—in order to stimulate the required emotional state.
sensory_speculation_approach.craft_use: Used when a needed object or person is inaccessible to physical contact; when an emotional state must be stimulated through sensory engagement with something distant or unreachable; when endowing someone with behavior never previously witnessed.
sensory_speculation_approach.when_to_use: When the object of the scene's emotion is across the stage and can't be touched; when the actor must feel attraction, terror, or desire toward something not yet experienced; when sensory endowment requires speculation about unfamiliar behavior.

sources.summary: Sensory Speculation: speculatively exploring through Sense Memory what it would feel like to experience an inaccessible object or person.

sub_personality_approach.name: Sub-Personalities (Choice Approach)
sub_personality_approach.description: Identifying which sub-personality controls the character in the material, then eliciting the matching sub-personality from within the actor's own instrument, so that the entire scene or play can be done from that authentic part of the actor.
sub_personality_approach.instrumental_use: Strongly instrumental: calls forth a part of the actor's actual personality that may not normally be accessible or expressed.
sub_personality_approach.craft_use: Can address multiple obligations simultaneously with a single choice. The sub-personality has its own unique intelligence for the material. Particularly powerful for complex characters controlled by a single dominant personality facet.
sub_personality_approach.when_to_use: When the character's behavior can be identified as emanating from a specific sub-personality (the victim, the warrior, the free spirit, the moralist, the demonic, etc.); when conventional approaches have not produced the authentic dimensional reality required.

sources.summary: Sub-Personalities: identify the sub-personality the character runs from; elicit matching one in self; can address all obligations with single choice.
Examples: the victim sub-personality for victim characters; the free spirit for impulsive, irresponsible characters.

creative_visualization_approach.name: Creative Visualization (Choice Approach)
creative_visualization_approach.description: Guiding oneself—eyes closed—into a real or imagined environment supported by all five senses, allowing the unconscious to produce thoughts and impulses that then carry over into the scene.
creative_visualization_approach.instrumental_use: Not primarily instrumental—but can pique unconscious connections deeply.
creative_visualization_approach.craft_use: Excellent foundation for time-and-place obligation. Can address emotional and other obligations. Later can be done with eyes open ('inner eye'). Always support with sensory engagement—avoid staying in the head.
creative_visualization_approach.when_to_use: When the scene requires a specific place reality (time-and-place obligation) that the actual stage environment cannot provide; when other approaches have not reached the needed emotional state; when the actor wants to transport himself into a vivid imaginary or remembered environment.

sources.summary: Creative Visualization: twenty-second and final choice approach; close eyes, guide into environment, support with five senses.
Addresses all seven major obligations; not to be confused with Affective Memory—entirely different process.

voice_dialogue_sub_personality.name: Voice Dialogue (Sub-Personality Technique)
voice_dialogue_sub_personality.description: A two-person facilitation process in which one actor (the facilitator) asks to speak to specific sub-personalities of the other, who physically moves to accommodate each sub-personality and speaks from that part of himself.
voice_dialogue_sub_personality.instrumental_use: Strongly instrumental: makes accessible previously disowned or suppressed sub-personalities, expanding the range of available emotional and behavioral life.
voice_dialogue_sub_personality.craft_use: Used to identify and call forth the sub-personality that matches the character's controlling personality structure. Facilitator must be non-judgmental and always return the person to an objective ego state after the facilitation.
voice_dialogue_sub_personality.when_to_use: When preparing for a role that requires a personality facet the actor has difficulty accessing; when exploring the range of one's own sub-personalities; when another actor can serve as skilled facilitator.

sources.summary: Voice Dialogue: facilitator talks to specific sub-personalities; person moves to accommodate new personality.
Example facilitation: the angry sub-personality reveals its conflict with the pleaser sub-personality.
Facilitator rules: non-judgmental; always return to ego state; never leave in sub-personality.

animal_spine_technique.name: Animal as Character Spine
animal_spine_technique.description: The actor chooses an animal whose essential qualities — movement rhythms, physical center of gravity, energy level, stealth, power, speed — match the character's inner life, then studies that animal directly (at a zoo, in nature, via video), absorbs its qualities, and translates them into human behavior as the foundation of the character's physicality.
animal_spine_technique.instrumental_use: Opens the actor's expressive body and releases habitual self-conscious movement patterns; gives permission to be bold and physically specific.
animal_spine_technique.craft_use: Creates a distinctive, coherent, and embodied physical life for a character that is rooted in a real behavioral logic rather than invented intellectually.
animal_spine_technique.when_to_use: During character preparation when a role calls for distinctive physical qualities or when the actor needs a non-psychological anchor for physicality. Requires ongoing reinvestment throughout rehearsal and filming.

sources.summary: Morris uses a black leopard's rhythms, centers, and tempo for his assassin 'Tippo' — going to the zoo for days, then periodically getting on all fours between scenes to reinvest in the animal quality.

method_reality_continuity.name: Method Reality Continuity (Off-Camera Preparation)
method_reality_continuity.description: The actor maintains the emotional, relational, and behavioral reality of the character continuously throughout the production process — including between takes, at meals, and in interactions with the other cast members — to prevent the authentic experience from being diluted by ordinary social behavior.
method_reality_continuity.instrumental_use: Keeps the actor's instrument primed and genuinely responsive; avoids the 'reset' problem where actors must manufacture emotion cold at each take.
method_reality_continuity.craft_use: Ensures the scene's emotional reality has genuine history and continuity, making the work organically consistent and believable.
method_reality_continuity.when_to_use: When playing characters whose relational reality with other characters is central to the scene (distance, contempt, attraction, rivalry); requires the other actors to understand what is happening or be coached to work with it.

sources.summary: De Niro maintains genuine rejection of Caruso between takes, sustaining the character dynamic; Morris understands and coaches Caruso to work within that reality.
Morris builds distaste and anger toward the music teacher from the start of the 'Fame' episode, before their confrontation scene.

primal_moan_preparation.name: Primal Moan
primal_moan_preparation.description: The actor makes loud, deep moaning sounds originating from deep in the body — not vocalizations but extended primitive moaning from the core — to physically discharge accumulated tension, emotional blocks, and self-consciousness before a scene or in preparation.
primal_moan_preparation.instrumental_use: Releases tension and emotional blocks held in the body; frees the instrument from constriction created by nervousness, self-consciousness, or fear of judgment on set.
primal_moan_preparation.craft_use: Prepares the actor's instrument to be genuinely available for emotional experience in the scene.
primal_moan_preparation.when_to_use: Before emotionally demanding scenes, especially when the actor is entering a high-pressure environment (big star set, emotionally vulnerable scene, exposure scene). Can be done quietly, though the technique is inherently loud. Requires private space or the courage to do it publicly.

sources.summary: Priscilla Barnes lies in the fetal position on the set of The Crossing Guard and performs a primal moan; Jack Nicholson hears it and joins in from across the soundstage in support.

enchanted_play_preparation.name: Enchanted Play State Preparation
enchanted_play_preparation.description: The actor deliberately evokes the joy, playfulness, and excitement of acting itself as a pre-performance state — through interaction with co-actors, through physical ritual (standing in stage lights, moving through the performance space), or through play — to access the 'enchanted sense of play' that allows natural performance.
enchanted_play_preparation.instrumental_use: Bypasses anxiety, exhaustion, or negativity by accessing the authentic joy of the craft; creates a positive emotional platform for the work.
enchanted_play_preparation.craft_use: Allows the actor to be fully present and responsive in scenes by arriving in a state of genuine engagement rather than tension.
enchanted_play_preparation.when_to_use: Particularly useful when the actor is tired, anxious, or struggling to 'get there.' Lucy Ball used it before shooting I Love Lucy; Morris used it in early morning summer stock arrivals.

sources.summary: Lucy Ball describes getting into makeup, interacting with Vivian Vance, and playing until she accesses the 'enchanted sense of play' to be Lucy.
Morris applies this in summer stock: arriving early, standing under stage lights, looking at the dark audience space, filling with the joy of acting before the show.

demonstrating_quality_directly.name: Demonstrating Character Quality Directly
demonstrating_quality_directly.description: When an audition or callback requires convincing a director or producer of a specific character quality you possess, the actor takes direct, bold physical action in the audition that embodies and demonstrates that quality rather than claiming it verbally.
demonstrating_quality_directly.instrumental_use: Liberates the actor from passive, pleasing audition behavior; channels real energy and conviction into action.
demonstrating_quality_directly.craft_use: Shows rather than tells the director exactly what the actor will bring to the role; creates a memorable and visceral impression.
demonstrating_quality_directly.when_to_use: In callbacks where the decision-maker is skeptical about a specific quality (strength, danger, vulnerability, humor); when words and past credits have been insufficient to change the perception. Requires courage and willingness to lose the job.

sources.summary: Morris coaches Linda Cristal to physically demolish the producer's office to demonstrate strength when the producer is unconvinced; she gets the role in 'The High Chaparral.'

working_within_costar_reality.name: Working Within a Co-Star's Reality
working_within_costar_reality.description: When a co-actor is maintaining Method reality-continuity off-camera, the less experienced actor recognizes what is happening and works within that reality rather than fighting it — mirroring the relational dynamic in real life to sustain the authenticity of both performances.
working_within_costar_reality.instrumental_use: Prevents the emotional rupture that occurs when an actor breaks character reality by seeking ordinary social contact between takes.
working_within_costar_reality.craft_use: Sustains the relational truth between the characters throughout the production; allows both actors to bring genuine history to their scenes.
working_within_costar_reality.when_to_use: When a co-star is maintaining reality off-camera and the actor does not understand why; when the director cannot or will not explain the co-star's process.

sources.summary: Morris advises David Caruso to keep trying to win De Niro's acceptance, playing within De Niro's reality without breaking it, rather than confronting the apparent coldness.

rapid_script_memorization.name: Rapid Memorization Under Pressure
rapid_script_memorization.description: The actor develops the ability to memorize significant amounts of material in a very short time under production pressure, treating memorization as an active engagement with the material rather than rote repetition.
rapid_script_memorization.instrumental_use: Develops confidence and reliability as a professional; reduces anxiety about the mechanical aspects of the craft.
rapid_script_memorization.craft_use: Allows the actor to be fully present in the scene without the interference of line anxiety.
rapid_script_memorization.when_to_use: Emergency situations on set where the actor receives new or different material with limited preparation time.

sources.summary: Given the wrong script and then the right one with 15 minutes to memorize, Morris does so and shoots the entire scene in one perfect take.

directing_instrumentally.name: Directing Instrumentally
directing_instrumentally.description: As a director or coach, addressing the actor's personal psychological blocks, self-esteem deficits, and inner emotional obstacles rather than only giving technical direction about the scene — treating the actor's instrument as the primary arena of work.
directing_instrumentally.instrumental_use: Frees the actor's expressive capacity by addressing root psychological causes of limitation; elevates self-esteem and sense of entitlement.
directing_instrumentally.craft_use: Allows the actor's newly freed instrument to naturally express the scene's requirements without technical overlay.
directing_instrumentally.when_to_use: When an actor's technical performance is limited by psychological blocks rather than craft ignorance; when an actor is playing 'small' due to damaged self-esteem; when actors are carrying trauma or self-doubt into their work.

sources.summary: Morris directs Harry Davis 'instrumentally,' helping to elevate his self-esteem and sense of entitlement whose damage was causing his acting to be limited.

socratic_coaching_method.name: Socratic Coaching (Question-Based Direction)
socratic_coaching_method.description: The coach or director works with an actor by asking specific questions about the character's intentions, relationships, and what they are communicating — rather than giving direct instruction or demonstrating — allowing the actor to discover clarity through their own answers and then reinvest in the material.
socratic_coaching_method.instrumental_use: Keeps the actor's ownership of the work; prevents the dependence and passivity that comes from receiving external direction. The actor's own answers engage their commitment.
socratic_coaching_method.craft_use: Clarifies objectives, relationships, and subtext through the actor's own articulation; the actor who can answer 'What are you communicating to your father?' clearly will perform it more organically.
socratic_coaching_method.when_to_use: When working with an actor whose performance lacks specificity or connection; when the actor is technically competent but psychologically vague about their objectives. Hopkins used this naturally; Morris describes it as effective.

sources.summary: Hopkins works with Jensen on the monologue by asking questions about what Jensen is communicating; Jensen's answers deepen his connection to the material.

fear_induction_for_terror_scenes.name: Real Stimulus for Terror/Fear Scenes
fear_induction_for_terror_scenes.description: When an actor cannot access genuine fear or terror for a scene through imagination or suggestion alone, the coach identifies a specific personal vulnerability and creates a situation that genuinely threatens or alarums that vulnerability — to demonstrate to the actor what genuine terror feels like in their body, giving them a reference point for the scene.
fear_induction_for_terror_scenes.instrumental_use: Bypasses intellectual attempts to manufacture fear; accesses the actual physiological response of genuine terror.
fear_induction_for_terror_scenes.craft_use: Gives the actor a real somatic reference for an extreme emotional state that they can then work toward organically in the scene.
fear_induction_for_terror_scenes.when_to_use: Only as a last resort when other approaches have failed; requires extraordinary care and immediate reassurance; must be followed by a thorough re-establishment of safety and trust. The technique is ethically fraught and Morris has expressed regret about its application even when it worked.

sources.summary: Morris uses a feigned knife threat to induce genuine terror in Sharon Tate for a film preparation; immediately reassures her afterward; she thanks him and says she knows what to do.

object_bonding_technique.name: Object Bonding (Personal Relationship with Stage Objects)
object_bonding_technique.description: The actor builds a genuine personal history with objects that are significant to their character — selecting them personally, inventing stories about them, sharing those stories repeatedly until a real bond is established — so that their relationship with those objects on stage is organic rather than performed.
object_bonding_technique.instrumental_use: Creates genuine emotional attachment to objects as extensions of the character's inner life; prevents the actor from merely indicating a relationship with props.
object_bonding_technique.craft_use: Fulfills the scene's requirement that the character have an authentic, deep relationship with significant objects (e.g., Laura's glass menagerie); the bond is real, not fabricated on cue.
object_bonding_technique.when_to_use: When a character's relationship with specific objects is central to their psychology or the scene's dramatic action; when the actor must convey deep emotional investment in physical objects. Requires extended preparation time — weeks or months of working with the objects.

sources.summary: Morris takes Erin to Hollywood Boulevard to find her own glass animals without influence; has her invent stories about each figure and share them in rehearsal repeatedly until she genuinely bonds with them.

real_action_parallel_rehearsal.name: Real Action Parallel in Rehearsal
real_action_parallel_rehearsal.description: The director assigns actors actions in rehearsal that literally parallel their character's activities in the play — using real-world conditions and consequences — so that the actor's responses are genuine rather than imagined. The emotional residue of these real actions then becomes available in performance.
real_action_parallel_rehearsal.instrumental_use: Generates real emotional responses (rejection, embarrassment, achievement, connection) that the actor can draw from organically.
real_action_parallel_rehearsal.craft_use: Prepares the actor to fulfil scene obligations (selling subscriptions, recounting youthful stories, declaring ambitions) with genuine felt experience rather than performed behavior.
real_action_parallel_rehearsal.when_to_use: In extended rehearsal processes where time allows for real-world parallels to be created; particularly useful for scenes requiring repeated emotional states that the actor might otherwise manufacture through technical means. Requires an extended rehearsal period.

sources.summary: Joan calls real people to sell nonexistent magazine subscriptions and is genuinely affected when they hang up. Joan shares real courtship stories. Billy Hayes discusses real ambitions. Tom writes real poetry to read to Laura.

period_research_for_character_world.name: Period Research for Character World-Building
period_research_for_character_world.description: The cast and director immerse themselves in the historical, economic, and cultural context of the period in which the play is set — through recordings, books, images, and cultural artifacts — to create a shared understanding of the world the characters inhabit.
period_research_for_character_world.instrumental_use: Creates a shared imaginative world for all cast members, reducing the artificiality of the rehearsal environment.
period_research_for_character_world.craft_use: Grounds the characters' behavior and concerns in a specific historical reality; ensures that references and motivations that arise from the period are genuinely felt rather than theoretically understood.
period_research_for_character_world.when_to_use: When directing period plays or any work set in a time substantially different from the present; most effective when done collectively by the whole cast, not just individually.

sources.summary: For The Glass Menagerie: recordings from the Depression era, Time-Life books about the Spanish revolution and American economy, material on Roosevelt's fireside chats — all to create a shared world.

being_work.name: BEING Work
being_work.description: The practice of finding and expressing total present-moment reality — including all impulses, interruptions, distractions, feelings — without editing, suppression, or intellectual control. The foundational practice of Morris's system.
being_work.instrumental_use: Frees the actor from social obligation, suppression, and imposed behavior. Establishes ground-zero reality from which all creative work can spring. Must be done before any scene work.
being_work.craft_use: Not directly a craft technique but the prerequisite for all craft. Once in a BEING state, actor shifts that state to meet scene obligations through choices and preparations.
being_work.when_to_use: Always, as a way of life. Before scene work. Before auditions. As daily practice.

sources.summary: BEING defined as working technique: to BE you must find out what you feel and express it totally.
BEING is primary and basic foundation.

rounds.name: Rounds
rounds.description: Group exercises in which actors encounter each other with extreme honesty and personal revelation — reluctancy exercises, honesty stream of consciousness, ego reconstruction — designed to break social impositions.
rounds.instrumental_use: Encourages extreme personal encounter. Eliminates social impositional life. Allows actors to experience real moments with each other without concern for imagined consequences.
rounds.craft_use: Builds relating skills, sensitivity to partners, and genuine connection that carries into scene work.
rounds.when_to_use: In class/workshop settings, especially early in training and whenever actors become too polite or disconnected.

sources.summary: Rounds described: Reluctancy Exercises, Honesty Stream of Consciousness, Ego Reconstructions. Required extreme personal encounter.

choice_work.name: Choice Work (Working for a Choice)
choice_work.description: The process by which the actor asks sensory questions and lets the senses answer, committing totally to that process even at the cost of the scene, until the process produces behavioral results.
choice_work.instrumental_use: By committing to the sensory process, the actor avoids intellectualizing and connects to genuine organic response.
choice_work.craft_use: Primary craft tool for meeting scene obligations. Select a choice and use sensory questions to make it real. If behavior does not match scene needs, adjust or replace choice.
choice_work.when_to_use: When there is a gap between current BEING state and the emotional state required by the material.

sources.summary: Working for a choice: ask sensory questions, let senses answer, commit totally even at cost of scene until behavioral fruit results.

vulnerability_preparations.name: Vulnerability Preparations
vulnerability_preparations.description: Exercises that stimulate the actor's ability to be sensitive and affected — to be open to both real and imaginary stimuli. They increase the threshold of availability on a day-to-day and acting level.
vulnerability_preparations.instrumental_use: Makes the actor ready to be affected. Increases sensitivity to real and imaginary stimuli. More vulnerability in life means more affectability in acting.
vulnerability_preparations.craft_use: Can also fulfill emotional obligations in a scene. Must achieve vulnerability before playing an uptight or closed character — must get what is underneath before playing the surface.
vulnerability_preparations.when_to_use: Before any scene work. When the actor is insulated or protected. When the scene requires emotional openness. In daily preparation.

sources.summary: Vulnerability preparations stimulate ability to be sensitive and affected by real and imaginary stimuli.
Must achieve vulnerability before playing an uptight character — must find what is underneath. Vulnerability feels naked and unacty but is essential.

imagination_preparations.name: Preparations for the Imagination
imagination_preparations.description: Exercises designed to stretch and stimulate the actor's imagination, helping them to create imaginary realities and to believe in circumstances that do not exist.
imagination_preparations.instrumental_use: Opens up the actor's capacity to pretend, create, and believe in imaginary circumstances. Essential for sense memory, choice work, and all creative acting.
imagination_preparations.craft_use: Pretend can be used directly as a choice in a scene. Storytelling and fantasy exercises generate material for imaginary choices.
imagination_preparations.when_to_use: Daily, as part of living preparation. Before rehearsals. Whenever imagination feels constrained or dry.

sources.summary: Preparations for imagination: must work on it daily as part of living preparation, stretching belief that anything is imaginatively possible.

observer_wonder_perceive.name: Observe, Wonder and Perceive
observer_wonder_perceive.description: An awareness exercise in which the actor observes what is obviously present, perceives what lies behind the obvious, and wonders about what they cannot know for certain. Done alone or with a partner.
observer_wonder_perceive.instrumental_use: Develops observational skills, sensory awareness, and curiosity. Pulls the actor out of self-involvement and narcissism. Trains the habit of being aware of someone other than yourself.
observer_wonder_perceive.craft_use: Used as a preparation to get related to a partner before a scene or rehearsal. Generates a living relationship through genuine investigation. A good way to start a rehearsal.
observer_wonder_perceive.when_to_use: Daily, as awareness training. Before any scene work with a partner. Anytime the actor is too in their own head.

sources.summary: Observe, Wonder and Perceive II: two-people version. Observe what is obviously there, perceive behind it, wonder about what cannot be known.
Actors are narcissistic — Observe, Wonder and Perceive trains attention on others. Transcends from deliberate decision to becoming part of life.

basic_imaging_with_sense_memory.name: Basic Imaging with Sense Memory Exercise
basic_imaging_with_sense_memory.goal: Learn to create a dimensionally real environment or event by combining inner visualization with systematic sensorial questioning, training all five senses to respond simultaneously.
basic_imaging_with_sense_memory.how_to:
1. Do a relaxation exercise to clear intrusive thoughts.
2. Begin with an inner-eye (eyes-closed) vision of a place or event — sketch in overall elements (trees, lake, mountains, sky).
3. Open eyes and transfer the image to outer vision (image with eyes open).
4. Begin asking sensorial questions directed at specific sense organs:
   - Visual: 'How many colors do I see? How do they blend?'
   - Tactile: 'Where on my face do I feel the wind? What does it feel like?'
   - Olfactory: 'What is the exact odor? Where in my nose do I detect it?'
   - Auditory: 'What is the most noticeable sound? Where does it come from?'
   - Gustatory: 'Can I taste any of the odors? Where in my mouth?'
5. Allow images to run free between sensorial questions — the image itself suggests what to explore next.
6. Intersperse imaging (free flow) with sensorial questions to deepen and dimensionalize the experience.
basic_imaging_with_sense_memory.who_should_do_it: All actors learning the imaging system; beginners to Sense Memory; any actor wanting to deepen the tangibility of their choices.
basic_imaging_with_sense_memory.why_it_works: Sense Memory responses happen in the body's actual sense organs — they create genuine physiological and emotional reactions. Combining with imaging keeps the experience flowing and prevents it from becoming purely analytical.
basic_imaging_with_sense_memory.additional_info: Morris gives two detailed examples: (1) a mountain lake scene (pp. 40-41), (2) a visit to the Metropolitan Museum of Art (pp. 43-44). The museum example shows how imaging can re-create real past events sensorially, producing emotional life appropriate for a scene obligation.

sources.summary: Instructions for Imaging with Sense Memory: sketch image, then ask sensorial questions, respond in specific sense organs.
Mountain lake example: detailed walkthrough of sensorial questions and image flow.
Art museum example: imaging a past event at the Met with full sensory engagement — re-creates real emotional experience.

imaging_sensations_exercise.name: Imaging Sensations Exercise
imaging_sensations_exercise.goal: Train all five senses to function as equal, simultaneous imagers — overcoming visual dominance and building sensory accessibility across all channels.
imaging_sensations_exercise.how_to:
1. Do a relaxation exercise.
2. Choose an image (real or fantasy).
3. Begin with a non-visual sense (smell or sound) to deliberately counter the habit of starting visually.
4. Allow each sense to respond with full emphasis — the same total engagement as you would give the visual sense.
5. Shift emphasis organically from sense to sense: hear the image, smell it, feel it, taste it, see it — in any order.
6. Let all senses blend and respond simultaneously as the image deepens.
7. No sensorial questions — allow each sense to respond fully and continuously.
imaging_sensations_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who are visually dominant in their imaging; anyone training in Morris's system who wants to deepen sensory accessibility.
imaging_sensations_exercise.why_it_works: By giving equal billing to all senses, the actor creates a more complete internal reality. The image becomes a total body experience, not just a mental picture, making it more convincing to the unconscious and more emotionally impacting.
imaging_sensations_exercise.additional_info: Morris's fantasy example (bright-red/orange ground, rainbow-colored forest, speaking creatures) illustrates how even a completely invented image produces real emotional impact when all senses are fully engaged. (Pages 46-49)

sources.summary: Exercise instructions: initially emphasize non-visual senses; let all senses blend as imagers.
Fantasy imaging example: detailed multi-sensory journey through an invented world producing genuine emotional experience.

involuntary_imaging_exercise.name: Involuntary Imaging Exercise (Class Format)
involuntary_imaging_exercise.goal: Access deeper, unconsciously mediated emotional material by working for a choice to stimulate involuntary images, then re-creating those images as a richer substitute approach.
involuntary_imaging_exercise.how_to:
Part 1 (approx. 15 minutes):
1. Identify a specific obligation from a scene (current or past).
2. Select a choice that would stimulate the desired emotional life.
3. Work for the choice using any choice approach.
4. While working, be acutely aware of all involuntary images that arise — no matter how abstract or seemingly unrelated.
5. Take note (mental or written) of as many as possible.

Part 2:
6. Recall the involuntary images from Part 1.
7. Re-create those images using Imaging as a choice approach.
8. Notice how the emotional life from re-creating the involuntary images compares to the original choice.
involuntary_imaging_exercise.who_should_do_it: Intermediate to advanced actors with some imaging experience; those wanting to access deeper emotional material and build a connection with their unconscious.
involuntary_imaging_exercise.why_it_works: The involuntary images are the unconscious's own translation of the conscious choice — connected to the obligation in the unconscious's pictorial language, and re-creating them produces richer, less intellectually filtered emotional life.
involuntary_imaging_exercise.additional_info: Morris reports that in class, nearly all actors found the involuntary images produced more impacting emotional life than their original choices, even when the images seemed abstractly unrelated to the scene material.

sources.summary: Class exercise format: work for choice, note involuntary images, re-create those images, compare results.
Finding: nearly all actors got richer emotional life from the involuntary images than from direct choices.

symbolic_imaging_journal.name: Symbolic Imaging Journal Practice
symbolic_imaging_journal.goal: Build a personal library of symbolic images that can serve as emotional triggers — shortcutting the process of accessing specific feeling states during scene work.
symbolic_imaging_journal.how_to:
1. Keep a dedicated journal for symbolic images (separate from choice journal and dream journal).
2. Record all symbolic images encountered — in dreams, sleep-wake states, or waking involuntary imaging.
3. Underline or highlight symbolic objects in each dream record.
4. Note what you felt during the symbol encounter and upon reflection.
5. Deliberately re-create each symbolic image (using Sense Memory or Imaging) to explore its emotional impact.
6. Track which symbols consistently produce specific emotional states.
7. Build a cross-reference of: symbol → emotional state → potential scene obligation it could fulfill.
symbolic_imaging_journal.who_should_do_it: All actors; especially those doing dream work and unconscious programming.
symbolic_imaging_journal.why_it_works: Symbols from the unconscious carry condensed emotional meaning — re-creating them can instantly unlock associated feeling states without the longer process of building a full Sense Memory or narrative choice.
symbolic_imaging_journal.additional_info: Morris recommends multiple journals: daily experience/sensory journal, choice journal, dream journal, and symbolic imaging journal.

sources.summary: Instructions for symbolic imaging journal: record symbols from dreams, note feelings, re-create to test emotional impact, cross-reference with potential choices.

hatful_of_rain_involuntary_imaging_examp.name: Involuntary Imaging — Hatful of Rain Scene Example
hatful_of_rain_involuntary_imaging_examp.goal: Illustrate how an actor can use involuntary images stimulated by Sense Memory work to access deep, unconsciously rooted emotional life for a scene requiring addiction/withdrawal experience.
hatful_of_rain_involuntary_imaging_examp.how_to:
1. Identify the obligation (in this case: physical symptoms and desperation of withdrawal from heroin; also grief, helplessness, and relational ambivalence).
2. Work sensorially to create the physical feelings of withdrawal.
3. While doing so, allow and note the involuntary images that arise (e.g., corridors of a rehab center, frosted glass, faces without features, metallic taste).
4. Re-create those involuntary images through Imaging, deepening the emotional connection to the scene's obligations.
5. Observe how the involuntary images access a richer, more dimensional emotional life than the physical symptoms alone.
hatful_of_rain_involuntary_imaging_examp.who_should_do_it: Actors approaching scenes involving addiction, trauma, or extreme emotional states who have relevant but different personal experience.
hatful_of_rain_involuntary_imaging_examp.why_it_works: The actor's prior personal experience with addiction (even a different substance) triggered unconscious memory-images that mapped onto the character's experience more fully than conscious reconstruction of physical symptoms alone.
hatful_of_rain_involuntary_imaging_examp.additional_info: Scene: Hatful of Rain — Johnny and Polo, brothers; Johnny is a heroin addict desperate for money for a fix; Polo refuses. Involuntary images included: white and gray corridors, antiseptic smell, long corridor with closed doors, frosted wire-mesh window, faces without features, metallic taste, prickling body sensations.

sources.summary: Scene description: Hatful of Rain, Johnny and Polo; addiction/withdrawal obligation identified.
Actor Jerry's involuntary images from rehab center memory: detailed list of fragmentary images.
By re-creating these involuntary images, Jerry accessed stronger helplessness, frustration, guilt — richer than physical symptoms alone.

fragmented_imaging_vulnerability.name: Fragmented Imaging for Vulnerability
fragmented_imaging_vulnerability.goal: Rapidly elevate the actor's vulnerability level using a themed, creative sequence of fragmented images drawn from personal life.
fragmented_imaging_vulnerability.how_to:
1. Choose a theme that you know will evoke vulnerability (loss, love, childhood, mortality).
2. Begin with a single evocative image related to that theme.
3. Allow rapid, non-sequential images to flow from that starting point, not needing to connect.
4. Creatively steer the images toward the theme, using Selective Emphasis on the most impacting ones.
5. Comment on images as they arise; include ALL five senses.
6. Allow emotional responses as they come — do not suppress them.
7. Continue until vulnerability level is elevated and emotional flow begins.
fragmented_imaging_vulnerability.who_should_do_it: Any actor needing to access vulnerability quickly; useful before any emotionally demanding scene.
fragmented_imaging_vulnerability.why_it_works: The fragmented, non-sequential structure keeps the intellect from editing; the actor is surprised by images that cut to deep feeling without the need to construct a coherent narrative.
fragmented_imaging_vulnerability.additional_info: Morris's example uses his mother's death, his father eating, and childhood memories — producing tears while typing. The images collectively produced loss, love, sadness, and vulnerability.

sources.summary: Fragmented Imaging vulnerability exercise example: images of mother's death, family memories, loved ones produce strong vulnerability.

free_association_imaging_exercise.name: Free-Association Imaging Exercise (Taped)
free_association_imaging_exercise.goal: Liberate unconscious material, build self-knowledge, elevate affectability and vulnerability as an involvement preparation, and discover new choices through completely undirected imaging.
free_association_imaging_exercise.how_to:
1. Lie down or sit comfortably; use a tape recorder if possible to capture images without breaking the flow.
2. Begin deep breathing; attempt to clear the mind.
3. When images arise, verbalize them if using a tape recorder; otherwise allow them silently.
4. Do NOT steer, edit, or lead the images. Accept whatever comes, no matter how absurd or unrelated.
5. When thoughts or commentary arise, simply note them and return to the image flow.
6. Continue for 20-40 minutes or until the flow naturally concludes.
7. After completing: listen to or review the images; note emotional responses, patterns, and insights.
8. Add to daily journal.
free_association_imaging_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors as a daily practice; especially valuable during rehearsal for a specific role.
free_association_imaging_exercise.why_it_works: Because the actor does not steer, unconscious material surfaces that would be blocked by goal-directed imaging. The emotional life produced goes beyond what conscious choices access.
free_association_imaging_exercise.additional_info: Morris's detailed taped example (pp. 76-79) demonstrates how the exercise spontaneously journeyed through childhood, Boy Scout days, work memories, and reflections on mortality — producing deep vulnerability, nostalgia, and emotional readiness. He reports his dreams that night reflected the imaging content.

sources.summary: Morris's taped Free-Association Imaging example: spontaneous journey through life, producing vulnerability and emotional readiness.
After the exercise Morris felt relaxed, nostalgic, contemplative, and vulnerable — ready to act.
The exercise yielded insights about childhood discrimination, love for his father, and feelings about mortality. Dreams that night reflected the content.

fantasy_imaging_before_performance.name: Fantasy Imaging to Elevate State Before Performance
fantasy_imaging_before_performance.goal: Rapidly transform a depressed or stressed pre-performance state into heightened emotional aliveness and readiness to act using a multi-sensory fantasy.
fantasy_imaging_before_performance.how_to:
1. Acknowledge the depressed or stressed state honestly.
2. Search for a topic that has always been pleasurably memorable — something that speeds up your heartbeat.
3. Instead of using a real experience, elect to explore a lifelong fantasy related to that topic.
4. Create the fantasy as a Story Image with full sensory engagement — all five senses from the beginning.
5. Allow the story to develop organically; follow the fantasy wherever it leads.
6. Let emotional responses surface and amplify — do not contain them.
7. Continue until the state has fully shifted and emotional energy is elevated.
fantasy_imaging_before_performance.who_should_do_it: Any actor who arrives at a performance in a depressed or stressed state and needs a rapid state change.
fantasy_imaging_before_performance.why_it_works: Fantasy bypasses the current negative state by hooking into the child's sense of play and imagination. The multi-sensory engagement makes the fantasy real enough to produce genuine physiological and emotional responses.
fantasy_imaging_before_performance.additional_info: Morris's clown example: an actor who loves the circus uses the fantasy of being a clown to transform depression into elation, culminating in a wave of love and acceptance from the imagined audience.

sources.summary: Process: identify depressed state, find pleasurably memorable topic, create fantasy story image.
Clown example: full sensory Story Image produces transformation from depression to joy and love.

benevolent_audience_imaging.name: Benevolent Audience Fragmented Imaging (On-the-Spot Fear Antidote)
benevolent_audience_imaging.goal: Rapidly reduce fear and anxiety immediately before or during performance by imaging the audience and environment as supportive and accepting.
benevolent_audience_imaging.how_to:
1. First, acknowledge the fear aloud (in class or rehearsal) or silently (in performance): 'I am shaking...I am afraid...'
2. Look for real evidence of benevolence in the actual environment (a smiling face, a warm eye) — relate to those available realities.
3. Using Selective Emphasis, amplify and enhance the real benevolence you find.
4. Then, using Fragmented Imaging, begin to image beyond what really exists: 'All the faces look friendly and supportive...the room looks brighter and more colorful...'
5. Build a complete image of being in an accepting, enthusiastic environment.
6. Include physical sensations of confidence and excitement.
benevolent_audience_imaging.who_should_do_it: Any actor suffering from performance anxiety, fear of auditions, or on-the-spot instrumental blocks.
benevolent_audience_imaging.why_it_works: Starting from real available benevolent stimuli makes the imaging grounded and believable; the actor then expands sensorially into imaging beyond reality, creating genuine confidence through the mind-body connection.
benevolent_audience_imaging.additional_info: This is distinguished from the long-term fear elimination process — it is a temporary, on-the-spot antidote. For permanent fear elimination, the two-step imaging process (re-experience and change) must be done daily outside of performance.

sources.summary: On-the-spot: acknowledge fear, find real benevolence available, selectively emphasize it, then image beyond reality into full acceptance.

imaging_environments_exercise.name: Imaging Environments Exercise (Relaxation)
imaging_environments_exercise.goal: Achieve a state of relaxation, involvement, and well-being through the creation of a beautiful or inspiring environment — real or imagined — at the start of a class, rehearsal, or before performance.
imaging_environments_exercise.how_to:
1. Eyes closed or open; begin with a non-visual sense if possible (olfactory is a good start).
2. Begin imaging a place that has previously produced relaxation and well-being (ocean, forest, desert, mountains, home).
3. Expand through all five senses: smell, tactile, auditory, visual, gustatory.
4. Respond sensorially — NOT intellectually — in the specific sense organ.
5. Let the image deepen and expand organically.
6. Variations: image being buoyant in water (floating weightlessly in a dark tank); image rocking (while physically rocking to start the movement).
7. Continue until relaxation, involvement, and well-being are achieved.
imaging_environments_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors; particularly useful at the start of class or before performance when tension is present.
imaging_environments_exercise.why_it_works: Any imaging that involves the actor takes him off the spot of self-consciousness. The environment image also directly produces physiological relaxation through the mind-body connection.
imaging_environments_exercise.additional_info: The environment selected should be personally meaningful and proven to produce well-being — Morris prefers natural environments. The rocking variant uses physical movement as an entry point; the movement can gradually be reduced as the image takes over.

sources.summary: Imaging Environments: create real or imagined place of well-being; all senses; used before class/rehearsal/performance.
Ocean example; rocking and floating variants detailed.

shyness_antidote_imaging.name: Shyness Antidote: Public Speaking Fantasy Imaging
shyness_antidote_imaging.goal: Progressively eliminate shyness and stage fright through Fantasy Story Imaging of successful, passionate public communication.
shyness_antidote_imaging.how_to:
1. Choose a public speaking scenario (starting small if needed: one person, then groups).
2. Using Fantasy Story Imaging, create: standing at a podium before thousands of people; silence and expectation in the air.
3. Begin to image speaking — hear your own voice filled with conviction and passion; feel your body shaking with excitement.
4. Image the audience's response: thunderous applause, rising to their feet, acceptance and validation.
5. Feel the sense of power, importance, and rightful centrality.
6. Progress to increasingly large audiences and more personal material.
7. Work up gradually: start with imaging one person, then small groups, then large groups.
8. Repeat daily.
shyness_antidote_imaging.who_should_do_it: Actors with shyness, stage fright, or difficulty opening up in front of others.
shyness_antidote_imaging.why_it_works: The unconscious that created the shyness through repeated negative experiences is reprogrammed through repeated positive imaging. Specificity and daily repetition are essential for the antidote to take hold.
shyness_antidote_imaging.additional_info: If the full scenario is too overwhelming to start, begin with imagining a satisfactory telephone conversation, then progress to in-person scenarios.

sources.summary: Shyness antidote: Fantasy Story Image of speaking passionately to thousands with thunderous response; start small and work up.

low_self_esteem_imaging.name: Low Self-Esteem Imaging
low_self_esteem_imaging.goal: Build a new, positive, emotionally grounded self-image by creating progressive story images of appreciation, admiration, and confident self-expression.
low_self_esteem_imaging.how_to:
1. Begin by imaging yourself as you truly feel you are inside — the best version of yourself physically, mentally, and emotionally.
2. Image yourself in an attitude of attractive power: strong, bright, articulate, creative.
3. Optionally, re-create events that contributed to the low self-esteem, then progressively modify those images.
4. Bring others into the image; allow yourself to feel you deserve their admiration.
5. Image yourself in situations where you would normally feel inadequate — now image yourself thriving.
6. Repeat daily, creating variations each time.
7. Keep a journal of which specific images have the greatest unconscious impact.
low_self_esteem_imaging.who_should_do_it: Actors with fragile egos, low self-esteem, or persistent feelings of inadequacy.
low_self_esteem_imaging.why_it_works: The self-images planted in the unconscious during formative years replay automatically; replacing them with new images through daily imaging progressively builds a new unconscious self-structure.
low_self_esteem_imaging.additional_info: Morris notes: 'Inside that heavy young girl is a beautiful and thin young woman!' — the imaging reveals and reinforces the inner self that already exists.

sources.summary: Low self-esteem imaging: build from self-appreciation, add attractive power and articulate confidence, bring in others, repeat daily.

emotional_liberation_imaging.name: Emotional Liberation Imaging (Anger/Conflict)
emotional_liberation_imaging.goal: Free the actor from inhibitions around expressing extreme emotions (anger, rage, conflict, vulnerability, grief) by creating Story Images that express the repressed emotion fully and successfully.
emotional_liberation_imaging.how_to:
1. Identify the specific emotional block (e.g., fear of anger, conflict phobia, inability to express grief).
2. Using Story Imaging with Sense Memory, create the environment where the emotion was originally suppressed — the specific place, the people involved, the sensory details.
3. Begin to re-experience the original scene sensorially.
4. Now, change the story: express the suppressed emotion fully — scream at the parent, stand up to the bully, confront the intimidator.
5. Image the changed outcome — the other person's response of shame, understanding, or capitulation; your own sense of power and righteousness.
6. Create an image of resolution and possibly connection after the confrontation.
7. Repeat daily across different confrontation scenarios and with different people from your life.
emotional_liberation_imaging.who_should_do_it: Actors who find specific emotional expressions blocked or uncomfortable; especially those with conflict phobia or unexpressed anger.
emotional_liberation_imaging.why_it_works: The inhibition is rooted in specific memories and unconscious structures built around suppressed experience. By sensorially re-creating the original scenario and changing the outcome, the actor accesses and reprograms the unconscious structure that maintains the inhibition.
emotional_liberation_imaging.additional_info: Morris's example (pp. 116-117): angry child confronting unreasonable father in Story Imaging; the father is moved to shame and love; the outcome is resolution. Note: the image should be structured so all senses respond to each element.

sources.summary: Emotional liberation imaging example: Story Image of righteously confronting father; outcome shifted to understanding and embrace.
Also applicable to conflict phobia: image yourself powerful and unintimidated; change the unconscious structure through repetition.

night_mother_relationship_preparation.name: Relationship Preparation via Endowment + Guided Imaging (Night Mother Example)
night_mother_relationship_preparation.goal: Build a deeply authentic mother-daughter relationship for a scene using a combination of real-life experiences, endowment imaging, guided imaging, and shared imaging.
night_mother_relationship_preparation.how_to:
Step 1 — Establish available realities: Note real similarities between the actors and the characters.
Step 2 — Relate as yourselves: Express moment-to-moment real feelings to each other; build a foundation of truth.
Step 3 — Live together (or spend extended time together): Share real-life experiences, habits, conversations.
Step 4 — Shared Imaging: Share personal experiences from your own life that parallel the play's circumstances.
Step 5 — Endowment imaging: Each actress talks to the other while imaging her own real mother/daughter in the other's place. Blend the image with the actual responses received.
Step 6 — Guided Imaging: Facilitate each other through personal experiences that closely parallel scenes in the play.
Step 7 — Simultaneous Guided Imaging: Both select parallel experiences and mutually facilitate each other through them.
Step 8 — Parallel Imaging (individual): Each actress images herself having the character's life and experiences.
night_mother_relationship_preparation.who_should_do_it: Actors in two-person plays with deep relationship obligations; applicable to any scene requiring intimate, historically grounded relationships.
night_mother_relationship_preparation.why_it_works: Multiple layers of real experience, imaging parallels, and guided exploration cumulatively build an unconscious relationship that feels lived-in — not performed.
night_mother_relationship_preparation.additional_info: This was Morris's preparation for 'night, Mother (Marsha Norman). The play has no intermission and only two characters; the relationship is everything. Morris also had the actresses literally live together during rehearsal.

sources.summary: Morris's preparation process for 'night, Mother: multiple relationship techniques layered.
Actresses identify available realities and personal parallels; Shared Imaging to build familiarity.
Endowment imaging: talk to partner while imaging real mother/daughter; blend images with real responses.

object_imaging_exercise.name: Object Imaging Exercise
object_imaging_exercise.goal: Stretch and elevate the imagination by creating a complete sensory-rich story or life for an ordinary room object, developing the capacity for innovative, multi-sensory imaginative thinking.
object_imaging_exercise.how_to:
Part A — Story Object Imaging:
1. Select any object in the room.
2. Look at it; engage all five senses with it.
3. Begin imagining: Where was it before it was here? What environments did it inhabit? What would it say if it could speak? What does it prefer? What are its relationships to other objects in the room?
4. Develop a complete story. Let the imagination run free.

Part B — Impulsive Object Imaging:
5. Look at the same or another object.
6. Allow whatever image arises to appear — do not steer or edit.
7. Move from object to object, spending just moments on each.
8. Welcome bizarre, illogical, or outrageous images — these are signs of success.
9. Engage all five senses with each image even if it is a fleeting fragment.
object_imaging_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors; especially those who are overly logical or who struggle to access their imagination spontaneously.
object_imaging_exercise.why_it_works: When logic is excluded, the imagination begins to tap into the unconscious — producing unexpected, rich, and emotionally resonant images. The exercise also trains all five senses to function as imagers.
object_imaging_exercise.additional_info: Morris's example: lamp imaging — what did it look like as a baby lamp? Does it have parents? Painting imaging — where is each person in a Paris street scene going? What are they saying? This works imagination muscles the way physical exercise works muscles.

sources.summary: Object Imaging: story version and impulsive version; all five senses; bizarre images indicate unconscious connection.
Examples with lamp and Paris street painting; no limitations; open the doors to imagination.

emotional_transitions_exercise.name: Emotional Transitions via Fragmented Imaging
emotional_transitions_exercise.goal: Practice moving between distinct emotional states within a scene using rapid, contrasting Fragmented Images, without intellectual effort or words.
emotional_transitions_exercise.how_to: 1. Identify the two emotional states the scene requires (e.g., joyful anticipation → shocked grief). 2. Before the scene, use Fragmented Imaging to build the first state:    image sensory details that evoke the feeling (sounds, smells, visual details of a person or place). 3. At the moment of emotional shift in the scene, immediately shift internal images to    contrasting fragments (e.g., from sparkle in eyes → sad eyes, stooped shoulders, gray skin, fear). 4. Allow no words—only rapid sensory flashes. Let the contrast drive the new emotional response. 5. Repeat the scene, refining which specific fragments produce the sharpest emotional pivot.
emotional_transitions_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors who struggle with forced or mechanical emotional transitions in scenes.
emotional_transitions_exercise.why_it_works: Contrasting sensory images activate the autonomic nervous system more directly than cognitive or verbal instructions. The rapid shift in internal imagery produces an organic emotional shift because the instrument responds to the new sensory reality before the conscious mind can interfere.
emotional_transitions_exercise.additional_info: This is an extension of Fragmented Imaging, applied specifically to the challenge of transitions. It works in tandem with the established preparation imaging: if the pre-scene preparation Fragmented Imaging was successful, the transitional imaging will be more powerful by contrast.

character_background_bluebeard_story_ima.name: Character Background Story Imaging: Love from a Stranger (Bluebeard)
character_background_bluebeard_story_ima.goal: Demonstrate how to build the psychological history of a morally extreme character (serial killer) through a series of Story Imaging episodes, mixing real parallels with fantasy.
character_background_bluebeard_story_ima.how_to: 1. Thoroughly read the script; note all facts about the character's history (however sparse). 2. Decide whether to use total fantasy or Believability (real + fantasy). Recommended: Believability. 3. Begin the first imaging episode at age five: 'I'm in my bedroom...I hear my parents arguing...'    Use all five senses: peeling wallpaper, smell of the room, sounds through the wall, etc. 4. Let a significant emotional event occur in the image (mother's drunken rage, physical strike).    Experience it with full sensory specificity. 5. End the episode, then move to the next developmental period (school years, adolescence, etc.). 6. Each episode should be infinitely more elaborate than indicated—all senses specifically engaged. 7. The actor uses real connections from their own life (e.g., actually was poor, felt unwanted)    mixed with invented events consistent with the character profile.
character_background_bluebeard_story_ima.who_should_do_it: Actors playing characters with extreme or alien psychology—villains, severely traumatized individuals, moral outcasts—where personal parallel experience is insufficient.
character_background_bluebeard_story_ima.why_it_works: By constructing a coherent narrative of the character's formative experiences, the actor creates an emotional logic for all of the character's behavior in the play. The mix of real and invented material triggers unconscious responses and makes the history feel lived.
character_background_bluebeard_story_ima.additional_info: The story imaging must produce the specific emotional underpinnings the character needs: in this case, humiliation, rejection, and hatred of women as symbols of the mother. Multiple episodes (5-10+) should be created in chronological order.

biff_death_of_salesman_character_relatio.name: Character Relationship Imaging: Death of a Salesman (Biff and Willy)
biff_death_of_salesman_character_relatio.goal: Build the complex emotional history of a character relationship using Fragmented Imaging + Imaging Sensations across multiple selectively emphasized life episodes.
biff_death_of_salesman_character_relatio.how_to: 1. Identify the most complex relationship in the play (Biff → Willy). 2. Select a personal choice: your own father (or equivalent figure) as the parallel. 3. Start with Available Stimulus: look for any similarities between your scene partner    and your father (face, mannerisms, voice, rhythm). 4. Begin Fragmented Imaging of selectively emphasized episodes from childhood to present:    - Baseball field (age 6): father's anger, tobacco breath, face in yours.    - Family dinner table (age 10): father boasting, looking at you disapprovingly.    - Graduation ceremony: father's arm around you, public pride and pressure. 5. Use Imaging Sensations as the process within each episode: all five senses simultaneously. 6. Cover 10-15 specific events; move to next when current feels complete. 7. Optionally supplement with Story Imaging or Fantasy Imaging of additional events.
biff_death_of_salesman_character_relatio.who_should_do_it: Actors playing characters in deeply complex, ambivalent, or historically layered relationships. Especially useful when the written relationship has years of history prior to the play's action.
biff_death_of_salesman_character_relatio.why_it_works: Selectively emphasizing experiences that mirror the script's relationship dynamics creates an authentic emotional history the actor can draw on throughout the run. Building this across multiple episodes (not just one memory) produces the complexity and ambivalence of a real long-term relationship.
biff_death_of_salesman_character_relatio.additional_info: The Available Stimulus exploration of the scene partner simultaneously helps establish the Endowment foundation—if the actor later uses Endowments, the preliminary similarities work is already done.

affective_memory_imaging_exercise.name: Affective Memory via Imaging (Court of Last Resort example)
affective_memory_imaging_exercise.goal: Re-create a specific traumatic or emotionally impactful past experience using Story Imaging + Imaging Sensations in order to access and reproduce the original emotions during a scene.
affective_memory_imaging_exercise.how_to: 1. Select a personal experience with the emotional quality needed by the scene. 2. Begin with sensory questions in the present tense, working chronologically    from the moment you arrived at the location (not at the emotional peak):    'I'm getting out of my car...What do I see?...What is the temperature?...' 3. Address every sensory detail: people's faces, sounds, smells, textures,    your own physiological state (tension, perspiration, heart rate). 4. As all senses begin responding simultaneously, transition from questions to    Imaging Sensations (let images flow: 'Smell coffee...feel the cold sponge...'). 5. Continue chronologically through the entire experience to its emotional conclusion. 6. Repeat the process multiple times.
