Wonder: encourage curious questions about the other person—personal life, thoughts, desires. Go back and forth between observing, perceiving, and wondering. Can be done silently (as inner monologue) or out loud with the other actor.
wonder_perceive_observe_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any pair of actors before scene work; especially when relationship feels artificial or curiosity about the partner is low.
wonder_perceive_observe_exercise.why_it_works: Genuine curiosity creates real involvement; can be run silently throughout an entire scene without anyone knowing. Establishes the foundation of authentic relationship.
wonder_perceive_observe_exercise.additional_info: Doing it silently sometimes excites more real curiosity than doing it out loud with the partner.

sources.summary: Three-part exercise: observe, perceive, wonder about the other actor.
Extended example of perceiving the other actor.

sense_memory_impacting_objects_exercise.name: Sense Memory with Emotionally Impacting Objects
sense_memory_impacting_objects_exercise.goal: Elevate emotional state to a desired level by sensorially recreating a meaningful object or person.
sense_memory_impacting_objects_exercise.how_to: Choose an emotionally impacting object (person, pet, photograph, letter, music, sound, place). Ask sensory questions about the object across all relevant senses—visual, tactile, olfactory, auditory, taste. Respond sensorially to each question. Continue through multiple senses. As emotional responses arise naturally during the sensory creation, encourage and express them. Do this before a scene or silently while waiting on set.
sense_memory_impacting_objects_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any actor needing emotional preparation before a scene; especially useful when silence is required.
sense_memory_impacting_objects_exercise.why_it_works: Sense Memory can be done silently without calling attention to itself. The sensory recreation triggers authentic emotional associations.
sense_memory_impacting_objects_exercise.additional_info: Can be done while waiting for a crew setup or while on stage before the curtain rises. The more expertise with Sense Memory, the faster the results.

sources.summary: Sense Memory with impacting objects is silent, unobtrusive; can be done while waiting on set.
Detailed example: creating father in chair using visual, tactile, olfactory questions.

abandonment_exercise.name: Abandonment
abandonment_exercise.goal: Purge tension instrumentally or build a foundation of volatile emotional life for a character.
abandonment_exercise.how_to: Lie on the floor. Have an all-out tantrum—move body in all directions with no physical interference or leadership. Scream and make huge uninterrupted vocal expurgations simultaneously. Instrumental use: continue until tension is purged. Craft use: choose specific content for the tantrum that relates to the emotional state needed; continue until the desired emotional state is stimulated.
abandonment_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who carry significant physical tension; actors playing highly volatile, irrational characters.
abandonment_exercise.why_it_works: The unrestricted physical and vocal expression purges conglomerated blocked emotion and stimulates the free flow of impulses.
abandonment_exercise.additional_info: Do it however long it takes. For craft use, must have specific goal: what emotional state to stimulate and what content to use.

sources.summary: Abandonment: all-out tantrum on floor with body and voice; dual instrumental/craft use.

go_crazy_exercise.name: Go Crazy
go_crazy_exercise.goal: Free the actor from restrictions, stimulate unbroken impulse flow, and build a foundation of flamboyant, colorful behavior.
go_crazy_exercise.how_to: Start with large commitment. Go from moment to moment with every impulse. Make sounds, say words, jump around, roll on the ground. Encourage all outrageous thoughts and impulses toward expression. Relate to things and people in the room—mix external and internal stimuli. Go from serious to absurd rapidly. Continue for 2-5 minutes or until on a good roll.
go_crazy_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who feel restricted or inhibited; actors preparing for free-flowing, expressive, or uninhibited characters.
go_crazy_exercise.why_it_works: The unstructured, exaggerated nature frees all restrictions and creates an unbroken impulse flow that carries into subsequent work.
go_crazy_exercise.additional_info: Must be large and exaggerated. The exercise doesn't have to make any sense.

sources.summary: Go Crazy: all-out exaggerated impulsive expression; 2-5 minutes; frees restrictions and creates unbroken impulse flow.

two_person_validations_exercise.name: Two-Person Validations
two_person_validations_exercise.goal: Build mutual positive regard and ego support between scene partners before rehearsing or performing together.
two_person_validations_exercise.how_to: Two actors sit together (or do it while making up or getting dressed). Conversationally share honest positive observations about each other. Selectively emphasize good things—not forced or structured. Be authentic; don't exaggerate or make things up. Continue for as long as needed.
two_person_validations_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any scene partners before a rehearsal or performance; especially useful when the relationship has become distant, competitive, or fraught.
two_person_validations_exercise.why_it_works: Based on truth with selective emphasis; genuine positive regard is stimulated by authentic positive observation. Works even better when observations relate to the material.
two_person_validations_exercise.additional_info: Can be done while making up or getting dressed—does not require special time allocation.

sources.summary: Two-Person Validations: honest positive observations; based on truth; conversational; do as long as needed.

feedback_exercise.name: Feedback
feedback_exercise.goal: To help the actor understand themselves better through direct, specific feedback from others, and to practice asking for response and reaching out to people.
feedback_exercise.how_to: Two actors sit together and ask each other important questions conversationally. Questions should relate to genuinely meaningful things: first impressions, what you find interesting about the other person, what you get from them in the work, what you wish were different. If courageous, go after the nucleus of your curiosity. The exercise can continue for as long as needed.
feedback_exercise.who_should_do_it: Suitable for rehearsal groups as well as classes — it can flush out negative mind-readings that impede creative work (e.g., actor who thinks director hates him).
feedback_exercise.why_it_works: Encourages reaching out to people and asking for response. Self-regulatory — you only participate if you want to. Flushes out negative projections and false assumptions about what others think.
feedback_exercise.additional_info: Don't waste time asking for unimportant feedback. Courage to go to the nucleus of curiosity determines the quality of the relationship built.

sources.summary: Feedback: conversational exchange of important questions; extended example shows depth and honesty possible.
Feedback exercise: two ways — mind-reading check and specific feedback request. Encourages reaching out to people.
Good for rehearsal groups — flushes out negative mind-readings that impede creative work. Self-regulatory.

parallel_improvising_exercise.name: Parallel Improvising
parallel_improvising_exercise.goal: Find authentic personal connection to material's emotional content by improvising a personally parallel situation.
parallel_improvising_exercise.how_to: Identify the emotional elements of the scene. Choose a parallel circumstance from your own life with similar emotional content. The parallel doesn't have to involve the same person as the scene (e.g., don't have to use your mother—use anyone with a similar emotional relationship). Improvise the parallel situation with complete freedom and irreverence, going wherever it leads. Include all inner monologue impulses during the improvisation. After the improvisation, return to the written scene with the emotional connection established.
parallel_improvising_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who feel disconnected from the emotional content of a scene; actors who can't find personal relevance in the material.
parallel_improvising_exercise.why_it_works: Personal parallels stimulate authentic emotional responses that can then be carried into the written text.
parallel_improvising_exercise.additional_info: Encourage total freedom to go wherever the improvisation takes you; complete irreverence to the written material.

sources.summary: Parallel improvising: personal parallel to scene's emotional elements; example from Glass Menagerie.

choice_hunt_exercise.name: Choice Hunt
choice_hunt_exercise.goal: Discover meaningful personal experiences from one's history that have strong emotional potential for use as choices.
choice_hunt_exercise.how_to: Lie down on your back in solitude with eyes closed. Go back in time to meaningful and emotional events: graduations, holidays, weddings, picnics, family events, significant encounters. Allow memories to surface naturally. Encourage the expression of all emotional impulses stimulated by re-creating the experiences. With regular practice, you will begin to remember things spontaneously even when not doing the exercise.
choice_hunt_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who feel their choice repertoire is limited; actors who have difficulty finding personally relevant choices for obligations.
choice_hunt_exercise.why_it_works: Memory releases its secrets gradually with regular practice; generates surprising impulses and emotional responses to experiences not consciously remembered.
choice_hunt_exercise.additional_info: Regular practice causes memory to release spontaneously even outside the exercise.

sources.summary: Choice Hunt: lying down in solitude; revisit meaningful events; surprising impulses generated; memory begins releasing spontaneously.

sense_memory_exercise_detailed.name: Sense Memory (Full Process)
sense_memory_exercise_detailed.goal: Create an absent object, person, or place so fully that the actor can actually see, hear, smell, feel, and taste it—making it real enough to stimulate authentic emotional responses.
sense_memory_exercise_detailed.how_to: Begin with the first sensory question directed to a specific part of one specific sense. For example (visual): 'What color are the walls of this place? How does the light affect the color?' Respond through the sense—actually attempt to see the answer, not just think it. Ask many questions per sense area (can be 50+). Leave and return to a sense as needed. Work all five senses: visual, tactile, olfactory, auditory, gustatory. Go back and forth between senses while sustaining previously created sensory impressions. The process continues during scene work—between lines and while listening to other actor.
sense_memory_exercise_detailed.who_should_do_it: All actors as a daily practice; anyone using Sense Memory as a choice approach in scene work.
sense_memory_exercise_detailed.why_it_works: The senses can be trained through daily practice to respond to absent objects as if present. Not an intellectual but a response process.
sense_memory_exercise_detailed.additional_info: Daily practice is essential—the ability to truly see/feel/smell absent objects comes with sustained practice over time, not immediately.

sources.summary: Sense Memory as choice approach; senses trained to respond to absent objects.
Detailed process with example questions.
Further example with olfactory and visual questions.
Process continues during scene; actor still hears and is affected by other actor.

available_stimulus_sweep_exercise_craft.name: Available Stimulus Exploration (Craft Use)
available_stimulus_sweep_exercise_craft.goal: Identify which elements in the actual environment can be used to fulfill scene obligations before resorting to other choice approaches.
available_stimulus_sweep_exercise_craft.how_to: Go through a systematic checklist of questions: How am I affected by the whole place? As I scan the environment, what affects or changes my emotional point of view? Are there sounds or odors that mean anything to me? What about the furnishings, colors, arrangement, light? How do I feel about individual people collectively? Individually? Do any lines or words in the play mean something to me? Does anything in the play, the actors, or the place remind me of something in my life? Use Inner Monologue to intensify whatever Available Stimulus affects you.
available_stimulus_sweep_exercise_craft.who_should_do_it: Any actor approaching a scene, before selecting other choice approaches.
available_stimulus_sweep_exercise_craft.why_it_works: Available Stimulus is the most efficiently accessible approach—when it works, it requires the least additional craft work. What is already there and real doesn't need to be created.
available_stimulus_sweep_exercise_craft.additional_info: Checklist can be expanded as the actor works with the approach. Sometimes a specific element needs Creative Manipulation to be fully useful.

sources.summary: Systematic checklist for Available Stimulus exploration.
Creative Manipulation of available elements to make them serve the scene.

believability_improvisation_exercise.name: Believability Improvisation
believability_improvisation_exercise.goal: Create a fabric of total reality between two actors by structuring a shared improvisation that mixes truth and appropriately chosen untruth.
believability_improvisation_exercise.how_to: Both actors identify the obligations and conflict elements of the scene. Begin an improvisation—possibly in a parallel circumstance—starting with actual truths from both actors' lives. Selectively emphasize the truths most relevant to the scene's needs. Progressively add untrue elements that serve the scene's obligations. Mix truth and untruth deftly until they become indistinguishable. Continue until both actors have created the emotional reality the scene requires.
believability_improvisation_exercise.who_should_do_it: Scene partners preparing for a scene where the actual relationship between them doesn't yet have the emotional depth required by the material.
believability_improvisation_exercise.why_it_works: When truth and untruth are skillfully mixed, the actors cannot distinguish between them—creating authentic emotional engagement with fictional circumstances.
believability_improvisation_exercise.additional_info: One of the most enjoyable choice approaches. The improvisational framework allows organic exploration before returning to the written text.

sources.summary: Both actors identify obligations, structure Believability improvisation together.
Extended example: two actresses mixing real life truths with carefully chosen untruths.

endowment_process_exercise.name: The Endowment Process (Sensory Exercise)
endowment_process_exercise.goal: Transform the perceived qualities of the other actor or an object sensorially, so that the actor responds to a changed stimulus that fulfills the scene's obligations.
endowment_process_exercise.how_to: Begin with the available actor or object as it really is. Ask sensory questions directed at the desired change, responding with the appropriate sense: eyes (color, clouding, blood vessels), skin (texture, color, temperature), hair (texture, hairline), posture (tremor, head position, shoulders), voice (sound, placement, word formation). For endowing another actor with a specific person's features: first identify similarities between the actress's face/body and the imaginary person's, then identify differences and sensorially begin to mold the actress's features toward the imaginary person. Continue asking hundreds of sensory questions across all sensory modalities until the imaginary person's features begin to overlay the real actor. As you succeed and become emotionally affected by the changes, the nature of the sensory questions changes organically.
endowment_process_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any actor who needs to feel a fundamentally different relationship to the scene partner than what the actual person provides; or who needs to relate to a prop or set element as something it isn't.
endowment_process_exercise.why_it_works: The Sense Memory process, when applied to an already-existing object or person, begins to shift the actor's perception. The conglomerate stimulus (real actor + sensory endowment) becomes the living embodiment of the choice.
endowment_process_exercise.additional_info: One choice and one choice approach can address two obligations simultaneously (relationship + emotional). The process can go on as long as it takes to complete the transformation.

sources.summary: Endowment process: detailed sensory questions to age a healthy actor.
As success comes, the nature of sensory questions changes; can address two obligations with one choice.
Endowing actress with wife's features: start with similarities, use contrasts, build sensorially across face, smell, body, voice, rhythm.

animal_externals_exercise.name: Animal Externals (Approach Exercise)
animal_externals_exercise.goal: Absorb the sense of a specific animal through the actor's own physical instrument, then translate that sense into human behavior for use in a role.
animal_externals_exercise.how_to: Find a place where you can observe a specific animal for long periods (zoo, wildlife, video study). Step 1—Overall rhythm: watch and practice the animal's rhythm; take it home and repeat; return and refine. Step 2—Leading center: identify what part of the animal crosses an imaginary line first; apply to your own body. Step 3—Secondary center: find the part that closely follows the leading center; put all three together. Step 4—Limitations: study and add what the animal cannot do; adapt to your body. Step 5—Centers of weight, balance, and power: identify and add to exploration. Step 6—Isolations: practice independent movement of body parts; break animal down and reassemble. Step 7—Corresponding and contrasting rhythms: identify parts moving in harmony vs. counterrhythm; work separately, then together. Step 8—Species and individual mannerisms: species mannerisms first; then individual. Add tempo (speed of rhythm) once secure with rhythms. Translation to human behavior: from all-fours to crouching to standing, retaining all elements, making them progressively more subtle. Sounds: explore where sounds originate in the animal's body; reproduce them from the same body location.
animal_externals_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any actor confronting a character requiring physical attitudes, primal impulses, or behavioral qualities not accessible through other means.
animal_externals_exercise.why_it_works: Approaching inner life from the outside in piques the unconscious. The body leads, and the thoughts, impulses, and emotional life follow the physical instrument's transformation.
animal_externals_exercise.additional_info: Time frame is entirely up to the actor. In public rehearsals, bring only the translated (human) form; in private work, use the full animal position. 'The work or process of a master craftsman is undetectable except to the eyes of an expert.'

sources.summary: Approach technique checklist: eight elements.
Step-by-step: rhythm home; add leading center; add secondary center; practice systematically.
Summary of full system; tempo added last; time frame up to actor.
Full checklist; species mannerisms first; individual mannerisms refine.
Translation to human behavior: degrees from all-fours to standing; inner sense goal.

imaginary_monologue_combined_exercise.name: Imaginary Monologue Combined with Sense Memory
imaginary_monologue_combined_exercise.goal: Dimensionalize an Imaginary Monologue by adding sensory questions about the imaginary person in between the words, allowing the person to appear to respond and creating unpredictability in the expression.
imaginary_monologue_combined_exercise.how_to: Begin establishing the imaginary person sensorially: shape of face, color of eyes, distance, posture. Then begin the monologue, pausing at natural points to ask sensory questions about what the person is doing or showing in response to what was just said. Example sequence: 'How far away is he standing?...How tall is he?...What is the shape of his face?...' then begin words, then mid-sentence ask 'What is his mouth like?...How is he looking at me as I talk to him?' then continue words. The sensory questions can begin before the monologue words and continue after the monologue ends. Allow the imaginary person's responses to genuinely affect what is said next.
imaginary_monologue_combined_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any actor using Imaginary Monologue as a choice approach who wants to deepen the reality and create genuine unpredictability.
imaginary_monologue_combined_exercise.why_it_works: Seeing the person you are talking to allows for their responses to be perceived—the imaginary person becomes more real and more reactive, producing less predictable and more organic emotional expression.
imaginary_monologue_combined_exercise.additional_info: A very natural combination: the two approaches support each other rather than interfering. Sense Memory part can start before the monologue and continue after it ends.

sources.summary: Combined Imaginary Monologue + Sense Memory: sensory questions interspersed; allows imaginary person to 'respond.'
Example showing alternating pattern of sensory questions and monologue words; both done simultaneously.

illnesses_restrictions_exercise.name: Illnesses and Restrictions — Creating a Headache
illnesses_restrictions_exercise.goal: Sensorially create a physical sensation (headache, bodily pain, or restriction) that organically produces the character behavior required by the scene.
illnesses_restrictions_exercise.how_to: Direct sensory attention to the affected body part and ask sensory questions responded to in that area: What do I feel in my forehead?...How high above my eyebrows?...Where do I feel the most?...How far does that feeling reach?...The right side?...The left?...How high up the head?...Around the sides?...At the back?...As I slowly move my head, what do I experience?...When I close my eyes, what happens?...Where does the pain seem to come from?...How does it change as it moves away from that point?...As I move, what happens in my head?...How do the sounds in this room affect the feeling?...What sounds particularly affect the pain?...As I bend forward, backward, what happens? For restrictions (body brace, handcuffs, neck brace): ask questions about the presence of the object—where does it touch the body, what is the texture and temperature, how much movement is prevented, what does resistance feel like.
illnesses_restrictions_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any actor required to play illness, pain, physical restriction, or any emotional state that a physical restriction would organically produce (irritability, fear, panic, helplessness).
illnesses_restrictions_exercise.why_it_works: The base reality (real sensory experience of pain or restriction) produces all the organic behavior that results. The actor doesn't communicate pain—he experiences it, and the behavior follows authentically.
illnesses_restrictions_exercise.additional_info: Works for any kind of physical restriction. A person in real pain behaves differently even in voice quality. The sensory process is the same as any Sense Memory work—the questions aim at the relevant body part.

sources.summary: Headache Sense Memory process: detailed questions about forehead, sides, back of head, movement, sounds.
Creating a neck brace: questions about pressure, texture, temperature, range of motion restriction.

creative_visualization_exercise.name: Creative Visualization Exercise
creative_visualization_exercise.goal: Establish a time-and-place reality and/or emotional state by guiding oneself into a specific imagined or remembered environment, allowing the unconscious to produce life that carries into the scene.
creative_visualization_exercise.how_to: Identify the scene's obligation (especially time-and-place and emotional obligations). Select a place—real or imagined—designed to stimulate the needed feelings and relationship to place. Close your eyes. Begin to visualize that environment: its colors, objects, sounds, smells. Guide yourself through the visualization like moving through a dream, from one element or event to another. Support the visualization at every point with all five senses—avoid intellectual processing. Allow yourself to get the sense of being in that place. As the unconscious begins to function, let thoughts and impulses emerge. When you feel ready, open your eyes and begin the scene, relating to the other actor and saying the lines from whatever emotional space you are in. The carryover is usually substantial. If needed, supplement with additional choices related to the environment. Advanced practice: attempt the process with eyes open, through the 'inner eye.'
creative_visualization_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any actor confronting a time-and-place obligation where the stage environment is insufficient; any actor who needs to transport themselves to a specific emotional reality through place.
creative_visualization_exercise.why_it_works: When the unconscious is engaged through vivid sensory visualization, it produces authentic thoughts and impulses that carry into the scene—not manufactured emotions but organic responses stimulated by the actor's genuine connection to the imagined environment.
creative_visualization_exercise.additional_info: When we were children we did this for play. It is also very relaxing and fulfilling as an adventure in its own right. Do not confuse with Affective Memory—it is an entirely different, more imaginative process.

sources.summary: Creative Visualization: guide through environment like a dream; allow unconscious to produce thoughts/impulses; carry over into scene.
Support with all five senses; never stay in the head; can do with inner eye once facile.

personal_voice_dialogue_exercise.name: Personal Voice Dialogue (Self-Facilitated Sub-Personality)
personal_voice_dialogue_exercise.goal: Call forth a specific sub-personality by speaking to it directly, allowing it to come forward and take over the actor's behavior for the scene.
personal_voice_dialogue_exercise.how_to: From a clear, objective, ego-state place of consciousness, identify the sub-personality you wish to reach. Speak to that part directly: 'I would like to hear from my [pusher/victim/free spirit/moralist, etc.]...Are you around?' Physically move over a foot or two to accommodate that part of yourself. Allow the sub-personality to respond and speak from its own point of view. Listen to what it has to say. If it does not respond immediately, continue trying—more coaxing or manipulation may be needed. Once you feel the sub-personality is present and in position—that it has taken control—begin the rehearsal or performance. Alternatively: identify the specific circumstances, stimuli, or conditions that naturally pique that sub-personality in your life, and create or relate to those conditions.
personal_voice_dialogue_exercise.who_should_do_it: Any actor who wants to call forth a specific sub-personality for a role and does not have a partner to facilitate Voice Dialogue.
personal_voice_dialogue_exercise.why_it_works: Speaking directly to the sub-personality from an objective place, and physically accommodating its response, creates a genuine internal shift rather than an external imitation. The sub-personality has its own unique intelligence for the material.
personal_voice_dialogue_exercise.additional_info: Success depends on approaching from an objective, conscious place. If another sub-personality in conflict with the desired one is in the driver's seat, the attempt may fail—resolve that conflict first. 'Be courageous in your exploration of this choice approach.'

sources.summary: Personal Voice Dialogue: actor calls on sub-personality alone; moves to accommodate when it responds.
Pusher example: responds immediately when called.

animal_study_and_translation.name: Animal Study and Translation
animal_study_and_translation.goal: To build a distinctive, embodied physical life for a character by inhabiting the movement qualities of an animal appropriate to the character.
animal_study_and_translation.how_to: Step 1: Identify an animal whose qualities (stealth, power, nervousness, laziness, predatory focus) match the character's essential nature. Step 2: Study the animal directly over multiple sessions — at a zoo, in nature, or via film — observing its rhythms, centers of gravity, movement tempo, and behavioral patterns. Step 3: Physically embody those qualities — get on all fours if necessary — and feel the animal's way of being in your own body. Step 4: Gradually translate those qualities into human form, retaining the animal's essence while moving like a person. Step 5: Periodically reinvest in the raw animal quality throughout rehearsal and production to prevent the human translation from becoming generic.
animal_study_and_translation.who_should_do_it: Actors working on characters with distinctive physical lives; actors who tend toward psychological abstraction and need physical grounding; actors whose characters require a specific quality of movement (stealth, danger, vulnerability, predatory awareness).
animal_study_and_translation.why_it_works: Anchoring character physicality in a real observed behavioral system gives the actor a specific, non-self-conscious physical truth to inhabit; the animal's rhythms provide a template that bypasses the actor's habitual self-presentation.
animal_study_and_translation.additional_info: Requires extended preparation time (multiple visits to observe the animal). The exercise may look bizarre to observers, as Morris discovered when forty crew members watched him crawling around on the floor. Requires actor courage to do on set.

sources.summary: Morris goes to the zoo for several days to study a black leopard for his character Tippo. Gets on all fours between scenes to reinvest; is discovered by the entire crew.

enchanted_play_ritual.name: Enchanted Play Ritual (Pre-Performance)
enchanted_play_ritual.goal: To access the genuine joy and playful engagement of acting as a pre-performance state, bypassing anxiety and fatigue.
enchanted_play_ritual.how_to: Option A (alone): Arrive at the theater or set early. Stand under the performance lights, look into the empty audience space or performance area, and consciously invite the excitement and joy of acting to fill you — remembering why you do this work, the pleasure of inhabiting stories and feelings. Option B (with co-actors): Engage in spontaneous playful interaction with your scene partners before shooting — let yourself play with them, find the fun in the material, let the joy of working together build naturally into a performance-ready state.
enchanted_play_ritual.who_should_do_it: Any actor preparing for performance who is experiencing anxiety, exhaustion, or numbness; actors who have lost connection to why they act; actors who feel the 'work' has become mechanical.
enchanted_play_ritual.why_it_works: Play is the actor's fundamental mode; accessing genuine play bypasses the self-monitoring, anxious part of the mind and allows natural, spontaneous behavior.
enchanted_play_ritual.additional_info: Lucille Ball's description of this process ('I get with Vivian and we play, and very soon I get into the enchanted sense of play and can be Lucy') is one of the most distilled accounts of instrumental preparation in the book.

sources.summary: Lucy Ball describes her version of this: makeup, costume, playing with Vivian Vance.
Morris practices his version: arriving early at summer stock theaters, standing in the lights, filling with the sense of play.

relaxation_exercise_group.name: Group Relaxation Exercise (Floor Work)
relaxation_exercise_group.goal: To physically and psychologically release tension from the body as a foundation for all other instrumental and craft work in class.
relaxation_exercise_group.how_to: All actors lie on the floor, typically in comfortable positions. The teacher/coach guides them through systematic release — possibly of individual body parts, possibly through breath, possibly through visualization. The actor allows the floor to support them fully, releasing the muscular holding patterns that accompany ordinary social consciousness. The session may last ten to thirty minutes.
relaxation_exercise_group.who_should_do_it: Any actor in class as a regular foundational practice; particularly important before emotionally demanding work or after intense scenes.
relaxation_exercise_group.why_it_works: Deep physical relaxation reduces the sympathetic nervous system's arousal, which underlies self-consciousness, muscular tension, and emotional blocks. When genuinely relaxed, the actor's instrument is available for authentic experience.
relaxation_exercise_group.additional_info: Morris's class was doing this exercise when the Manson-era police entered with guns drawn — and the spontaneous startle response when everyone bolted upright demonstrated that the actors had achieved genuine relaxation, not performed relaxation.

sources.summary: Morris's acting class lying on the floor in relaxation exercise during the Manson murder week; police enter with guns drawn; the students bolt upright — demonstrating genuine rather than performed relaxation.

script_breakdown_for_coaching.name: Script Breakdown for Acting Coaching
script_breakdown_for_coaching.goal: For a coach working with an actor on unfamiliar material or a specific role, to break the script down scene by scene and identify the acting obligations, character needs, and moments requiring special attention before coaching begins.
script_breakdown_for_coaching.how_to: The coach reads the script thoroughly, identifying: (1) what each scene requires emotionally and behaviorally; (2) where the character's relationships with other characters must be felt specifically; (3) what instrumental and craft tools may be needed; (4) which scenes require special preparation. Then works with the actor scene by scene, addressing instrumental work before craft work.
script_breakdown_for_coaching.who_should_do_it: Acting coaches working with actors preparing for specific productions, particularly when the coach has limited time (as Morris had nine weeks with Schwarzenegger).
script_breakdown_for_coaching.why_it_works: Scene-by-scene breakdown prevents the actor from arriving at emotionally demanding moments unprepared; allows coach to target instrumental work precisely where it is needed.
script_breakdown_for_coaching.additional_info: Morris describes this when working with Schwarzenegger: 'When we finally got to the script, I broke it down scene by scene.' He was initially honest with the director that nine weeks was very short time and that he might confuse Arnold rather than help him.

sources.summary: Morris's approach to coaching Schwarzenegger: breaking the script down scene by scene before working on the acting.

one_person_being.name: One-Person BEING
one_person_being.goal: To practice BEING in its fullest form — allowing and expressing everything felt, vocally, verbally, and physically, without restriction.
one_person_being.how_to: Follow Personal Inventory. Stop asking 'How do I feel?' and simply BE. Allow all feelings to express themselves vocally, verbally, and physically. Acknowledge obstacles that get in the way. If you start to comment on or interfere with your impulses, include the commentary, then follow the underlying true impulses. Can be done sitting, standing, or lying down.
one_person_being.who_should_do_it: All actors. Good follow-on to Personal Inventory. Especially important for actors who suppress or who are overly controlled.
one_person_being.why_it_works: One expression gives way to the next — flow of reality becomes compelling. Allows actor to find personal gap between obligation and impulse. Reveals what is being avoided.
one_person_being.additional_info: See taped dialogue with E.J. in Chapter 1 for practical example of what BEING looks and feels like in practice.

sources.summary: Exercise defined: follow Personal Inventory, stop asking the question, just BE.
One expression gives way to next. Find gap between obligation and impulse.

i_am_i_want_i_need_i_feel.name: I Am, I Want, I Need, I Feel
i_am_i_want_i_need_i_feel.goal: To surprise the actor with impulses and feelings by forcing rapid, impulsive self-expression in a structured format before intellectual editing can occur.
i_am_i_want_i_need_i_feel.how_to: Speak aloud rapidly in stream-of-consciousness, starting every sentence with one of the four statements (not necessarily in order): 'I am...', 'I want...', 'I need...', 'I feel...' Respond impulsively — do not take a beat to think. If you go blank, say the first thing that comes to mind no matter how illogical.
i_am_i_want_i_need_i_feel.who_should_do_it: All actors. Especially valuable for those who intellectualize or premeditate their responses.
i_am_i_want_i_need_i_feel.why_it_works: The rapid format and structured categories prevent the conditioned mind from filling the blank with prepared responses. Impulsiveness bypasses the censor and produces real feelings.
i_am_i_want_i_need_i_feel.additional_info: Can repeat the four stems many times. Encourage going back and forth between the four statements.

sources.summary: Exercise defined: rapid, impulsive, structured format. Purpose is to surprise yourself with what comes out.

vulgarity_exercise.name: Vulgarity Exercise
vulgarity_exercise.goal: To break through propriety-based social conditioning that restricts an actor's freedom of expression, by doing taboo behaviors in a supportive class environment.
vulgarity_exercise.how_to: In front of the class, engage in crude, socially prohibited behavior — picking nose, scratching, belching, using profanity, physical crudeness. The class provides acceptance and encouragement. Even partial completion cracks the ice.
vulgarity_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors whose propriety concerns or social conditioning are creating a cluster of mannerisms blocking real feelings. Especially those from strict or conservative backgrounds.
vulgarity_exercise.why_it_works: After even partial completion, actor is freed from an entire cluster of social mannerisms that stood between them and real feelings. The class atmosphere is permissive, supporting awareness that the actor has the right to do these things.
vulgarity_exercise.additional_info: Similar exercises include Sensuality and Anti-Social. Doing the most difficult thing first eliminates less difficult problems in one fell swoop.

sources.summary: Vulgarity exercise described: pick nose, scratch, belch, profanity. Even partial completion frees cluster of social mannerisms.

ego_reconstruction.name: Ego Reconstruction
ego_reconstruction.goal: To help actors perceive each other more clearly and receive constructive, honest feedback that builds self-awareness and self-worth.
ego_reconstruction.how_to: Sitting in a circle (the round), observe and perceive the people around you. Express to individuals your perception of them in a way you feel would be constructive or helpful to their growth. Only one person speaks at a time — avoid two-way conversations. Whether you agree or disagree with what is said to you, just listen. Two limitations: you may not be cruel for the sake of being cruel, and you may not violate anyone's privacy without permission.
ego_reconstruction.who_should_do_it: Acting class groups and rehearsal groups. Led by a skillful and responsible leader.
ego_reconstruction.why_it_works: The exercise is as beneficial for the person expressing as for the one hearing. When an actor hears the same things said to them over a period of time, they begin to accept them as valid. People are surprisingly affirmative in this exercise.
ego_reconstruction.additional_info: Part of the Rounds family. Done in a circle. Leader enforces no cruelty and no privacy violations.

sources.summary: Ego Reconstruction: round exercise expressing constructive perceptions of others. Beneficial for both speaker and listener.

reluctancy.name: Reluctancy
reluctancy.goal: To progressively open all of the actor's doors of expression by having them expose things they are uncomfortable, ashamed, or afraid to say publicly. Teaches that fear of the skeletons in the closet is unfounded.
reluctancy.how_to: Done in the round. Categories: Self Reluctancy (anything about yourself that is difficult to say), Encounter Reluctancy (things you are reticent to say to another person in the room), Physical Reluctancy (anything about your body that concerns you), Sexual Reluctancy (anything sexual you feel reluctant to express). Avoid two-way conversations. The leader maintains control and safety. No assaulting or hurting others.
reluctancy.who_should_do_it: Not for casual rehearsal groups. Must be led by a skillful and responsible leader in a group that meets regularly.
reluctancy.why_it_works: The inhibitions you start with disappear over time, and this freedom carries directly into acting. The more you expose yourself in Reluctancy, the more areas are open to you on stage. Everybody has skeletons — it is okay.
reluctancy.additional_info: Can be dangerous without a skilled, responsible leader. Over time it is exciting, sometimes explosive, often funny, and always freeing.

sources.summary: Reluctancy defined: expression of things you are uncomfortable, ashamed, or reluctant to say. Four categories. Progressive opening of expression.
Reluctancy opens all doors. Freedom carries into acting. There is only you — the character is you.

how_i_see_myself.name: How I See Myself (Self Image)
how_i_see_myself.goal: To examine the actor's self-image and compare it with how others actually perceive them, identifying gaps between self-perception and reality.
how_i_see_myself.how_to: Done in the round, individually and voluntarily (or called on by the leader). In 2-10 minutes, talk about how you see yourself — not how you think others see you, but how you feel you are. Cover all facets of yourself. When finished, ask the group for feedback: Is the way I see myself consistent with the way you see me? Multiple people give feedback.
how_i_see_myself.who_should_do_it: Acting class groups, ideally with an experienced leader. Anyone who needs clarity on the gap between self-image and reality.
how_i_see_myself.why_it_works: Invaluable for dealing with inconsistencies between what you think you are and what you actually are. The feedback directs you specifically to areas that need work. Sometimes a person's self-image is delusional — feedback brings reality.
how_i_see_myself.additional_info: Feedback can come from 7-8 people or all group members in marathons. The trap is second-guessing what others think — stay true to your actual self-image.

sources.summary: How I See Myself: 2-10 minutes talking about your self-image, then asking for group feedback. Invaluable for identifying gaps between self-image and reality.
Feedback reveals inconsistencies between what you think you are and what you actually are, directing you to areas needing work.

two_people_being.name: Two-People BEING
two_people_being.goal: A four-part preparation exercise to establish BEING with a partner, get related, begin sensory work, and prepare for scene work.
two_people_being.how_to: Part 1: Both actors semi-audibly express all anxieties and tensions about being in front of the group in stream-of-consciousness style. Part 2: Observe-Wonder-Perceive your partner. Part 3: Work for a simple sensory choice in relation to your partner without obligating to any result (e.g., create eyeglasses on partner, different hair color, specific look in eyes). Part 4: If preparing for a scene, begin working for your first choice in that scene; if just a BEING exercise, obligate yourself to something you want to feel toward your partner (jealousy, desire, compassion, fear) and work sensorily for it. Throughout, acknowledge and express anything that intrudes on your awareness.
two_people_being.who_should_do_it: Any two actors who are about to rehearse together, do a scene, or audition together. Also valuable as a daily BEING practice.
two_people_being.why_it_works: Addresses tension about performing for others, gets partners truly involved with each other rather than performing, and transitions naturally into choice work for the scene.
two_people_being.additional_info: Important to remain standing throughout — intensifies the feeling of being on the spot. Make the obligation specific: not general jealousy but what you are jealous about specifically.

sources.summary: Two-People BEING: four-part exercise. Semi-audible acknowledgment of anxieties, Observe-Wonder-Perceive, simple sensory choice, then scene choice or obligation choice.
BEING exercise gets actor in touch with who they are, what they feel, how they're functioning here and now. That reality is the only place you can act from organically.

hold_hands_look_at_each_other.name: Hold Hands and Look at Each Other
hold_hands_look_at_each_other.goal: To begin a genuine relationship between two actors through sustained, undefended eye contact and physical connection.
hold_hands_look_at_each_other.how_to: Two people sit and hold each other's hands comfortably, looking into each other's eyes. Allow and encourage everything that happens to happen. At first you might be embarrassed and want to look away. Continue for at least 15 minutes (best results with long duration). No talking allowed.
hold_hands_look_at_each_other.who_should_do_it: Any two actors, especially those who do not know each other or who have a superficial relationship.
hold_hands_look_at_each_other.why_it_works: Sustained eye contact and physical connection bypass social defenses and produce genuine emotional responses. At the end of the exercise a real relationship has begun which can go in any direction.
hold_hands_look_at_each_other.additional_info: Best results when done for a long period of time — at least 15 minutes.

sources.summary: Hold Hands and Look at Each Other: 15+ minutes of eye contact while holding hands. No talking. Many feelings expressed. A real relationship begins.

sense_memory_exercise.name: The Sense Memory Exercise (Core Practice)
sense_memory_exercise.goal: To train the senses to recreate objects that are not present, building the foundational craft skill for all choice work in acting.
sense_memory_exercise.how_to: Select an object (ideally food or drink to engage all five senses). Place it in front of you. Ask sensory questions systematically through each sense category (spatial, visual, tactile, temperature, olfactory, taste, auditory), spending time in each category before moving to the next. Do not use labels or descriptive language — let senses supply answers. Work with the real object, then put it aside and ask the same questions working without it (imaginary). Compare to catch what was missed. Return to real object. Repeat this cycle. Eventually do entire exercise without the object. Chronological order of action: place object, approach object, lift, bring to face, taste, swallow, replace.
sense_memory_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors at all levels. Must be practiced daily. Beginners: 45 min to 1 hour daily with inanimate objects. Advanced: multiple objects at different stages simultaneously.
sense_memory_exercise.why_it_works: Trains senses to respond to imaginary stimuli. The same ability that allows recreating a coffee cup allows recreating a suicidal emotional reality. Sense memory is the cornerstone of the craftual foundation. With years of practice, the response becomes unconscious and automatic.
sense_memory_exercise.additional_info: Key rules: no language answers (not 'hot' but the sensation itself), categorical question support required, chronological order must be respected, senses can be intermingled but support each other. Sense memory is not pantomime and not intellectual — it takes place in the senses. Practice 45 min to 3 hours daily.

sources.summary: Sense Memory Exercise detailed: select object, ask sensory questions by category, work with and without object. Cornerstone of craftual foundation.
About the Sense Memory Exercise: must be practiced daily. Will elicit unconscious organic responses of astounding dimension.
Sense memory must become part of your philosophy — a way of living, not just a clinical exercise.

cluster_of_four.name: The Cluster of Four
cluster_of_four.goal: An ideally balanced group of four exercises to begin any class, rehearsal, or work session. Systematically addresses tension, awareness, feeling, and desire.
cluster_of_four.how_to: In sequence: (1) Relaxation exercise (Logey, Tense and Relax, Deep Breathing, Rag Doll, or similar); (2) Sensitizing — raising your senses to higher levels of sensitivity; (3) Personal Inventory — finding out how you feel; (4) What Do I Want — as part of Personal Inventory. The sequence is not accidental: address physical tension first so you can sensitize, then find out how you feel and what you want.
cluster_of_four.who_should_do_it: Any actor at the start of a work session. Used in class every week for years as the standard opening exercise.
cluster_of_four.why_it_works: The sequence is carefully ordered: relaxation enables sensitizing; sensitizing enables honest Personal Inventory; Personal Inventory grounds the actor in reality before creative work begins.
cluster_of_four.additional_info: Morris used this to start every class for years. Sensuality exercise works well following the Cluster of Four.

sources.summary: Cluster of Four: relaxation, sensitizing, Personal Inventory, What Do I Want. Sequence arrived at by trial and error. Used to start every class.

being_workout.name: The BEING Workout
being_workout.goal: To build the habit of eliminating obstacles that keep the actor from being themselves with anyone, by practicing BEING with a series of changing partners.
being_workout.how_to: Done with a series of changing partners in a group or acting class. With each partner: start by relating on a here-and-now basis, acknowledging all distractions. Deal with all obstacles between you and the other person (fear, social obligation, sexual attraction, hostility, unsaid feelings, lack of interest). Express these audibly or semi-audibly. Once obstacles are acknowledged and expressed, BE. Repeat with each changing partner.
being_workout.who_should_do_it: Acting class groups. Can also be adapted for daily BEING practice by doing it encounter-style with each person you meet in life.
being_workout.why_it_works: Repetition builds the habit of eliminating obstacles to BEING with anyone. Each person presents a different set of obstacles. The goal is to accept life as it is and become more comfortable with uncomfortable feelings.
being_workout.additional_info: Can be adapted for daily encounter-style practice with anyone met in daily life.

sources.summary: BEING Workout: series of changing partners, each time acknowledging obstacles to BEING then BEing. Builds habit of eliminating obstacles with anyone.

evocative_words.name: Evocative Words
evocative_words.goal: To stimulate emotional vulnerability and life by saying single words or brief phrases connected to a meaningful experience, bypassing the storytelling/reporting impulse.
evocative_words.how_to: Think of a meaningful experience. Instead of telling the story, say single words or brief phrases connected to that time and place — people, what they wore, objects, sounds, weather, etc. Say them audibly or semi-audibly. Do not explain or report. Example: 'Park. Bench. Trees. Children playing. Carousel music. Excitement. Blue sky. He's coming. Blond. Graduation ring. Serious eyes. No. Please. Lost. Cold.' If pleased with the vulnerability level, stop. If wanting more, move to another experience.
evocative_words.who_should_do_it: All actors. Useful alone, in front of a group, or as a choice in a scene.
evocative_words.why_it_works: When you tell the story, the obligation to be understood takes precedence over being emotionally affected. Single words bypass the reporting impulse and go directly to emotional impact.
evocative_words.additional_info: Can be used as a preparation before going on stage, as a choice in a scene (saying words between lines to stimulate emotional life), or for vulnerability work. Discovered by accident while working with an actress named Carey.

sources.summary: Evocative Words: say single words connected to a meaningful experience instead of telling the story. Bypasses the reporting impulse and goes directly to emotional impact.
Can use as preparation, as choice in a scene (saying words between lines), or for vulnerability. Discovered by accident.

imaginary_monologue.name: Imaginary Monologue
imaginary_monologue.goal: To elicit meaningful gut-level emotional investments by speaking to an absent person as if they were present, covering things that would be difficult or impossible to say in reality.
imaginary_monologue.how_to: Talk to someone who is not present as if they really were there. Speak about things that would be either difficult or impossible to say if the person were really present. Can be done for vulnerability, as a preparation before a scene, or as a choice within a scene. Can be directed toward someone you love, someone who is dead, a parent, grandparent, or any significant person.
imaginary_monologue.who_should_do_it: All actors. Particularly valuable when emotional investment in a relationship is needed. Useful in many contexts.
imaginary_monologue.why_it_works: Without the consequence of the other person actually being present, the actor can say what they truly feel, eliciting genuine gut-level emotional responses. Originally called the Steering Wheel Monologue because Morris did it while driving.
imaginary_monologue.additional_info: Can also be used in a scene as an imaginary monologue to a real partner but talking to someone else (exercise #48), or as a dialogue with a real partner while taking personally what is said (exercise #49).

sources.summary: Imaginary Monologue: talk to someone absent as if present, covering things impossible to say in reality. Elicits gut-level emotional investments.

sensory_inventory.name: Sensory Inventory
sensory_inventory.goal: To investigate all five senses and discover how they work, how they are affected by stimuli, and what specific questions appeal to each sense.
sensory_inventory.how_to: Systematically investigate all your senses and what makes them work. Ask how each sense responds to real objects. Take an emotional inventory alongside the sensory one — how do you feel about each object? Practice approaching objects you normally take for granted with all your senses. Daily repetition progressively raises sensory acuity.
sensory_inventory.who_should_do_it: All actors. Daily practice. Essential preparation for sense memory work.
sensory_inventory.why_it_works: As you repeat daily, more and more parts of each sense begin to function. It is an endless investigation that stimulates increasingly higher levels of sensory awareness.
sensory_inventory.additional_info: Helps the actor discover which sense is strongest and which need more work. Reveals the specific questions that appeal to the actor's personal sensory apparatus.

sources.summary: Sensory Inventory: investigation of all senses. As you repeat day by day, sensory acuity grows. Endless investigation stimulating increasingly higher awareness.

sensuality_exercise.name: Sensuality Exercise
sensuality_exercise.goal: To increase awareness and sensory availability of the entire body by engaging in uninhibited sensual exploration of self, objects, and (optionally) other people.
sensuality_exercise.how_to: Three parts: (1) Sensuality toward your own body — touch yourself all over sensorily and sensually, feeling shapes and contours, encouraging your body to respond to your hands, including genital areas and any sexual response. Let your body find its own sensual rhythm and movement. (2) Sensuality toward inanimate objects — relate sensually to wall, floor, rug, chair, etc. Expose all parts of body to object. (3) Sensuality toward people — touch hands, cheek to thigh, face in hair, back to back rhythmic contact. Taste, smell, and touch things you would conventionally avoid. Variation: Primal Sensuality — same exercise but more animal and primitive in movements and sounds.
sensuality_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors. Effective following the Cluster of Four. Both solo and group versions.
sensuality_exercise.why_it_works: Increases awareness and sensory availability of the entire body. Breaks down barriers more quickly, especially in the Primal Sensuality variation.
sensuality_exercise.additional_info: May stimulate sexuality — acceptable if working for that purpose, but main purpose is bodily awareness and sensory availability. Primal Sensuality variation breaks down barriers more quickly.

sources.summary: Sensuality: three-part exercise. (1) self, (2) inanimate objects, (3) people. Increases awareness and sensory availability of entire body.
Primal Sensuality variation: more animal and primitive movements and sounds. Breaks down barriers more quickly.

super_consciousness.name: Super Consciousness
super_consciousness.goal: To deliberately and temporarily elevate awareness to an extreme level, training a permanently higher baseline level of awareness through daily repetition.
super_consciousness.how_to: Make an arbitrary decision to become super conscious, super aware of everything in all five sensory areas. Decide to hear every possible sound from most obvious to most subtle. Devour everything through all five senses. Do it for 10-15 minutes.
super_consciousness.who_should_do_it: All actors, as part of daily awareness preparation.
super_consciousness.why_it_works: Daily repetition of Super Consciousness automatically raises your level of awareness even when not deliberately exercising it.
super_consciousness.additional_info: Somewhat unnatural — the acute observations may make natural relating to others difficult during the exercise. But daily repetition makes the elevated awareness automatic.

sources.summary: Super Consciousness: deliberate decision to become super aware of everything in all five sensory areas for 10-15 minutes. Daily repetition automatically raises awareness level.

positivity_and_validation.name: Positivity and Validation
positivity_and_validation.goal: To change a negative ego-state to a positive one through deliberate focus on positive aspects of the self and environment.
positivity_and_validation.how_to: Two approaches: (1) Start with whatever positive impulses you genuinely feel and verbalize them, building from there. If this leads to more positive feelings, good. If not, begin to exaggerate. (2) When so negative that even thinking positive seems impossible, make an arbitrary commitment to say only positive things about everything you see, hear, taste, smell, and touch — even if it means lying at first. Validation variation: go around the room and express supportive, validating thoughts about each person honestly. By honestly validating others, you begin to feel better about yourself.
positivity_and_validation.who_should_do_it: Any actor with a negative ego-state. Especially effective before performing or rehearsing when feeling depressed or hopeless.
positivity_and_validation.why_it_works: The arbitrary commitment to positivity connects at some point with the seeds of buried positivity. Validation: by giving out sincere compliments, the actor's own ego-state improves.
positivity_and_validation.additional_info: Validation is a variation that works through validating others rather than focusing on self.

sources.summary: Positivity: two approaches — start from genuine positivity and build, or arbitrarily commit to positivity when deeply negative. Validation variation: validate others to improve own ego-state.

magic_pocket.name: Magic Pocket
magic_pocket.goal: To build ego-state by imagining that you carry something magnificent in your pocket — using imagination and sensory process to create a profound sense of possession.
magic_pocket.how_to: Imagine that in one of your pockets you have something magnificent — a studio contract, an Academy Award telegram, keys to a Rolls Royce, a wallet that never runs out, a letter from someone you love. Can be done through simple suggestion or through sense memory. Walk around with your hand in your pocket or patting the outside of it. Allow the belief to grow until you feel totally in possession of the most important thing in your life. The magic aspect is vital — encourage yourself to believe.
magic_pocket.who_should_do_it: Any actor needing ego elevation. Can be done anywhere — on a movie set, on the street, in a class, at a party.
magic_pocket.why_it_works: The sustained belief in the magical possession elevates ego-state and produces a sense of confidence and importance that changes behavior.
magic_pocket.additional_info: Can be done through simple suggestion or through full sense memory process.

sources.summary: Magic Pocket: imagine something magnificent in your pocket. Encourage belief to grow. Vital that the magic aspect is believed.

take_your_due.name: Take Your Due
take_your_due.goal: To assert the actor's own value and ask for what they deserve, building confidence and self-worth.
take_your_due.how_to: Express out loud in words and action all the positive things you feel you have coming to you. Ask for what you want from various people around you. Talk about areas where you feel you have been misunderstood and have not received your due. Make your statement physically as well — standing to full height, taking up rightful space, being flamboyant in gestures if you wish.
take_your_due.who_should_do_it: Most effective done in a group or with another person. Any actor suffering from low self-worth or inability to assert themselves.
take_your_due.why_it_works: As you do this exercise repeatedly, trust grows. You gain confidence in your ability to make your own statement.
take_your_due.additional_info: The physical component is important — make the statement with your whole body, not just words.

sources.summary: Take Your Due: express all positive things you have coming, ask for what you want, talk about being misunderstood. Physical expression equally important.

desert_exercise.name: The Desert Exercise
desert_exercise.goal: An advanced sense memory exercise to create a complex, total environment through progressive sensory accumulation.
desert_exercise.how_to: You are stranded in the desert. Create all the elements of exposure, exhaustion, thirst, heat, vastness, and isolation. Begin with one element — for instance, heat. Make the room hotter than it is. Add the desert visually, locating the sun. These support each other. It may take two weeks to work up a sweat. Add one element at a time of the total reality. If successful, heat itself may stimulate thirst.
desert_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who have been doing sense memory for a while. Not recommended for beginners. Can also be done as a group improvisation.
desert_exercise.why_it_works: Progressive sensory accumulation allows one element to support and trigger others, building a total reality that produces genuine physical and emotional responses.
desert_exercise.additional_info: Not recommended for beginners to sense memory. Can be done with a group as an improvisation.

sources.summary: Desert Exercise: create total desert environment progressively. Begin with heat, add elements one at a time. May take two weeks to work up a sweat.

farmer_s_market.name: Farmer's Market
farmer_s_market.goal: To train awareness, observation, and personal point of view by working in a rich public environment filled with sensory stimuli.
farmer_s_market.how_to: Go to a place like a farmer's market — a public setting with abundant sensory stimulation. Observe, Wonder and Perceive everything around you. Take an inventory of all your senses. Notice how you feel about everything you perceive. Develop your personal point of view in relation to what you see, hear, smell, taste, and touch.
farmer_s_market.who_should_do_it: All actors. Part of the Cluster of Four as an awareness preparation. Can be done in any rich sensory environment.
farmer_s_market.why_it_works: A real-world environment provides stimulation that class settings cannot. The richness of public life forces genuine reactions and personal points of view.
farmer_s_market.additional_info: Referenced in the book as a key awareness preparation exercise. Also used as preparation for being in specific environments required by a play.

sources.summary: Farmer's Market: awareness exercise done in a rich sensory public environment. Part of instrumental preparation for awareness.

group_vulnerability.name: Group Vulnerability
group_vulnerability.goal: To create a progressively more sensitive, vulnerable atmosphere in a group by sharing emotional experiences, making the group ready for creative work.
group_vulnerability.how_to: Two approaches: (1) One individual shares an emotionally-rich experience with the group. The group identifies with the vulnerability. As more individuals share, the atmosphere becomes progressively more sensitive. (2) The group works simultaneously and individually, privately, for their own choices. The vulnerable responses are infectious — emotion in the room has a snowball effect, feeding on itself.
group_vulnerability.who_should_do_it: Acting class groups and rehearsal groups. Especially effective before doing emotionally demanding material. If in a professional rehearsal, the director should lead off with a vulnerable experience.
group_vulnerability.why_it_works: Vulnerability is contagious — like laughter or tears, the emotional life in the room builds on itself. Creates a collective readiness to go deeper.
group_vulnerability.additional_info: Works for classroom or rehearsal settings. Director modeling vulnerability first is especially helpful in professional settings.

sources.summary: Group Vulnerability: two approaches — individual sharing or simultaneous private work. Emotion is infectious and builds on itself.

pretend.name: Pretend (Self and Two-People)
pretend.goal: To stretch the imagination by engaging in child-like pretending, creating imaginary circumstances without logic constraints.
pretend.how_to: Either by yourself or with another person, silently or out loud, pretend whatever pleases you to pretend, as a child would. Solo: talk out loud and go through physical actions supporting the pretend circumstances. Duration: 5 minutes to a whole day in the real world acting out the pretend scenario. Two-people: pretend anything together — an ocean cruise, a long-term relationship, an argument. Both actors feed the reality with more and more imaginary circumstances and physical actions.
pretend.who_should_do_it: All actors. Good preparation for a scene or to get a relationship going with another actor. Can also be used as a choice in a scene.
pretend.why_it_works: The spinning of pretense often leads to actual sensory responses — seeing, hearing, tasting, feeling the imaginary objects. Also stretches the actor's belief in imaginary realities.
pretend.additional_info: Can be used directly as a choice in a scene. Joan used it as a choice in a film scene where she had to pretend a ridiculous centerpiece was an Oscar Award.

sources.summary: Pretend: child-like pretending alone or with another person. Spinning pretense leads to actual sensory responses. Can be used as a choice in a scene.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to begin learning imaging
recommendations.recommendation: Start by becoming conscious of what imaging you already do naturally: what kinds of images, when they occur, what patterns emerge. Then practice Imaging with Sense Memory using real places or events from your life, always opening the eyes once the inner image is established.

sources.summary: Begin by becoming aware of existing imaging patterns; image with eyes open after starting with eyes closed.
Start with an environment; open eyes shortly after beginning; involve all senses.

recommendations.condition: Actor is visually dominant and neglects other senses in imaging
recommendations.recommendation: Deliberately begin Imaging Sensations exercises with a non-visual sense (smell, sound, or touch). Practice giving the non-visual senses the same total emphasis you'd give the visual. Over-correct until all senses become habitual.

sources.summary: To overcome visual dominance, initially use non-visual senses with greater emphasis than visual.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to use imaging as a choice approach in a scene
recommendations.recommendation: Identify the scene obligation, select a choice from your own life that would stimulate the appropriate emotional response, then create that choice through Imaging with Sense Memory. Work with all five senses simultaneously, become part of the image (do not watch it on a screen), and let the images run free between sensorial explorations.

sources.summary: Become part of the image — do not watch it on a screen.
Image with outer eyes open; involve all five senses; let the image flow.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to access deeper, unconsciously rooted emotional life for a scene
recommendations.recommendation: During rehearsal, work for your conscious choice while carefully noting all involuntary images that arise. After the session, re-create those involuntary images using the Imaging technique — they likely represent the unconscious's own translation of your obligation and will produce richer emotional life than your original choice.

sources.summary: Re-creating involuntary images produces more dimensional emotional life than original choices.
Involuntary images are the unconscious's translation of the conscious choice.

recommendations.condition: Actor uses Sense Memory alone and finds it overly analytical
recommendations.recommendation: Combine Sense Memory with Imaging: establish the overall environment through imaging first, then use sensorial questions to deepen specific elements. Allow the image to run freely between questions rather than remaining in pure question-response mode.

sources.summary: Imaging+Sense Memory combination is richer than either alone; image provides flow while SM provides specificity.

recommendations.condition: Actor is doing sleep-wake state work, free association, or unconscious programming and finds sensorial questioning intrusive
recommendations.recommendation: Switch from Imaging with Sense Memory to Imaging Sensations — allow all senses to respond simultaneously without the question-response structure, preserving the uninterrupted flow needed for these altered-state techniques.

sources.summary: Imaging Sensations is appropriate when sensorial questioning would interrupt the altered state needed for unconscious programming or free association.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants a reliable shortcut to specific emotional states in performance
recommendations.recommendation: Build a personal library of symbolic images through journal practice. Identify symbols that consistently produce specific feeling states. Use these as shortcut triggers — re-creating the symbol in the moment of need rather than working through a full choice approach.

sources.summary: Symbolic images can serve as reliable emotional triggers once catalogued and tested.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to quickly elevate vulnerability or emotional availability before a scene
recommendations.recommendation: Use Fragmented Imaging with a personal theme (loss, love, childhood) that you know affects you. Let images tumble without needing them to connect. Steer toward the impacting moments using Selective Emphasis. Works much faster than Story Imaging.

sources.summary: Fragmented Imaging: fast, can address all obligations, uses Selective Emphasis; vulnerability example given.

recommendations.condition: Actor keeps anticipating and guiding his own choice exploration (self-facilitation bias)
recommendations.recommendation: Switch to Guided Imaging with a scene partner. Because you cannot predict the facilitator's questions, you explore the choice without premeditation — discovering what the choice actually does to you rather than what you expect it to do.

sources.summary: Guided Imaging frees the actor from anticipatory bias — facilitatee cannot predict questions.

recommendations.condition: Scene partners have a long shared history that does not appear in the script
recommendations.recommendation: Use Shared Imaging to build that history. One actor shares images of relevant personal experiences; the other images exactly what is described (not parallels). Do this scene-by-scene, also covering extra-textual life between scenes. Consider doing sessions in a hypnagogic state to encourage the shared material to enter dreams.

sources.summary: Shared Imaging builds shared history including extra-textual life; hypnagogic state deepens the bond.

recommendations.condition: Actor arrives at performance depressed, stressed, or emotionally shut down
recommendations.recommendation: Use Fantasy Story Imaging — find a topic that has always been pleasurably memorable (something that speeds up your heartbeat), then create a fully multi-sensory fantasy story based on it. Allow the emotional response to build and amplify. The clown/circus example demonstrates the transformation from depression to joy and love.

sources.summary: Fantasy Imaging transforms depression through pleasurably memorable topic and full sensory Story Image.

recommendations.condition: Actor has a persistent fear (auditions, exposure, ridicule, conflict) that keeps returning
recommendations.recommendation: Begin a daily imaging practice for fear elimination: (1) re-experience the fear's origins fully through sensory imaging until the source is clear; (2) change the image — rewrite the outcome to one of power, success, and confidence. Do this repeatedly over weeks or months. This reaches the unconscious where the fear was originally encoded and reprograms it.

sources.summary: Two-step fear elimination process: re-experience then change the image; works on the unconscious encoding of the fear.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to use Verbalized Imaging but keeps describing images in words rather than experiencing them
recommendations.recommendation: Watch for the distinction: verbal description short-circuits the sensory process. When you verbalize, use minimal language as a trigger ('I see...', 'I smell...') and then let the sense respond sensorially. After verbalizing, reduce and eventually eliminate the words as the imaging becomes self-sustaining. Verbalize only to START the process and to stay on track.

sources.summary: When verbalizing images, do NOT respond in words — keep sensory responses non-verbal or the process short-circuits.

recommendations.condition: Actor has difficulty starting the imaging process
recommendations.recommendation: Use Verbalized Imaging as a starting mechanism: begin by acknowledging the difficulty aloud ('I feel reluctant to start...I'm not sure where to begin...I'm afraid it won't work...'), then sneak into imaging by simply starting 'I see...' and filling in the first thing that comes. The acknowledgment of obstacles clears them so the imaging can begin.

sources.summary: Start Verbalized Imaging by acknowledging the obstacles aloud, then begin 'I see...' — the acknowledgment clears the block.

recommendations.condition: Actor is blocked on the spot before a performance or class and cannot reach a BEING state
recommendations.recommendation: Use I'm Five Years Old imaging: start the prompt 'I'm five years old and I...' and respond with images, not words. Progress through ages. This simultaneously creates selfless involvement, elevates affectability, and piques unconscious memories — tripling the instrumental benefit in a single exercise.

sources.summary: I'm Five Years Old imaging variant: triple benefit for on-the-spot instrumental work.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to build an authentic relationship with a scene partner quickly
recommendations.recommendation: Layer three approaches: (1) Shared Imaging — each shares personal experiences that parallel the play's circumstances; (2) Guided Imaging — facilitate each other through relevant personal experiences without anticipatory bias; (3) Relationship Imaging — while relating to the real partner, silently image modifications in their behavior. Progress to Endowment via Imaging once a foundation is established.

sources.summary: Multiple relationship imaging techniques layered to build authentic relationship.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to build the unconscious background of a character between rehearsals
recommendations.recommendation: Practice Parallel Imaging daily: image yourself living the character's life using the play's actual circumstances. Weave in personal parallels where they resemble the character's experiences. Do this before sleep or in the sleep-wake state for faster unconscious absorption. Keep a journal of which images produce the most impact.

sources.summary: Parallel Imaging for unconscious character background: image oneself in the character's life; personal parallels; absorbs into unconscious.

recommendations.condition: Actor's imagination feels weak, overly literal, or unable to generate creative choices
recommendations.recommendation: Do daily imagination-elevating exercises: Object Imaging (give an ordinary object a complete life and story), Impulsive Object Imaging (allow bizarre, illogical images from ordinary objects), Free-Association Imaging, and Fantasy Imaging. When images become outrageous or nonsensical, celebrate — it means the imagination is hooking into the unconscious.

sources.summary: Bizarre/illogical images in Object Imaging indicate the imagination is beginning to hook into the unconscious.

recommendations.condition: Actor is addressing the time and place obligation and the environment is unfamiliar
recommendations.recommendation: Select a personal place that carries the same emotional and historical meaning as the play's environment — not just visually similar. Consider the character's history in that place. Create it through Imaging with Sense Memory if it is remote; use Imaging Sensations if it is vivid. Put yourself in it; surround yourself with the sensory elements; let the emotional response come from the place itself.

sources.summary: Time and place selection must carry the same meaning to the actor as the play's environment carries to the character; create with full sensory immersion.

recommendations.condition: A scene requires an emotional transition between two distinct states at a specific moment
recommendations.recommendation: Use Fragmented Imaging transitionally: establish the first emotional state with positive sensory fragments in preparation, then at the moment of shift, rapidly generate contrasting fragments (e.g., from bright eyes/laughter → sad eyes/stooped shoulders/gray skin). No words—only sensory flashes. The contrast drives the organic shift.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to fulfill the emotional obligation for a scene requiring heroic grandeur or a specific emotional quality they cannot generate from memory alone
