recommendations.recommendation: Select a piece of music that personally evokes the required emotion and use Imaging Sensations to hear it internally during the scene. Pre-load the music in preparation so it can be maintained internally through performance.

recommendations.condition: Actor must play a character with a psychologically extreme or morally alien background (villain, severe trauma, etc.)
recommendations.recommendation: Build a character background through a series of 5-10 Story Imaging episodes, starting at age five and moving chronologically through formative events. Mix real parallels from your life with imagined events (Believability) so the history integrates with your unconscious. Focus each episode on experiences that create the specific emotional underpinnings (rejection, humiliation, rage) that explain the character's behavior.

recommendations.condition: Actor is building a complex, long-standing character relationship (e.g., father-son over decades)
recommendations.recommendation: Use Fragmented Imaging + Imaging Sensations across multiple selectively emphasized episodes from your relationship with a parallel person in your life. Cover at least 10-15 episodes across different ages and contexts. Begin with Available Stimulus exploration of your scene partner to establish the Endowment foundation simultaneously.

recommendations.condition: Actor is using Affective Memory and the conventional Sense Memory approach feels insufficient to trigger the full emotional response
recommendations.recommendation: Transition from Sense Memory sensory questions to Imaging Sensations mid-process: start with chronological sensory questions (Sense Memory approach) to build the environment, then as all senses begin working simultaneously, allow the questions to cease and let Story Imaging Sensations take over. Repeat the entire Affective Memory process multiple times to compress it and deepen reliability.

recommendations.condition: Scene partner is not stimulating the relationship feelings demanded by the material
recommendations.recommendation: Use Imaging as craft technique with Endowments: after exhausting Available Stimulus (real similarities between partner and your choice), use Fragmented Imaging to sculpt your choice's unique sensory qualities onto the partner. Create a conglomerate endowment so that the partner's actual behavior registers as coming from your choice person.

recommendations.condition: A choice approach (e.g., Endowments, Affective Memory, Objects That Come into Contact with the Body) lacks its own built-in technique
recommendations.recommendation: Use Imaging as the craft technique. Along with Sense Memory and Sensory Suggestion, Imaging can serve as the approach technique for any choice approach that does not have a built-in process. Imaging Sensations is the most versatile form for this purpose.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to use subpersonalities as an acting tool but does not yet know their own selves
recommendations.recommendation: Begin with instrumental voice dialogue with a qualified facilitator. Identify primary selves and disowned selves, develop an aware ego. Only after developing familiarity with the selves should you attempt to use subpersonalities as a craft choice approach. Supplement with journal writing, dreams, and role-playing solo.

recommendations.condition: Actor must play a character who is clearly dominated by a specific energy (obsessive achiever, dictator, victim, warrior, etc.)
recommendations.recommendation: Identify the character's dominant subpersonality, access that self using the most appropriate technique (voice dialogue, imaging, role-playing), and allow it to carry the character's entire sense of life. The subpersonality will know how to respond to other characters without intellectual direction. For secondary relationships, identify which self the character inhabits when relating to each other person and access that self accordingly.

recommendations.condition: Actor is emotionally blocked before rehearsal or performance and cannot access deep feeling
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Primal Moan or Primitive Abandonment as preparation. Assume the fetal position and moan from the lower abdomen (Primal Moan), building progressively louder and deeper. Or use Primitive Abandonment standing, with large animal sounds and total physical commitment. After either exercise, allow quiet assimilation, then move directly into choice work for the material.

recommendations.condition: Actor's work consistently feels shallow, indicates disconnection from unconscious resources
recommendations.recommendation: Establish a daily practice of Personal Inventory, Nonverbal Personal Inventory, and Personal Point of View to elevate consciousness and invite unconscious impulses. Note any abstract or mysterious responses that surface and explore them via Imaging. Add Primal Moan and Primitive Abandonment several times a week. Begin dream journaling and studying the messages of disowned selves.

recommendations.condition: An unconscious impulse surfaces spontaneously during Personal Inventory and seems significant
recommendations.recommendation: Deliberately re-create that impulse through Imaging (Fragmented, Story, or Verbalized). Start anywhere in the experience, allow images to run out, do not edit impulses. Repeat several times. Each re-creation brings the unconscious material closer to conscious awareness and widens the channel.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to access a nonverbal or primal subpersonality that does not respond to verbal techniques
recommendations.recommendation: Use Movement/Dance, Externals (Animals), Drawing/Sculpture, or Primitive Abandonment to access these selves. Experiment with animals that match the needed energy: apes/gorillas for primitive/aggressive selves; big cats for predator energy; reptiles for satanic/instinctual energy; birds vary widely. If solo, use stream-of-consciousness drawing or clay sculpting while in that energy.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to use a vivid or recurring dream as unconscious material for their work
recommendations.recommendation: Record the dream immediately upon awakening. Do not analyze it—re-create it through Imaging starting from the beginning, using all five senses in a relaxed state. At moments of high emotional intensity, stay with and encourage the feelings. Repeat several times. If using as an acting preparation, move directly from the dream re-creation into choice work for the material.

recommendations.condition: Actor is stuck in conceptual or preplanned responses and cannot access organic moment-to-moment impulses
recommendations.recommendation: Use Disconnected Impulsivity (one-person or two-person Switch-Trick). Express impulses at high speed with no logical connections and no premeditation. After the exercise, do Personal Inventory. Note any meaningful items that arose and use them as starting suggestions for a second run (Adding Meaningful Suggestions variant). Practice daily or weekly.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to prime the unconscious as preparation for a rehearsal or performance
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Logy exercise to deliberately induce the hypnagogic state. Lie prone, exaggerate body weight, make 'logy' sounds, approach the sleep threshold. When images arise, be aware of them without directing them. After the exercise, move directly into working for the choice that addresses the material. Alternatively: use one hour of Unconscious Suggestions (Bedrooms, Schoolrooms, etc.) before a monologue or scene.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to build a regular daily practice for deepening unconscious connection
recommendations.recommendation: Establish a rotation of: Personal Inventory + Nonverbal Personal Inventory (daily), Disconnected Impulsivity (daily or several times a week), Logy/hypnagogic practice (nightly as you fall asleep), Dream journal (every morning), Unconscious Suggestions cluster (weekly – bedrooms, schoolrooms, playgrounds, religious experiences), Fears Imaging (periodically), Night Games (bedtime fantasy journeys). Treat this as a permanent life practice, not a temporary exercise.

recommendations.condition: Actor keeps dismissing spontaneous inner voices and images during rehearsal or performance
recommendations.recommendation: Train as a Professional Experiencer: practice acknowledging every inner voice and image during Personal Inventory work. Express them without editing. In scene work: allow any inner voice or image to influence your behavior through the lines. The audience will discover the next moment at the same time you do—which is when unconscious inspiration makes acting transcendent.

recommendations.condition: Actor is playing a psychologically complex character whose inner life seems 'beyond their emotional range'
recommendations.recommendation: Identify the character's dominant psychological theme (suppression, abandonment, rage, etc.). Look in your dream journal for a dream that shares that theme. Re-create the dream through Imaging as preparation. If no such dream exists, order one up: think about the theme throughout the day and give yourself a pre-sleep suggestion before bed. Supplement the dream choice with Active Imagination to dialogue with its key figures.

recommendations.condition: Actor cannot remember early childhood memories that might be relevant to their work
recommendations.recommendation: Begin Perspective Inventory: review yesterday, then expand one week at a time, progressing backward through your life over multiple weeks. Supplement with Abstract Places from Childhood imaging using the Logy exercise as preparation. Use 'I'm five years old and I…' to anchor specific ages. Allow abstract, discontinuous fragments to arise without seeking logical continuity.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to build a repertoire of choices for future use
recommendations.recommendation: Keep a dream journal—record every morning without fail. Work through the full Unconscious Suggestions cluster (bedrooms, schoolrooms, toys, joys, romances, religious experiences, playgrounds, fragments, abstract places). After each imaging session, note which experiences produced the richest emotional responses—these are your most powerful unconscious-based choices.

recommendations.condition: Actor keeps having recurring dreams that feel significant but cannot identify their meaning
recommendations.recommendation: Re-create the dream through Imaging using Symbolic Imaging for the key objects. Apply Active Imagination: place each major figure in the dream in a chair and dialogue with them. Note whether the figure is subjective (part of yourself) or objective (represents another person). Cross-reference the dream's theme with what is happening in your life at present. Share the dream in a group/workshop context for additional insight.

recommendations.condition: Actor has been cast in a major role and has weeks before rehearsal begins
recommendations.recommendation: Begin Programming the Unconscious immediately: 1. Read the play three times (objective → dynamics → character). Start mini-journal. 2. Identify parallel people for each character relationship. 3. Use Fragmented and Story Imaging to create pre-play character history. 4. Begin dream journal; encourage dreams related to the character. 5. Image before sleep using hypnagogic state. 6. Keep programming journal. Use Walter Mitty Option when no real parallel is available.

recommendations.condition: Actor cannot find a real parallel experience for a specific scene's emotional demands
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Walter Mitty Option: create a totally imaginary experience using Imaging (Story or Fragmented), centering yourself in the action repeatedly until the unconscious accepts the imagined event as real. Supplement with Believability (real elements mixed with fantasy). Repeat the imaging many times over multiple sessions.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to use a dream as a performance trigger during a scene
recommendations.recommendation: Identify a strong symbol from the dream (an object, image, or figure). Use Symbolic Imaging to create that symbol as a flash image during the scene. The symbol, having been thoroughly worked in the dream journal and re-creation process, will act as a trigger that liberates the associated unconscious emotion and dimension into the performance.

recommendations.condition: Actor is playing a character whose psychology does not seem to match their own personality at all
recommendations.recommendation: Use Selective Emphasis (John Jones principle): identify the parallel experiences in your own life that DID produce similar emotional underpinnings to the character's, then isolate and enhance ONLY those—deliberately excluding all experiences that produced different or contradictory traits. Remember: we all possess all universal subparts—every character's psychology has a parallel thread somewhere in any human life.

recommendations.condition: Actor has completed Programming the Unconscious and is now in performance
recommendations.recommendation: Trust the foundation that has been built. The unconscious has been imprinted with the parallel experiences. In performance: allow unconscious impulses and inner voices to surface and include them in your behavior through the lines. Use symbolic images from your dream work as flash triggers. The programming will continue to deepen as the run continues.

recommendations.condition: When using the programming process to prepare a role
recommendations.recommendation: Use Step 5 (Imaging Yourself Playing the Part) at any point during the programming process: stand outside yourself and see yourself as the character moving through every environment of the play with total organic reality. Story Imaging works best. This builds confidence and clarifies the character's through-line.

recommendations.condition: When using the hypnagogic state for character programming
recommendations.recommendation: Prepare a specific, pre-planned image before lying down. When the hypnagogic moment arrives, deploy it immediately—there are only a few seconds. Images can be literal, abstract, or symbolic (e.g., for feeling trapped: being bound in a small room, or inside a glass cylinder filling with water). Practice elongating the window. Use the same approach with antidotal images during deprogramming.

recommendations.condition: When integrating dreams into the programming process
recommendations.recommendation: Follow the seven-step blueprint: record all dreams, study them for content and symbols, use dream imagery as character choices, repeatedly re-create significant dreams, plant seeds in the hypnagogic state, watch for disowned subpersonalities appearing in dreams, and steer any lucid dreams into the character's world.

recommendations.condition: When encountering a lucid dream during a programming period
recommendations.recommendation: Gently and lightly steer the dream toward the play's environment and the character's emotional content. Do not be heavy-handed or the dream will break. If this occurs repeatedly, it indicates a strong unconscious-conscious connection is forming.

recommendations.condition: When beginning any unconscious programming journey
recommendations.recommendation: Cultivate and verify a strong eleventh level of consciousness before starting. Know from the outset what you are doing and why. A well-developed eleventh level of consciousness is the safety mechanism that keeps the process creative and sane. If at any time the process does not feel right, stop and adjust.

recommendations.condition: When finishing a production where unconscious programming was used
recommendations.recommendation: Begin the deprogramming process immediately. Use the programming journal to work through every entry in order, contrasting each imaging parallel with your actual reality. Use the mantra repeatedly ('I am not this character; I worked on him for artistic reasons'). Implant antidotal images in the hypnagogic state and order counter-dreams. Trust the unconscious's homeostatic function to support the reset.

recommendations.condition: When no real-life parallel exists for character circumstances
recommendations.recommendation: Apply the Walter Mitty option with Fantasy Story Imaging as a complete alternative. Follow the written scene verbatim in imagination. Use all five senses. Substitute the co-actors, real people, or entirely fictitious people for the other characters. Mix fantasy and reality elements freely. With repetition, the fantasy reality becomes indistinguishable from real experience and programs the unconscious equally effectively.

recommendations.condition: When the actor works with dark or traumatic characters
recommendations.recommendation: Use targeted unconscious suggestions that align with the character's darkest experiences (illness, death, impoverishment, abandonment) to stimulate relevant dreams. Image these suggestions repeatedly before sleep. Record all resulting dreams and use their imagery as character parallels or direct programming material.

recommendations.condition: Actor feels disconnected or unaffectable — not moved by life, people, or scenes
recommendations.recommendation: Begin a daily consciousness-expansion practice: curiosity exercises, deep listening, personal-point-of-view work. Expand awareness of the world before trying to act in it.

sources.summary: Components of consciousness: curiosity, imagination, awareness, sensitivity, perception, interest, knowledge, affinity for life — all must be deliberately developed.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to access unconscious impulses in performance
recommendations.recommendation: First establish a solid being state, then work irreverently with choices. Expanded consciousness — through daily practice of its components — forms the bridge to unconscious impulses.

sources.summary: Three-stage chain: being → irreverence → ultimate consciousness. Cannot skip stages.
More conscious openness → greater access to unconscious impulses.

recommendations.condition: Actor has lost curiosity or feels bored by the world
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Curiosity Expansion Exercise: deliberately ask questions about everything, including things that seem uninteresting. Pursue deductive observation in public places.

sources.summary: Curiosity was suppressed by education and social pressure. It can be deliberately re-awakened by removing those barriers and questioning everything.

recommendations.condition: Actor struggles to listen to scene partners genuinely
recommendations.recommendation: Practice deep listening daily — dissect environmental sounds, listen for what people mean rather than what they say. Develop interest in everything people communicate verbally and vocally.

sources.summary: Listening reveals feeling, personality, and truth behind words. Train ears as instruments of deep reception.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to access unconscious material more consistently, not just by accident
recommendations.recommendation: Undertake the conditioning process: work daily with dream awareness practices, personal inventory, affective memory, and primal exercises. Consistency builds the conditioned conscious-unconscious channel.

sources.summary: The process of establishing the conscious-unconscious relationship is conditioned — daily work paves a road that can be traveled every time you act.
Cumulative process — each day's work adds layers. When the unconscious becomes consistent, the actor is multidimensional and inspired.

recommendations.condition: Actor feels their intuition is unreliable or nonexistent
recommendations.recommendation: Begin acknowledging and acting on intuitive feelings daily. Ask 'What do I feel about this person/situation?' and trust even fleeting responses. Track which intuitions prove accurate.

sources.summary: Intuition is conditioned knowledge, not mysterious gift. It is strengthened through use and acknowledgment.
Many intuitive impulses go unexpressed. Track and acknowledge them — strength comes from use.

recommendations.condition: Actor feels blocked by words — text feels intellectual rather than felt
recommendations.recommendation: Replace the written text with sounds or gibberish to find the emotional impulses, then use the Inner-Outer Monologue technique to reintegrate inner life with the written words.

sources.summary: Sounds and gibberish replace words, eliminating concepts and opening the actor to pure impulse. Inner-Outer Monologue reintegrates inner life with text.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs maximum emotional availability before a demanding scene with extreme emotional requirements
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Ultimate-Consciousness Trio: Primal Moan → Primitive Abandonment → Switch-Trick in Fear Areas, done consecutively without interruption. This is the most powerful single preparation sequence in Morris's system.

sources.summary: The Trio combines all three primal exercises. Done consecutively, the cumulative momentum produces amazing things from a very deep place.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to build a reliable library of choices that access the unconscious
recommendations.recommendation: Begin a Choice Hunt journal: catalogue all experiences, images, fears, and memories that produce strong emotional responses. Test each choice in acting work. Build this repertoire consistently — the larger and more tested it is, the more reliable unconscious access becomes.

sources.summary: Keep a journal listing all strong choices and underlining the ones that reliably access unconscious responses.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to access extreme grief, terror, or helplessness for a role
recommendations.recommendation: Use Affective Memory, selecting the most powerful relevant past experience. Approach it through sensory re-creation of the surrounding context — do not attack the emotional moment directly. The Primal Moan can be done first to lower emotional defenses.

sources.summary: Re-create all surrounding context before approaching the emotional moment. All questions in the here and now.
Morris's own experience with Dark of the Moon: used affective memory of a death/terror experience — the fullest ultimate-consciousness experience he ever had.

recommendations.condition: Two actors need to achieve genuine ensemble before a scene and are starting from cold
recommendations.recommendation: Follow the six-step ensemble preparation sequence: Personal Inventory → Relaxation if needed → Dealing with the Demons → Selfless Involvement → Ensemble Preparation (choose the most appropriate exercise) → Working for the Choice. Don't rush — wait until both actors genuinely feel the ensemble state before transitioning to material.

sources.summary: The six steps provide a systematic pathway from wherever the actors are to a genuine ensemble state.

recommendations.condition: Actor keeps breaking ensemble when transitioning from preparation to the scene material
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Ensemble Workout's progressive structure (silence → sounds → gibberish → words) to make the transition gradual. Also, continue the ensemble preparation silently under the lines of the scene — use 'You Make Me Feel' and Working for a Choice simultaneously, without abruptly switching.

sources.summary: Continue preparation silently under lines to create a bridge between the exercise and the scene. The overlap prevents abrupt switching.
The Ensemble Workout's progressive structure avoids abrupt transitions — each part piggybacks the last.

recommendations.condition: Actor has difficulty maintaining moment-to-moment reality and keeps slipping into premeditated, performed responses
recommendations.recommendation: Return to the Being Workout — acknowledge what is actually happening in the moment, including the fact that you've lost contact. Use 'You Make Me Feel' to rebuild the chain of authentic reactions. Consider using the Believability exercise to re-anchor in real feelings.

sources.summary: When actors short-circuit moment-to-moment reality to service material cerebrally, they stop being real and must return to a genuine starting point.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to build a comprehensive record of their process and choices
recommendations.recommendation: Start an Actor's Daily Journal: record everything of significance seen, felt, thought, and observed every day. Include the choices discovered, exercises done, observations about people, and catalogue of strong choices (especially those that access the unconscious). Carry it everywhere.

sources.summary: The journal chronicles stimuli, breaks them into choices, and serves as a blueprint of the daily work process.

recommendations.condition: Actor doesn't know what to do in a rehearsal
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Obligation-Choice-and-Choice-Approach Chart as the primary organizing framework. Start by identifying all the obligations of the material, then select choices and approaches for each. The chart provides a blueprint to follow from scene to scene.

sources.summary: The chart is a working document that maps obligations to choices and approaches — a clear blueprint for each rehearsal.

recommendations.condition: Actor struggles with a character very different from themselves
recommendations.recommendation: Start from the four-part character analysis. Find all points of similarity first — use craft to elevate those. Then address differences through the three most dimensional characterization approaches: Externals, Affective Memory, or Sub-Personalities.

sources.summary: Find similarities first. Use craft to bring them to the surface. Address differences through the appropriate choice approach.
The three most dimensional characterization approaches: Externals, Affective Memory, Sub-Personalities.

recommendations.condition: Actor is disconnected from their own childhood, making emotional access difficult
recommendations.recommendation: Use the 'I'm Five Years Old' journal — write the journal in first person at each age, beginning with the youngest accessible memory. This unlocks stored emotional experiences that increase vulnerability and availability.

sources.summary: Some actors are completely out of touch with their childhood. The 'I'm Five Years Old' journal helps unlock those stored experiences.

recommendations.condition: Actor has tried a choice but it isn't producing the desired emotional response
recommendations.recommendation: Consider switching choice approaches before abandoning the choice itself. The choice may be right but the approach wrong. Try: (1) Selective Enhancement to amplify real available elements; (2) Sharing Monologue to approach it verbally; (3) Evocative Images for a purely visual approach; or (4) inner monologue to sustain the choice during the scene.

sources.summary: The value of 25 choice approaches: a choice may be impelling but the approach may be wrong for that actor. More options = greater likelihood of success.

recommendations.condition: Actor playing a character with a strong psychological profile very different from their own
recommendations.recommendation: Identify the sub-personality that most closely matches the character's psychological driver. Use journal conversation to invite that sub-personality to express itself. Work for specific choices that appeal to that energy — gradually elevating it over the rehearsal period.

sources.summary: Getting familiar with sub-personalities gives a repertoire for characterization. Identify which one controls the character.
To elicit a specific sub-personality: journal conversation with it, or choices that appeal to and elicit that energy.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to understand a character's physical mannerisms and actions beyond surface choices
recommendations.recommendation: Go to the psychological layer first — the character's inner impulses drive their physical behavior. Once the psychological motivation is identified and created, the physical expression often emerges organically.

sources.summary: Actions and interactions are perhaps the most important way to find out who a character is — they reveal the inner impulses driving behavior.
The psychological area is often the impelling force behind all the other three areas — deal with it first.

recommendations.condition: Actor is working in film with little or no rehearsal time
recommendations.recommendation: Do all exploratory work at home in advance: identify obligations, create the place sensorially, explore character through the four parts, test choice approaches. Know your choices so thoroughly that on-set work can focus on technical demands while the emotional instrument is already conditioned.

sources.summary: Film requires complete preparation before the set. Home work creates the foundation.
Work at home to do most of the preparatory work so that on-set efficiency is maximized.

recommendations.condition: Actor feels blocked during a rehearsal and cannot access the needed emotional life
recommendations.recommendation: Do a Vesuvius — express all frustrations and blocked feelings with full intensity. Then return to the Personal Inventory to find where you actually are. Consider a Primal Moan to lower the emotional defenses before trying the choice again.

sources.summary: When blocked in rehearsal, a Vesuvius expresses all frustrations and clears the instrument.
If you dry up during a monologue, return to the Primal Moan and repeat the process.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to use the unconscious as a resource in rehearsal
recommendations.recommendation: Include UC preparations in rehearsal (Primal Moan, Primitive Abandonment, 'I'm Five Years Old'), review the scene before sleep and encourage dreams about it, stay alert to sleep-wake state suggestions, and maintain a journal log that records any dream-based discoveries.

sources.summary: Before bedtime: review the scene using choices. Encourage dreams about the work. Notice sleep-wake state suggestions. Do UC preparations before rehearsal.

recommendations.condition: Actor feels their work is not evolving across multiple rehearsals
recommendations.recommendation: Maintain a detailed Journal Log after every rehearsal. Record what choices were tried, what worked, what didn't, and what needs to be addressed next time. Use the Obligation-Choice-and-Choice-Approach Chart to ensure all obligations are being systematically addressed, not just the ones that come easily.

sources.summary: Describing failures is as important as recording successes. We learn from failing. The journal makes this learning explicit.
After multiple rehearsals the work becomes simple — conditioned choices work quickly. This only happens with systematic record-keeping and analysis.

recommendations.condition: Actor has lost the joy and playfulness in their acting — it feels like work rather than play
recommendations.recommendation: Return to the magical child. Actively engage in play, imagination exercises, and situations that allow childlike spontaneity. Do fantasies and story-making daily. Reconnect with the 'I-must' games of childhood — acting is playing. The enchanted sense of play is the foundation of acting magic.

sources.summary: Acting is like playing childhood pretend games. The enchanted sense of play is the key. We must re-cultivate the magical years.
The magical child is always inside. How well you treat it determines how often it comes out.

recommendations.condition: Actor believes they cannot achieve inspired, unconscious-level work because they haven't experienced it
recommendations.recommendation: The research shows that most actors have never had a full ultimate-consciousness experience, but all actors can develop the conditioned pathways that make it far more likely. Begin the daily work: consciousness expansion, primal exercises, dream awareness, I'm Five Years Old, affective memory. The conditioning creates the road that can be traveled every time you act.

sources.summary: Most actors have never had the ultimate-consciousness experience. But with the right techniques, a new breed of actors can achieve it with regularity.
The process is conditioned. Daily work paves a road that can be traveled every time you act.
Cumulative process — each day adds layers. When the unconscious becomes consistent, the actor is multidimensional.

recommendations.condition: Director or colleague challenges the actor's process as overcomplicated or unnecessary
recommendations.recommendation: Techniques are the actor's tools. Without tools there is no dependability — the actor must then hope for inspiration and pray for the right circumstances. Process does not replace talent or prevent naturalness; it opens the doors to allow the natural, organic instrument to express itself consistently under any conditions.

sources.summary: Accused of overcomplicating acting with all these involvements and techniques — but techniques are the actor's tools. Without tools there is no dependability.
Oliver's response to Hoffman in The Marathon Man is about not needing all the 'Method' stuff — but the point is that process creates the ability to do it even without the external conditions.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to understand the full integrated system from Morris's perspective
recommendations.recommendation: The system rests on three sequential stages: (1) Being — moment-to-moment truthful flow of impulses; (2) Irreverence — total freedom in choice exploration; (3) Ultimate Consciousness — when conscious work is fed by unconscious impulses. Prerequisites: expanded consciousness (curiosity, awareness, sensitivity, perception, interest, knowledge, imagination, affinity for life). Daily practices: instrumental exercises to free the instrument, craft techniques to fulfill material, UC exercises to condition the conscious-unconscious channel.

sources.summary: Three sequential stages: being → irreverence → ultimate consciousness.
Training in two areas: instrumental (freeing the instrument) and craft (fulfilling material obligations).

recommendations.condition: An actor is experiencing tension before going onstage or on camera
recommendations.recommendation: Acknowledge tension openly rather than fighting or hiding it. Use Dealing with the Demon to track and accept it through Stream of Consciousness. Follow with Selfless Involvement or Observe, Wonder, and Perceive to redirect attention outward. Tension means you care about your work.

sources.summary: Tension can be dealt with through Dealing with the Demon rather than forging ahead. Chase and accept the demon's presence rather than fighting it.

recommendations.condition: An actor feels disconnected from their moment-to-moment impulses during a scene
recommendations.recommendation: Take a Personal Inventory mid-scene. Ask how you feel, acknowledge any imposed behavior, and redirect to real underlying impulses. Use inner monologue to stay on reality track. Imposition stops automatically when Personal Inventory is engaged.

sources.summary: When aware of imposition, take Personal Inventory. Ask 'How do I feel? Am I expressing that? If not, why not?' Automatically stops imposed behavior and directs energy to real impulses.

recommendations.condition: An actor is severely blocked — cannot function, cannot feel anything
recommendations.recommendation: Begin with Abandonment (large sounds and movements, no form, 2-3 minutes). Follow with large commitment exercises like Yeah, Yeah! Then do Personal Inventory to assess state before proceeding to craft work.

sources.summary: Abandonment can transform completely frozen actors. After large commitment exercises, actors begin to function well.

recommendations.condition: An actor's ego state is low due to inactivity or professional rejection
recommendations.recommendation: Do Sense of Worth, Count Your Blessings, and Superman/Superwoman exercises. Continue daily work even without employment — daily work is itself the most powerful ego restorer. Diversify creative energy through painting, writing, or directing.

sources.summary: Ego-nourishing activity is work. Daily homework vital to ego state. Amazing how good you feel after a week of working every day.

recommendations.condition: An actor cannot find the right choice for a scene's emotional obligation
recommendations.recommendation: Use Task Inventory: clearly identify the obligation, recall a time you felt that way, identify what caused those feelings, and use those specific causes as the choice approach. If still stuck, try Affective Memory on a directly relevant past experience.

sources.summary: Task Inventory: identify obligation, recall similar emotional experience, identify its cause, use those specifics as choice.

recommendations.condition: An actor fears their fear will be detected by the director and crew
recommendations.recommendation: Understand that fear means you care about your work — it is a mark of the serious artist, not amateurism. Admit fear openly (leveling). Then use Dealing with the Demon and Selfless Involvement to redirect. The elaborate defense systems of hiding fear take three times the energy of dealing with it.

sources.summary: Fear is destructive only when undealt with. Most actors create complex defense systems. But fear means having something at stake — it means caring about the work.

recommendations.condition: A director is giving result direction rather than process guidance
recommendations.recommendation: Translate result directions into internal obligations. When told 'be angrier,' find what specific choice would stimulate that anger in you. Communicate to the director in terms of what you are working for, building collaboration through process language.

sources.summary: Result direction: director blueprints emotions and asks actor to deliver. Actor must find the internal choice equivalent of the result requested.

recommendations.condition: An actor finds sense memory work tedious and boring
recommendations.recommendation: Add whimsy — ask imaginative questions like 'If this cup of coffee could speak, how would it sound?' or 'If it had personality, what would it be like?' Make the work playful. When getting grim, it is time to play.

sources.summary: When sense memory gets tedious, ask whimsical questions about the object. Rule: when getting grim and avoiding work, it's time to play.

recommendations.condition: An actor feels restricted and self-conscious when trying to say the exact written dialogue
recommendations.recommendation: Continue working for choices and creating emotional life while paraphrasing the dialogue. Once the organic life is flowing, gradually return to exact text. Alternatively, break the lines into impulse units and approach each unit sensorially.

sources.summary: Options for dialogue restriction: paraphrase while working for choices, then return to text once emotional life is established.

recommendations.condition: An actor is shooting a film out of sequence and needs to maintain character consistency
recommendations.recommendation: Create a Continuity Chart before filming begins. Before each scene, re-create the appropriate emotional base using the chart as guide. Use sense memory and choice work to enter the correct emotional state for each scene's position in the story.

sources.summary: Continuity chart: since films shoot out of sequence, map emotional content scene by scene. Create appropriate impulses and emotions for each scene using this chart.

recommendations.condition: An actor is dealing with another actor who is unresponsive or uninvolved
recommendations.recommendation: Use the available reality of the other actor through Selfless Involvement and Creative Leadership. If filming and other actor won't relate, follow their eyes until they must connect, or do something unexpected that forces an impulsive response. Always stay professional and on script.

sources.summary: With unresponsive actor: follow their eyes until they must relate, or do something unexpected that forces impulsive response. Be prepared to carry the ball.

recommendations.condition: An actor is going on an audition or interview
recommendations.recommendation: In the outer office, do not 'out-cool' others — allow your real state to be present. In the inner office, if anxious, let it show authentically: 'I feel a little anxious; I really want this part.' Ask for a couple of minutes to work for choices that match what the character feels. BE rather than perform.

sources.summary: In interviews: let real state show, admit anxiety if present, ask for time to work for choices matching what character feels at that time.

recommendations.condition: An actor is preparing a cold reading for an audition
recommendations.recommendation: Step 1: Read first for syntax and style — make no emotional decisions. Step 2: Identify relationships and what characters are doing and feeling. Step 3: Identify emotional obligations. Step 4: Work for choices. Only then incorporate available realities in the room.

sources.summary: Cold reading: first read for syntax; second for understanding relationships; third for identifying emotional obligations; then work for choices.

recommendations.condition: An actor is experiencing professional inactivity and depression
recommendations.recommendation: Deal with economics first — get work to fulfill financial obligations and self-respect. Exercise the body. Work on instrument and craft 4-5 hours daily. Diversify creative energy (paint, write, direct). Get into a class or play. Fill life with active, productive people.

sources.summary: Help yourself: deal with economics, exercise body, work on instrument/craft 4-5 hours daily, diversify creativity, get into class or play, fill life with productive people.

recommendations.condition: An actor receives criticism that is painful to hear
recommendations.recommendation: Separate ego from work. Listen to the critique for technical content rather than as a judgment of personal worth. A solid craft foundation allows objective assessment of whether criticism is accurate and perceptive or merely a carping put-down.

sources.summary: When criticism is painful: separate ego from work. Those who fail to do so miss helpful craft pointers by responding as if personally attacked.

recommendations.condition: An actor wants to develop their imagination
recommendations.recommendation: Do daily exercises that specifically stimulate and challenge the imagination: Word Game, Must Game, and creative play. Treat imagination as a muscle — exercise it daily or it becomes flabby. Be open to everything and observe everything as a student of human behavior.

sources.summary: Developing imagination: instrumental involvement. Daily exercises challenging imagination are essential. Actors who stop working on imagination level off into predictable work.

recommendations.condition: An actor is doing characterization work
recommendations.recommendation: Do not think of the character as a different person. Find in yourself the impulses, feelings, and behaviors that correspond to what the character experiences. Use selective emphasis to bring forward the parts of yourself that match the character. Everything onstage must come from you.

sources.summary: Mistake: separating yourself from character. When this happens the game is over. Everything onstage must come from you. Find your own basis for the character's behavior.

recommendations.condition: An actor notices they have developed mannerisms and behavioral dependencies
recommendations.recommendation: Take Personal Inventory to identify the difference between what you really feel and what you are imposing. Challenge yourself to express impulses rather than habitual patterns. Seek scary feedback from trusted people about patterns they observe in your work.

sources.summary: Mannerisms: external behavioral expressions with no connection to inner reality. Actor unconsciously adopts things that 'work' and multiplies them into a bag of tricks.

recommendations.condition: An actor wants to maintain career integrity under commercial pressure
recommendations.recommendation: Maintain values, principles, and unique point of view even when commercial pressure suggests compromising them. Develop true talent rather than conforming to market expectations. Integrity and developed unique perspective are what create lasting contributions.

sources.summary: Maintain integrity and principles. 'If it means giving up your soul, drive a taxi.' True talent and unique point of view create work that outlasts commercial product.

recommendations.condition: An actor is avoiding daily practice due to tedium or lack of immediate reward
recommendations.recommendation: Understand that daily work is like building a bank account — payoff is cumulative. Make the work enjoyable by varying exercises and adding playfulness. Approach each day's practice as if going to a job that is a joy. Daily work dramatically improves ego state.

sources.summary: Few actors become master craftsmen because most are in it for the brass ring. Joy in the creative process is the essential ingredient. Vary exercises and make work playful.

recommendations.condition: An actor is apathetic — feeling fine but not stimulated or affectable by anything
recommendations.recommendation: Recognize apathy as a deceptive insulation — it is not peace, it is emotional protection. Diagnose the specific problem with Personal Inventory. Use expurgative exercises to break through. Do not accept apathy as your baseline.

sources.summary: Apathy: deceptive — you think everything is fine when actually insulated. Protecting from feeling anything because pain is avoided. This insulation is not okay for an actor.

recommendations.condition: An actor is trying to misuse rehearsal as a cumulative performance
recommendations.recommendation: Reframe rehearsal as exploration. Major involvement during rehearsal should be with the choice, not with expressing the meaning of the material simultaneously. Allow failure and experimentation. Work for choices in isolation first, then integrate.

sources.summary: Misusing rehearsal as cumulative performance leaves no room for experimentation and failure. Proper rehearsal is exploration.

recommendations.condition: An actor has difficulty trusting their impulses enough to make the first committed expression
recommendations.recommendation: Work with Trust exercises and impulsivity work. Take the chance of doing nothing — allow the emptiness rather than filling it with planned behavior. Trust that genuine impulses will arise when you are prepared and present. Impulsivity work trains the habit of following impulses rather than overriding them.

sources.summary: Trust: difficulty making the first big commitment of expression. All doubts saying 'it's going to sound phony.' But this commentary short-circuits BEING. Must trust the impulse.

recommendations.condition: An actor wants to develop a balanced daily work program
recommendations.recommendation: Structure daily work to include: relaxation exercises (vary: Rag Doll, Logy, Weight and Gravity), Personal Inventory, sensitizing, minimum 30 minutes sense memory, impulsivity work, ego preparations as needed, and craft work with material. Give equal time to instrument and craft. Vary the exercises to avoid tedium.

sources.summary: Balanced work program: variety in relaxation exercises, daily sense memory minimum 30 minutes, Personal Inventory always, equal time to instrument and craft.

recommendations.condition: An actor is studying with a teacher or workshop and wants to make the most of it
recommendations.recommendation: Choose a class that most piques your interest and challenges your instrument. Do not misuse class as the sole source of feedback. Do homework every day. Do scene work every week. Use class to identify instrumental problems and get guidance on eliminating them. Take and use all productive criticism.

sources.summary: Workshop: artist must have a place to fail. Choose based on what challenges instrument. Visit several, audit, judge for yourself.

recommendations.condition: Actor experiences chronic physical tension before or during acting obligations (auditions, rehearsals, performances)
recommendations.recommendation: Use tension-release exercises (Logy, Tense and Relax, Rag Doll, Deep Breathing, Stretch and Yawn, Weight and Gravity) for immediate relief, then work on addressing root causes of tension (fears, self-esteem, conditioning) throughout training.

sources.summary: Tension exercises are temporary fixes; root causes must be addressed for permanent elimination.

recommendations.condition: Actor has very large tension that ordinary relaxation techniques cannot break through
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Terrific Trio (Abandonment, Vesuvius, Exorcism) — can be done in a full version at home or in class, or a 'mini' version in private (e.g., a restroom stall before an audition).

sources.summary: Terrific Trio used when other approaches don't work; Abandonment alone sometimes enough.
Mini abandonment successfully used before audition.

recommendations.condition: Actor experiences mental tension (racing thoughts, fears, mental distractions) even after physical tension has been released
recommendations.recommendation: Use Imaging to Relaxation (creating peaceful multisensory scenes), focused attention on an environmental object to erase thoughts, or meditation techniques.

sources.summary: Mental tension requires different approach: imaging, object focus, or meditation.

recommendations.condition: Actor resists or fights against their feeling of tension or self-consciousness, making it worse
recommendations.recommendation: Use Allow, Permit, Accept and Include — verbally acknowledge and accept each feeling without trying to eliminate it. Best done in front of a group.

sources.summary: Resistance amplifies tension; acceptance reduces it.

recommendations.condition: Actor fears exposing private feelings or being vulnerable in performance
recommendations.recommendation: Begin Intimate Sharing and Exposure with one trusted friend. Progressively expand the circle of disclosure over time until class-level sharing is comfortable.

sources.summary: Fear of exposure addressed progressively from trusted friend outward.

recommendations.condition: Actor has difficulty being physically or emotionally intimate in scenes
recommendations.recommendation: Begin with animals, then children, then close friends — expressing genuine affection at each stage before advancing. Repeat 'I love you' in real-life conversations to build the habit.

sources.summary: Progressive intimacy exposure: animals → children → friends → broader expressions of love.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to deepen consciousness and eliminate blind spots about their own obstacles
recommendations.recommendation: Develop all five components of consciousness daily: train Observation by deliberately registering environments; expand Awareness of world events; practice Curiosity by asking why about everything; make a daily Commitment to the work; and actively build Information across many subjects.

sources.summary: Consciousness is prerequisite for identifying and eliminating obstacles.
Five components of consciousness described.

recommendations.condition: Actor feels inadequate to meet a specific character or scene obligation
recommendations.recommendation: Do Acknowledging Your Accomplishments — audibly list all craft knowledge and techniques available to address that specific obligation. Then, if imagination-facile, Image a Positive Outcome using all five senses.

sources.summary: Fear of inadequacy in specific area: list accomplishments, use imaging for positive outcome.

recommendations.condition: Actor plays it safe, avoids taking chances, and produces predictable, unexciting work
recommendations.recommendation: Address fear of criticism and judgment through The Round (Ego Reconstruction, Reluctancy, Feedback, Validation) and Accepting Critique exercises. Use Be Critical of Everyone to understand the other side of criticism.

sources.summary: Fear of criticism causes safe, predictable work. Antidotes: Round, Accepting Critique, Be Critical.

recommendations.condition: Actor has fear of embarrassment and refuses to risk unpredictable behavior
recommendations.recommendation: Use cluster of embarrassment exercises progressively: Silly Dilly → Go Crazy → Distortion Exercise → Outrageous Monologue → Slot Machine → Professor Irwin Kory. After experiencing these extremes, scene obligations feel safe by comparison.

sources.summary: Fear of embarrassment cluster of exercises makes scene behavior seem tame by comparison.

recommendations.condition: Actor takes rejection personally and becomes inhibited or people-pleasing after auditions
recommendations.recommendation: Practice Objectifying Rejection mentally (analyzing what factors actually drive casting decisions). Use Objective Affirmations daily to separate personal worth from professional outcome. Apply all fear of criticism and self-esteem exercises.

sources.summary: Fear of rejection: objectify decisions, separate identity from profession, daily affirmations.

recommendations.condition: Actor bluffs on set rather than admitting ignorance
recommendations.recommendation: Practice 'I Don't Know' Exercise and 'I Don't Know and Ask to Be Informed' — both in class and in daily life. Long-term: develop consciousness through reading, curiosity, and awareness of the world.

sources.summary: Fear of appearing ignorant: practice 'I don't know.' Long-range: elevate consciousness.

recommendations.condition: Actor is emotionally protected and cannot access vulnerability in performance
recommendations.recommendation: Use Primal Moan (fetal position, 6-7 min), Coffin Monologue, Deathbed Monologue, and Intimate Sharing — repeatedly over time. Also Rock and Stroke (with partner) and I'm Just a Little Boy/Girl.

sources.summary: Fear of vulnerability: multiple antidotal exercises required, must be repeated over time.

recommendations.condition: Actor is blocked in the area of conflict — cannot express confrontational or aggressive feelings organically
recommendations.recommendation: First do a Sharing Monologue identifying the origin of the conflict obstacle. Then use: Conflict Encounters, Accuse and Indict, Virginia Woolf (one or two person), Anger and Rage Expression, and Imaginary Monologues in Conflict Areas.

sources.summary: Conflict block: identify origin first, then use direct conflict exercises.

recommendations.condition: Actor is afraid of losing control emotionally at moments of peak feeling
recommendations.recommendation: In safe environments (class, workshop, rehearsal), push to maximum emotional heights repeatedly with very impacting stimuli. At moments of internal commentary, immediately reinvest in the choice/stimulus. Experience accumulates evidence that control is not lost.

sources.summary: Fear of losing control: experience in safe places; reinvest at commentary moment. Evidence accumulates.

recommendations.condition: Actor is depressed and cannot access creative energy
recommendations.recommendation: Start with Personal Inventory to identify the cause. Then use: Litany of Accomplishments, Imaging a State of Well-being, Daily Work Schedule, and Perspective Inventory.

sources.summary: Depression as obstacle: Personal Inventory first, then litany of accomplishments and imaging well-being.

recommendations.condition: Actor has difficulty accessing social aggression and conflict in dramatic scenes
recommendations.recommendation: Use progression of social obstacle exercises: Antisocial Tirade → Antisocial Vesuvius → Irrational Tantrum → Network Exercise → This Is What I Usually Say vs. What I Really Want to Say → Personal Inventory II in daily life. Also use Virginia Woolf and conflict exercises from previous section.

sources.summary: Social obstacles: multiple exercises target different levels of antisocial expression.

recommendations.condition: Actor has sexual obstacles or blocks that limit their presence, appeal, or ability to handle sexual material
recommendations.recommendation: Progress through: Sensualizing → Blatant Sexuality → Creating Sexual Fantasies Publicly → Raunch and Sleaze → Ask for Sexual Feedback. Also choose scenes/monologues with explicit sexual content (e.g., 'Nuts' for women, 'Shampoo' for men).

sources.summary: Sexual obstacles: public exercises most effective. Conditioning took a lifetime; requires commitment and repetition.

recommendations.condition: Actor's body is unexpressive or physically inhibited
recommendations.recommendation: Use physical freedom exercises: Power Preparation, Ingestation, Body Talk, Animation and Freedom, Fencing, Shadowboxing, Classical Actor Workout, Primitive Abandonment, Superman/Superwoman, Externals. Repeat many times.

sources.summary: Physical blocks: multiple exercises required, repeated many times.

recommendations.condition: Actor has difficulty with physical and emotional intimacy with scene partners
recommendations.recommendation: Use: Intimate Sharing and Exposure (one and two person), Rock and Stroke (two and group versions), Sentence Completion Exercise with intimacy-focused sentence stems. Also apply general fear of intimacy progression from earlier section.

sources.summary: Intimacy blocks: multiple exercises address progressive intimacy exposure.

recommendations.condition: Actor cannot be genuinely emotionally affected by stimuli (impressive block)
recommendations.recommendation: Use: Personal Inventory, Personal Point of View, I Am/I Want/I Need/I Feel, Primal Moan, I'm Five Years Old and I—, Sharing Monologues (emotional emphasis), Imaginary Monologues, Surround Yourself with Impacting Choices, Allow/Permit/Accept/Include.

sources.summary: Impressive inhibitions: multiple exercises to increase affectability. Must be repeated.

recommendations.condition: Actor feels everything but cannot express it (expressive insecurity)
recommendations.recommendation: Apply all fear-of-emotion exercises, plus: Passions Workout, Litany of Woes, Expressing Fears/Love/Hate, Get the Dime, Dumps, Power Preparation, Ingestation, Irrational Tantrum.

sources.summary: Expressive inhibitions: all fear-of-emotion exercises apply plus additional expressive exercises.

recommendations.condition: Actor feels intellectually insecure or limited
recommendations.recommendation: Acknowledge and objectify first. Identify whether origin is psychological damage (seek therapy/consciousness work) or education gap (take classes, read daily). Build vocabulary through dictionary reading. Ask 'how, why, what' habitually. Practice saying 'I don't know' without shame.

sources.summary: Intellectual insecurities: objectify, identify source, get education if needed, build vocabulary, practice 'I don't know.'

recommendations.condition: Actor has chronic self-esteem problems that return every time they act or audition
recommendations.recommendation: All ego/self-esteem exercises (Count Blessings, Superman, Evangelist, Affirmations, Taking Your Due, etc.) provide temporary relief — use them consistently before each acting obligation. To address the root cause permanently, identify specific damaging historical events and begin the Repairing the Damage process (Re-Creating the Experience, Second Chance, Verbal Approach).

sources.summary: Self-esteem exercises: temporary. Repairing the Damage: permanent. Both needed.

recommendations.condition: Actor has identified a specific historical experience causing lasting damage to self-esteem
recommendations.recommendation: Use Re-Creating the Experience (primary method) — recall, re-create sensorially, then modify to positive version. Supplement with Second-Chance Exercise (imaginary monologue saying what wasn't said) and Verbal Approach (repeating modified version aloud). Also use Appeals to the Unconscious (before sleep), dreams, and hypnagogic state. Must repeat many times over a period of time.

sources.summary: Full repair process: multiple methods, repeated many times. Permanent reprogramming of unconscious.

recommendations.condition: Actor has lost imaginative spontaneity and has difficulty believing in imagined circumstances
recommendations.recommendation: Begin daily inner child resurrection practice: revisit childhood environments, events, friends, and toys sensorially. Maintain episodic fantasies. Engage in pretend play with physical props. Also use Choice Hunts, I'm Five Years Old, and I'm Just a Little Boy/Girl exercises.

sources.summary: Inner child resurrection: daily practice required. Lost capacity is not permanent.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs a quick ego boost before an audition or performance
recommendations.recommendation: Use: Magic Pocket (can be done anywhere), Accepting the Academy Award, Superman/Superwoman, Evangelist or Rainmaker, Validate and Affirm Yourself, Count Blessings and Accomplishments.

sources.summary: Multiple quick ego-boost exercises available; choice depends on actor's needs.

recommendations.condition: Actor builds self-esteem as an ongoing daily practice
recommendations.recommendation: Maintain: Affirmations (several times daily with 'I deserve'), Self-Esteem Journal (daily entries + daily/weekly review), Two-Person Validation Workouts (with trusted partner), Progress Report (regularly), Perspective Inventory (weekly).

sources.summary: Affirmations: daily mantra. Journal: daily entries. Validation: with partners. Ongoing self-esteem cultivation.

recommendations.condition: Actor recognizes that he repeats the same vocal coloring or emotional tone in every performance, or that his voice sounds manipulated rather than organic
recommendations.recommendation: Work to identify the vocal dependency. Begin with Personal Inventory to connect with real moment-to-moment feelings, then carry those feelings into the lines. Use Nonverbal Imaginary Monologues, the One-Sound Exercise, and 'I Feel...' Then Say the Line to rebuild the connection between feeling and vocal expression.

recommendations.condition: Actor repeats the same physical mannerisms across all performances or has physical habits that operate automatically
recommendations.recommendation: Become conscious of the specific mannerisms and watch for them daily. The primary antidote is training to work from a being state — when the actor is fully in the moment and using a choice process, physical dependencies naturally diminish because the concentration goes to the real impetus for behavior.

recommendations.condition: Actor is prone to emotional dependencies — compulsive laughing, crying, raging — or other repeated emotional shticks
recommendations.recommendation: Recognize the dependency as a replacement for real impulses. Work with Personal Inventory to find out what is really being felt, and use Moment-to-Moment Inclusions to express those real feelings instead of defaulting to the familiar emotional response.

recommendations.condition: Actor functions primarily from an intellectual place — 'mind-mouth connection' — and has difficulty stimulating genuine emotion
recommendations.recommendation: Address the intellectual dependency by returning to instrumental foundations. Use Personal Inventory, Being Irreverent to Concept of the Material, and Moment-to-Moment Inclusions. The actor may also need to work on fear of emotion and emotional blocks from earlier in the system.

recommendations.condition: Actor observes splits — body disconnected from emotion, voice carrying different emotion than body, or expression driven entirely by intellect
recommendations.recommendation: Splits signal that real impulses are being suppressed. Stop and do Personal Inventory to find out what is genuinely being felt. Include those impulses in the expression. Do not attempt to restore the 'correct' emotion through willpower — that will only recreate the split.

recommendations.condition: Actor is compensating — imposing a relaxed demeanor over real tension, or substituting one emotion for another that feels more acceptable
recommendations.recommendation: Begin with selfless involvement to get out of yourself. Use Observe, Perceive, and Wonder with the other actor or environment. This shifts concentration outward and dissolves the internal monitoring that drives compensational behavior. Then use a choice to address the scene's emotional obligations.

recommendations.condition: Actor is redirecting authentic impulses into more comfortable emotional expressions, creating a split between what is felt and what is expressed
recommendations.recommendation: Become aware of the redirection — the first step is consciousness. Then do an inner monologue with Personal Inventory to reconnect with the real impulse. Once reconnected, reinvest in the choice to get back to addressing the scene's obligations. If the redirection stems from an inhibition, identify and address the specific block using the relevant instrumental exercises.

recommendations.condition: Actor is conceptually attached to the material — imposing predetermined emotional ideas about how the piece should be performed
recommendations.recommendation: Use the anticoncept exercises in sequence: Inner-Outer Monologue to reveal the gap between concept and real feeling; 'I Feel...' Then Say the Line to ground each line in organic feeling; Disconnected Impulsivity to break conceptual thinking patterns; and Potpourri of Monologues to demonstrate that organic acting transcends any specific text.

recommendations.condition: Actor is premeditating — planning how to say lines and emotionalize them before getting there, functioning from a cerebral place
recommendations.recommendation: In the midst of premeditated expression, ask what you are really feeling right now and include those impulses instead of saying the next line as planned. Use the same anticoncept exercises. Trust that organic impulses are more compelling than premeditated ones, even when they feel irreverent to the scene.

recommendations.condition: Actor is commenting on his own performance while performing, creating a split between observation and involvement
recommendations.recommendation: Turn the commentary into an asset by including the impulses that arise from the commentary in the expression itself. Do not suppress the commentary — include it. In almost all cases the expression of commentary is acceptable behavior for the character in that circumstance.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to prepare instrumentally before a rehearsal
recommendations.recommendation: Follow the full instrumental preparation sequence: (1) Relaxation exercises to address obvious and subliminal tension. (2) Expanded Personal Inventory to assess current emotional state versus where the scene requires him to be. (3) Address any gap with specific exercises (imaginary monologue, Observe/Perceive/Wonder, etc.). (4) If working with a partner, do the Two-Person Being Workout.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to prepare instrumentally for an audition
recommendations.recommendation: Work through the audition preparation sequence: relaxation exercises → ego/self-esteem check (Count Your Blessings, Pollyanna workout if needed) → selfless involvement in the waiting room with the actual environment and people present. The instrumental preparation must be complete before addressing the craft obligations of the material.

recommendations.condition: Actor feels disconnected from real feelings or is 'naturally' acting—imitating truth rather than experiencing it
recommendations.recommendation: Begin daily practice of the Emotional-Point-of-View Inventory to build genuine moment-to-moment responsiveness and reconnect with authentic impulses.

sources.summary: Daily use of inventory promotes BEING and is the starting place for the craft process.

recommendations.condition: Actor intellectualizes emotions or describes feelings rather than expressing them
recommendations.recommendation: Practice the Emotional Point of View, Without Words (Nonverbal) exercise daily. Bypass verbal processing entirely to access real impulse fabric.

sources.summary: Nonverbal version prevents intellectualization; words often describe rather than express feelings.

recommendations.condition: Actor notices split between what they feel and what they perform; suppresses non-scene-consistent impulses
recommendations.recommendation: Practice the Inclusion Exercise with a memorized monologue. Allow all impulses to ventilate through the text without concern for fulfilling meaning.

sources.summary: Refusing to include real impulses creates short-circuiting; inclusion creates compelling, unpredictable performance.

recommendations.condition: Actor's inner life disconnects from scripted text during scene work
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Inner-Outer Monologue technique in rehearsal: alternate between written lines and personal inner monologue, ensuring the inner life carries over into the written text.

sources.summary: Inner-Outer Monologue bridges gap between personal reality and scripted text; good rehearsal tool.

recommendations.condition: Two actors preparing to rehearse a scene together; both feel artificial or disconnected
recommendations.recommendation: Begin with the BEING Workout: face each other and express all obstacles, fears, and concerns on a moment-to-moment basis until both feel genuinely comfortable.

sources.summary: BEING Workout is recommended first step when rehearsing with another actor; achieves threshold of real life.

recommendations.condition: Actor struggles with fear of exposure or inability to be genuinely vulnerable on stage
recommendations.recommendation: Practice Intimate Sharing with another actor. Sharing personal material addresses the fear of exposure that blocks BEING freedom.

sources.summary: Fear of exposure is major BEING block; Intimate Sharing exercise directly addresses it.

recommendations.condition: Actor is falling into result consciousness—going for the emotion of a scene rather than working from the moment
recommendations.recommendation: Switch from DOING consciousness to BEING consciousness. Start from a real state of life, work for a choice that genuinely affects you, and irreverently follow impulses wherever they lead. Trust that the natural human response will occur.

sources.summary: Antidote to result consciousness: start from real state of life, work for a real choice, allow natural response.

recommendations.condition: Actor resists the irreverent approach because of fear of judgment from directors, peers, or the conventional acting community
recommendations.recommendation: Use ego preparations (Making My Statement, Due-Taking, Feeling Special) before rehearsal. Communicate your process to the director. Stand your ground—process is worth fighting for.

sources.summary: Stand your ground with process even under criticism; communicate approach to director.
Ego preparations help establish right to work irreverently.

recommendations.condition: Actor is too tied to line readings or the conceptual meaning of the written text in rehearsal
recommendations.recommendation: Use rehearsal irreverence techniques: Sounds with a Choice, Gibberish, Paraphrasing Improvisation, or Nursery Rhymes. These techniques break the grip of text-concept and allow impulse-based exploration.

sources.summary: These techniques eliminate concept and premeditation during rehearsal exploration.

recommendations.condition: Actor's impulsive flow is interrupted during a long run or performance; self-consciousness creeping in
recommendations.recommendation: Immediately use Personal Inventory as an Inner Monologue under the lines of the scene. Once organic groove is restored, reinvest in the original choice.

sources.summary: Personal Inventory run as Inner Monologue is the antidote when impulsive flow is interrupted in performance.
After regaining organic groove, reinvest in original choice.

recommendations.condition: A choice has lost its strength after repeated use in a long run
recommendations.recommendation: Add fresh sensory elements to the existing choice: new, emotionally and sensorially exciting questions. This can be done during performance itself since the basic choice remains the same.

sources.summary: When choices lose strength, add fresh sensory elements; can be done during performance.

recommendations.condition: Actor is playing a one-dimensional character or character work feels superficial—expressing the emotion rather than creating the impetus
recommendations.recommendation: Use Antithetical Explorations: spend rehearsal time exploring the character's opposite qualities, stimulate organic loving/tender impulses, then find organic reasons within the scene to be rejected so resentments and the character's behavior build authentically.

sources.summary: Antithetical Explorations discover underlife and fragment concepts; avoid superficial behavior.
Build character's actual behavior from organic frustration of antithetical impulses.

recommendations.condition: Actor questions whether including an impulse in performance is appropriate or misinterprets the material
recommendations.recommendation: Apply the irreverence test: 'Is it not possible for the character to behave or respond in that way at that moment?' If yes, it is permissible. It is an extremely rare occurrence when the answer would be no.

sources.summary: The test for inclusion in performance: ask whether the character could possibly behave that way.

recommendations.condition: Actor feels tension or self-consciousness before or during a scene
recommendations.recommendation: Begin with the Standard Relaxation Cluster: Weight-and-Gravity Relaxation, Deep Breathing, Stretching and Yawning. Follow with Personal Inventory. Involvement is the natural enemy of tension—use Available-Stimulus Sweep or Wonder/Perceive/Observe.

sources.summary: Relaxation is the starting point; the only place from which an artist can create.
Involvement is the natural enemy of tension and self-consciousness.

recommendations.condition: Actor is disconnected from their scene partner; relationship feels artificial or the actor is not genuinely curious about them
recommendations.recommendation: Use Wonder, Perceive, and Observe—ideally before the scene, but it can be run silently throughout. Also consider Sensory Exploration aligned with the first emotional obligation of the scene.

sources.summary: Wonder/Perceive/Observe takes actor into the other person and establishes foundation of relationship.
Sensory Exploration used as involvement prelude to first choice in scene.

recommendations.condition: Actor's senses are sluggish and unresponsive during sensory choice work
recommendations.recommendation: Intersperse Sensitizing while working for the choice in the scene. 'Live' in the unresponsive sense with concentrated attention until it tingles with responsiveness, then reinvest in the sensory questions for the original choice.

sources.summary: Sensitizing used when senses are sluggish during choice exploration; intersperse it while actively working for the choice.

recommendations.condition: Actor's ego state is significantly lower than the character's—character is supremely confident, powerful, or dominant
recommendations.recommendation: Use Superman or Joy-and-Euphoria as a craft preparation. Commit to the exercise at the extreme. Continue until in a significantly different ego state closer to the character's.

sources.summary: Superman for large ego obligations; Joy and Euphoria stretches instrument to expressive extreme.

recommendations.condition: Actor is hard on themselves and struggling with self-criticism; needs to build self-appreciation to match character's positive self-view
recommendations.recommendation: Use Counting Your Blessings or Progress Reports as a craft preparation. Must be based on genuine truth—don't embellish. Follow with Two-Person Validations with scene partner.

sources.summary: Counting Your Blessings: good for actors hard on themselves.
Progress Reports: must not embellish reality; must be genuinely believed.

recommendations.condition: The character is emotionally extreme (volatile, irrational, rageful) and the actor needs a large emotional foundation
recommendations.recommendation: Use large expurgatives: Abandonment (with specific content for the character's emotional life), Vesuvius (with content aimed at building needed threshold), or Go Crazy (to free all restrictions). For underlying anger covered by controlled exterior, use Anger-and-Rage Expression first, then find organic reason to cover it.

sources.summary: Large expurgatives for large emotional obligations; not for subtle levels.
Anger-and-Rage: create underlife of anger first, then find organic justification to cover it.

recommendations.condition: Actor's relationship with scene partner feels artificial, uninvested, or routine; the emotional relationship demanded by material is not being felt
recommendations.recommendation: Use relationship preparations: Nonverbal Relating Exercise, Wonder/Perceive/Observe (adjusted for the specific relationship quality needed), Looking for New Things in the Partner, or Two-Person Validations. Choose based on what specific relational quality is missing.

sources.summary: Nonverbal communication removes word-barrier and deepens direct emotional contact.
Wonder/Perceive/Observe for relationship: adjust to promote specific relationship emotional point of view needed.

recommendations.condition: Actor is working on a scene and needs to identify what to work on—which obligations are most pressing
recommendations.recommendation: Systematically check all seven obligations: Time-and-Place, Relationship, Emotional, Character, Historic, Thematic, Subtextual. Identify which ones are not yet being felt organically. Prioritize based on what the material's most impelling element is.

sources.summary: Seven obligations as a blueprint; importance and order of approach vary with each piece of material.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to fulfill the emotional obligation but keeps playing surface emotion without the underlying need and passion
recommendations.recommendation: Build the emotional pyramid from the bottom: first work for choices that stimulate the CHARACTER'S DESIRE (the passion and need), then find choices that create the OBSTACLES to that desire. The conflict between these creates the emotional experience naturally.

sources.summary: Must work for passion and need first, then obstacles; the conflict between them produces the emotional life organically.
Without the passionate desire, frustration and anger lack the depth of the author's intent.

recommendations.condition: Actor is in a rigid or resistant environment (conservative director, non-exploratory cast) and needs to establish irreverent freedom before scene work
recommendations.recommendation: Do irreverent preparations privately: Animation Workout, Freedom Workout, Outrageous Monologue, or Go Crazy in the dressing room or at home. Use discretion about when to do them publicly vs. privately.

sources.summary: Irreverent preparations in rigid environments: often better done privately; use discretion.

recommendations.condition: The emotional gap between where the actor is and what the scene requires is too large to bridge directly
recommendations.recommendation: Build an interim preparation bridge in steps.
